input.title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #77053: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 77053
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77053
- Title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API
- State: OPEN
- Author: firat-elbey
- Labels: docs, gateway, size: M, extensions: lmstudio, proof: supplied

Body:
```markdown
Title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API

## Summary

- Problem: LM Studio JIT (Just-In-Time) loaded models — models loaded on-demand when inference is first requested — can stay resident in VRAM longer than desired on shared/local GPU boxes.
- Why it matters: users may want OpenClaw-triggered LM Studio loads to unload after a shorter idle window using LM Studio's native TTL (Time-To-Live) mechanism, without changing inference payload compatibility.
- What changed: `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` now opts into passing `ttl` in the native LM Studio `POST /api/v1/models/load` body, alongside the existing `context_length`. This tells LM Studio to automatically unload the model after the specified number of seconds of idle.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): OpenAI-compatible inference requests are not mutated, `onPayload` TTL injection was removed, and OpenClaw does not force a default TTL when `ttlSeconds` is unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

LM Studio users can opt into idle unload behavior for OpenClaw-triggered JIT loads.

Add the following to your `openclaw.json` (find it with `openclaw config path`):

```json5
{
  models: {
    providers: {
      lmstudio: {
        params: {
          ttlSeconds: 300, // seconds of idle before LM Studio unloads the model
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

When configured, OpenClaw sends `ttl: 300` only to LM Studio's native `/api/v1/models/load` endpoint. When omitted or invalid, OpenClaw sends no `ttl` field and LM Studio keeps its own default behavior (60 min JIT idle).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local development environment
- Runtime/container: LM Studio extension unit tests
- Model/provider: LM Studio
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds`

### Steps

1. Configure LM Studio with `params.ttlSeconds`.
2. Trigger an LM Studio inference path that preloads a model.
3. Inspect the native LM Studio load request and the downstream OpenAI-compatible inference payload.

### Expected

- `/api/v1/models/load` includes `ttl` only when `ttlSeconds` is a positive integer.
- Inference payloads are unchanged and do not receive `ttl`.
- Concurrent preloads with different TTL values do not dedupe into the same in-flight load.

### Actual

- Matches expected behavior.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

29 LM Studio tests passing, covering:

- configured TTL in native load body
- no TTL when provider config is unset or invalid
- no `onPayload`/OpenAI-compatible inference payload mutation
- TTL included in the preload dedupe key
- existing preload/context-length behavior preserved

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the LM Studio load wrapper, model-load request construction, preload dedupe key, and docs update.
- Edge cases checked: unset, zero, negative, `NaN`, string, and very large `ttlSeconds`; concurrent requests with different TTL values.
- Live verified: LM Studio UI confirmed `ttl` shown on model load request; model auto-unloaded after configured idle period on RTX 5090 with JIT + Auto-Evict enabled.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: no migration required; users who want shorter LM Studio idle unloads can add `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` from config.
- Files/config to restore: LM Studio provider config only.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: LM Studio rejecting `/api/v1/models/load` bodies with `ttl`, or models unloading sooner than a user expected after opting in.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LM Studio API behavior around `ttl` may vary by version.
  - Mitigation: TTL is opt-in and omitted unless explicitly configured.
- Risk: incorrectly applying TTL to OpenAI-compatible inference requests could break proxy compatibility.
  - Mitigation: all `onPayload` TTL injection was removed and tests assert inference payloads are not mutated.
## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: LM Studio JIT-loaded models had no way to configure an idle TTL via OpenClaw — `/api/v1/models/load` was called without a `ttl` field, leaving models under LM Studio's default 60-minute idle lifecycle regardless of user preference.

- **Real environment tested**: Local LM Studio server (0.3.x) on RTX 5090 Windows host (`10.0.0.91:1234`) with JIT loading and Auto-Evict enabled. Embedding model: `text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Added `"models": { "providers": { "lmstudio": { "params": { "ttlSeconds": 60 } } } }` to `openclaw.json`
  2. Triggered an LM Studio embedding warmup via OpenClaw memory search
  3. Verified the native load body with a direct `curl` probe (see evidence below)

- **Evidence after fix**: The `ttl` field is now present in the native `/api/v1/models/load` body. Confirmed by direct `curl` to the same LM Studio REST endpoint OpenClaw calls — LM Studio acknowledges the field (returning `"Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'ttl'"` on the local 0.3.x instance, which confirms the key is received; LM Studio 0.3.6+ accepts it silently and applies the TTL). On the RTX 5090 machine with LM Studio 0.3.6+ and Auto-Evict enabled, the model was observed to unload after the configured idle window.

```
$ curl -s -X POST http://10.0.0.91:1234/api/v1/models/load \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5", "context_length": 8192}'
{
  "type": "embedding",
  "instance_id": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5:2",
  "load_time_seconds": 1.546,
  "status": "loaded"
}

$ curl -s -X POST http://10.0.0.91:1234/api/v1/models/load \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5", "context_length": 8192, "ttl": 60}'
{
  "error": {
    "message": "Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'ttl'",
    "type": "invalid_request",
    "code": "unrecognized_keys"
  }
}
```

The second response proves OpenClaw now sends `ttl` in the load body — the field reaches LM Studio. LM Studio 0.3.x rejects it as unrecognized (pre-TTL-support build); LM Studio 0.3.6+ with TTL support accepts it and applies the idle lifecycle. Unit tests independently verify the field is present for valid positive integer configs and absent for unset/invalid ones (35/35 passing).

- **Observed result after fix**: `ttl` is present in the `/api/v1/models/load` request body for both inference preloads and embedding warmups when `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` is configured. Without configuration, the field is omitted and LM Studio behavior is unchanged.

- **What was not tested**: Auto-unload behavior on LM Studio 0.3.6+ was verified on the RTX 5090 dev machine but not captured as a screenshot in this PR. Embedding warmup TTL pass-through is covered by the new `embedding-provider.test.ts` unit tests.
```

input.title: [Feature/Bug]: Local Ollama embeddings fail when proxy is enabled (SSRF defenses ignore NO_PROXY)
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #81249: [Feature/Bug]: Local Ollama embeddings fail when proxy is enabled (SSRF defenses ignore NO_PROXY)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 81249
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/81249
- Title: [Feature/Bug]: Local Ollama embeddings fail when proxy is enabled (SSRF defenses ignore NO_PROXY)
- State: OPEN
- Author: repla73
- Labels: enhancement

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

OpenClaw's internal proxy system enforces SSRF protections by clearing bypass variables, making it impossible to natively exempt local embedding providers (like Ollama on 127.0.0.1) from being routed into an active HTTP proxy tunnel.

### Problem to solve

When proxy.enabled is set to true, OpenClaw forces all traffic through the defined proxy. Local loopback traffic intended for Ollama (127.0.0.1:11434) is sent into the external proxy container (e.g., Gluetun). Because the proxy container cannot resolve the host machine's localhost, the connection fails with a 503 HTTP Tunneling error.

The system aggressively strips standard environment variables like NO_PROXY and GLOBAL_AGENT_NO_PROXY to prevent SSRF vulnerabilities. Furthermore, attempting to define a bypass array directly in openclaw.json triggers a schema validation error (Unrecognized key: "bypass"). This leaves users with no native configuration path to whitelist local interfaces.

### Proposed solution

Introduce a dedicated, secure whitelist mechanism within openclaw.json (e.g., proxy.bypassList or a specific proxy.allowLocal boolean) that safely exempts standard loopback interfaces (127.0.0.1, localhost, ::1) from the proxy routing rules without fully disabling SSRF protections for external IP ranges.

### Alternatives considered

Currently, the only way to bypass this limitation is highly convoluted:

Re-binding the host's Ollama service to listen on a Docker bridge network IP (e.g., 172.19.0.1).

Creating an artificial custom provider in the OpenClaw configuration pointing to that bridge IP.

Re-pointing the memorySearch system to use the mock provider instead of the native Ollama integration.
Alternatively, users must abandon OpenClaw's native proxy settings entirely and build OS-level iptables/nftables rules to isolate the routing.

### Impact

This routing flaw breaks the core experience for users operating self-hosted, privacy-focused deployments on headless servers or isolated infrastructure where routing outbound traffic through a VPN/Proxy is mandatory, but local LLMs should remain on the loopback interface.

### Evidence/examples

Log output showing the proxy tunnel failure for local embeddings:


[memory] embeddings rate limited; retrying in 574ms
Embeddings error: fetch failed | Request was cancelled. | Proxy response (503) !== 200 when HTTP Tunneling
Log output showing the schema rejection when attempting to configure a bypass:


Gateway failed to start: Error: Invalid config at /home/ubuntu/.openclaw/openclaw.json.
proxy: Unrecognized key: "bypass"
Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to repair, then retry.

### Additional information

OpenClaw Version: v2026.5.6 (npm managed)

OS: Ubuntu 24.04 ARM64 (Oracle OCI)

Provider: Local Ollama (qwen3-embedding:0.6b)

Proxy Setup: Gluetun HTTP proxy bound to 127.0.0.1:8888
```

Keep open. Reproducibility: no. live high-confidence reproduction was run. Network/security product judgment is needed before adding a new proxy bypass config surface, so this is not a safe autonomous fix-PR candidate.

**Security**
Needs attention: The requested behavior changes a deliberate managed-proxy SSRF boundary and needs network/security product review before implementation.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Define an owner-approved narrow local model-server proxy exception and document/test it, rather than enabling a general NO_PROXY-style loopback bypass.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

No live high-confidence reproduction was run. Source inspection shows proxy.enabled clears NO_PROXY and sends Ollama /api/embed through guarded fetch and the managed proxy, so the routing premise is source-reproducible while the exact 503 depends on the reporter's proxy/container setup.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Unclear. A general proxy.bypassList or proxy.allowLocal could solve this deployment, but the safer maintainable path is a narrow, owner-approved local model-server exception with SSRF/proxy regression coverage.

Security concerns:

- [medium] Avoid a broad local proxy bypass — `docs/security/network-proxy.md:45`
  Current docs intentionally reject destination-based loopback bypasses because localhost or 127.0.0.1 would let high-risk SSRF targets skip the filtering proxy; any exception should be narrow, explicit, and covered by proxy and SSRF tests.
  Confidence: 0.9

Acceptance criteria:

- If approved: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/zod-schema.proxy.test.ts src/infra/net/proxy/proxy-lifecycle.test.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.test.ts
- If approved: run docs listing and verify the public network proxy and Ollama provider docs after behavior changes.
- If approved: run a live proxy/Ollama smoke where the proxy cannot reach host loopback and verify only the approved local model-server path bypasses.

What I checked:

- **Issue context reviewed:** The live issue is open, externally authored, labeled enhancement, and includes logs for Ollama embedding requests failing through an HTTP tunnel plus schema rejection for an attempted proxy.bypass key.
- **Current proxy schema has no bypass field:** On current main, ProxyConfigSchema is strict and exposes enabled, proxyUrl, and loopbackMode only, matching the reported unrecognized bypass-key behavior. ([`src/config/zod-schema.proxy.ts:15`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/80ca48418a21/src/config/zod-schema.proxy.ts#L15), [80ca48418a21](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/80ca48418a21))
- **Managed proxy clears NO_PROXY:** applyProxyEnv installs the configured HTTP proxy into proxy environment variables, marks managed proxy active, records loopbackMode, and clears both no_proxy and NO_PROXY. ([`src/infra/net/proxy/proxy-lifecycle.ts:76`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/80ca48418a21/src/infra/net/proxy/proxy-lifecycle.ts#L76), [80ca48418a21](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/80ca48418a21))
- **Ollama embeddings use guarded fetch:** The Ollama embedding provider builds the /api/embed URL from the configured base URL and calls fetchWithSsrFGuard via its remote HTTP helper, with no local proxy-bypass option in that path. ([`extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.ts:90`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/80ca48418a21/extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.ts#L90), [80ca48418a21](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/80ca48418a21))
- **Managed proxy path uses env proxy dispatcher:** In strict guarded-fetch mode, an active managed proxy plus configured proxy env causes fetchWithSsrFGuard to resolve the destination under policy and then use createHttp1EnvHttpProxyAgent. ([`src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts:414`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/80ca48418a21/src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts#L414), [80ca48418a21](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/80ca48418a21))
- **Docs define the security boundary:** The network proxy docs say NO_PROXY is cleared because localhost or 127.0.0.1 bypass entries would let high-risk SSRF targets skip the filtering proxy, and later state OpenClaw does not expose a general local-network bypass for local model servers. Public docs: [`docs/security/network-proxy.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/security/network-proxy). ([`docs/security/network-proxy.md:45`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/security/network-proxy), [80ca48418a21](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/80ca48418a21))

Likely related people:

- **@jesse-merhi:** Authored the operator-managed proxy feature and recent Gateway/proxyline work that defines the managed proxy routing and loopback bypass boundary. (role: recent proxy feature contributor; confidence: high; commits: [2633b1491413](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/2633b1491413b09eaa096461ba2a70c56b9d2a72), [5b00cd1ae12a](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/5b00cd1ae12ab058128e73bef1e315cac1bc7fa2), [6921d9072e91](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/6921d9072e91df4761edc37048f990c97774026b); files: `src/infra/net/proxy/proxy-lifecycle.ts`, `src/infra/net/proxy/external-proxy.e2e.test.ts`, `docs/security/network-proxy.md`)
- **@steipete:** Recent Ollama provider commits shaped the embedding/provider routing path that reaches guarded fetch and the managed proxy layer. (role: recent Ollama/provider-routing contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [a3e067426163](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/a3e0674261633234aba1bdb855c4bb4a511608a7), [dc78d584482c](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/dc78d584482c18e25ef29925d1d86f323df9b95e), [313a19c94096](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/313a19c94096ab0ace247757f36030341d632376); files: `extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.ts`, `extensions/ollama/src/provider-base-url.ts`, `extensions/ollama/index.ts`)
- **@nico-hoff:** Introduced the earlier Ollama memory embedding provider work that this issue exercises before the current provider implementation moved under the Ollama plugin. (role: Ollama memory embedding feature contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [3eec79bd6cb7](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/3eec79bd6cb71f2e11dea10a10bd93922d9a7345); files: `src/memory/embeddings-ollama.ts`, `src/memory/embeddings-ollama.test.ts`, `src/agents/memory-search.ts`)
- **@DhtIsCoding:** Authored memory-host remote embedding proxy work in guarded-fetch/proxy helper code adjacent to embedding traffic proxy semantics. (role: adjacent memory proxy contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [f408bba9de72](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f408bba9de720d79ffdab6a420f3199f8610035e); files: `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/remote-http.ts`, `src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts`, `src/infra/net/proxy-env.ts`)
- **@Kaspre:** Recent SSRF policy hardening touched guarded fetch, provider base URL trust, and local/private origin handling relevant to evaluating a local bypass safely. (role: recent SSRF policy contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [44840007d42d](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/44840007d42d89155d7d9a323dc81438618e26b6); files: `src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts`, `src/infra/net/ssrf.ts`, `src/plugin-sdk/ssrf-policy.ts`)

Remaining risk / open question:

- A broad loopback or local-network bypass could weaken the managed-proxy SSRF boundary if it is not narrow, explicit, and regression-tested.
- The exact 503 tunneling symptom depends on the reporter's Gluetun/Ollama deployment, although the source routing path is clear.
- The product contract needs to avoid divergent proxy behavior across Ollama, LM Studio, vLLM, SGLang, and other self-hosted providers.

input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #80479: feat(memory/embeddings): add openai-compatible provider for self-hosted servers (llama.cpp, Ollama, vLLM, TGI, LocalAI)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 80479
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80479
- Title: feat(memory/embeddings): add openai-compatible provider for self-hosted servers (llama.cpp, Ollama, vLLM, TGI, LocalAI)
- State: OPEN
- Author: yaanfpv
- Labels: docs, size: M, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, dependencies-changed, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- Problem: operators running a self-hosted OpenAI-compatible embeddings server (llama.cpp's `llama-server`, Ollama via its `/v1` surface, vLLM, TGI, LocalAI, llamafile, or any reverse-proxied internal instance) have no clean adapter for it. Pointing the bundled `lmstudio` adapter at it triggers an LMStudio-only "load model" warmup that hangs against generic servers and stalls the gateway event loop for ~30 seconds per memory-lancedb embedding-provider rebuild. Pointing the bundled `openai` adapter at it works, but inherits global OpenAI headers/attribution/api-key resolution, and a removed `embedding.baseUrl` line silently falls back to api.openai.com which leaks embedded text to the cloud.
- Why it matters: the symptom is gateway freezes that show up as multi-second `sessions.list` backlogs and a flooded gateway log. Operators spend hours diagnosing what is actually a UX gap: the bundled adapters do not include a generic local-server option, and the existing in-process `local` adapter (node-llama-cpp on a `.gguf` file) does not cover operators who run their embeddings server as a separate HTTP process.
- What changes: adds a new bundled extension `extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings/` that registers an `openai-compatible` memory embedding provider. The adapter has no warmup, no global config inheritance, fails-fast on missing `embedding.baseUrl`/`embedding.model`, and does not auto-select (operator must explicitly opt in with `embedding.provider: "openai-compatible"`).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no existing adapter touched. `lmstudio`, `openai`, `mistral`, `gemini`, `voyage`, `bedrock`, `deepinfra`, `ollama`, in-process `local` adapters all behave byte-identically. The Plugin SDK surface is unchanged; the new adapter consumes the same public exports the other bundled adapters do. No protocol change, no schema change, no migration, no telemetry. The existing in-process `local` adapter stays as-is for operators who load `.gguf` files in-process via node-llama-cpp; the two adapters are complementary, not redundant.

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Docs

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80476
- Related #72875 (operator confusion: `provider: "local"` actually means in-process node-llama-cpp, not HTTP)
- Related #72937 (open PR fixing #72875's registration timing for the in-process `local` adapter)
- Related #66163 (closed: `Unknown memory embedding provider: ollama`, the precedent that led to the bundled ollama adapter; this PR follows the same pattern, generalized)
- Related #74204 (open: `memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs` too low for local GGUF; same operator profile)
- Related #74761 (open: Document oMLX as a memorySearch embedding provider; oMLX exposes an OpenAI-compatible API and would just work through this adapter without further plugin code)
- Related #60994 (closed: cannot reliably connect to remote Ollama / LM Studio via LAN; adjacent operator pain in the same ecosystem)
- Related #42270 (closed: LM Studio backend regression; same lmstudio-adapter brittleness)
- [x] This PR adds a new feature

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: an operator running `llama-server` (llama.cpp) with the BGE-M3 embedding model on `http://localhost:8081/v1` had memory-lancedb captures triggering ~30-second event-loop stalls every time the embedding provider rebuilt, because the `lmstudio` adapter's `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` warmup hangs against llama.cpp's OpenAI-compatible server (which does not expose LMStudio's load-model endpoint). The new `openai-compatible` adapter routes through the same generic `createRemoteEmbeddingProvider` factory the other adapters use, just without the warmup phase. Embeddings work end-to-end on the first call, no preload required.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.4.1 on Apple Silicon (arm64). `llama-server` from llama.cpp serving `bge-m3-Q8_0.gguf` (605 MB, 1024 dimensions) on `http://127.0.0.1:8081`, with `--ngl 24 -c 32768 -np 4 -b 512 -ub 512 --mmap --mlock --cont-batching --api-key <set>`. Live `~/.openclaw/` with memory-lancedb enabled.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran `pnpm test extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings` to validate the adapter posture and the no-warmup invariant. Then invoked the new factory directly through `node --import tsx` against the live llama-server, capturing the round-trip latency for both `embedQuery` and `embedBatch`. Independently verified the same llama-server endpoint with `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer ..." http://localhost:8081/v1/embeddings` returns 1024-dim vectors with the same model name.
- Evidence after fix:

  Live invocation of the new adapter from a small Node script (`node --import tsx`):

  ```
  $ node --import tsx /tmp/proof-openai-compatible-embeddings.mjs
  [proof] target  : http://localhost:8081/v1
  [proof] model   : text-embedding-bge-m3
  [proof] apiKey  : <set>
  [proof] factory : 1ms (no warmup, just client construction)
  [proof] client  : baseUrl=http://localhost:8081/v1 model=text-embedding-bge-m3
  [proof] embed   : 124ms, dims=1024, head=[-0.0392, 0.0370, -0.0289, 0.0161, ...]
  [proof] batch   : 25ms, count=4, dims=1024
  [proof] OK. openai-compatible embeddings adapter wired end-to-end against llama.cpp.
  ```

  Notice the factory took 1 ms (the lmstudio adapter would have taken up to 120 s here against the same server), and the actual embedding round-trip is 124 ms with the expected 1024-dim BGE-M3 output.

  Independent confirmation of the same endpoint via curl, showing the local server answers OpenAI-shaped requests without any vendor-specific preamble:

  ```
  $ curl -sS -m 5 -H "Authorization: Bearer ..." -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d '{"model":"text-embedding-bge-m3","input":"hello"}' \
      -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} time=%{time_total}s\n" \
      http://localhost:8081/v1/embeddings | tail -c 200
  ...,0.05932944267988205],"index":0,"object":"embedding"}]}
  HTTP 200 time=0.069077s
  ```

  Targeted regression test for the adapter posture and the no-warmup invariant:

  ```
  $ pnpm test extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  4 passed (4)
     Duration  4.52s
  ```

- Observed result after fix: provider construction takes 1 ms (no warmup network call). The adapter holds the per-plugin baseUrl/model exactly as configured, with no fallback to any global config block. Embeddings round-trip in well under 200 ms against the live local server. Existing adapters (`openai`, `lmstudio`, `mistral`, `gemini`, `voyage`, `bedrock`, `deepinfra`, `ollama`, in-process `local`) are untouched.
- What was not tested: did not run the new adapter inside an actual openclaw gateway process end-to-end, because the dist bundle does not include the new extension yet (the source-only invocation above is the closest equivalent without a release-tagged build). Did not run `pnpm check:changed` in Testbox; targeted `pnpm test extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings` plus targeted `npx oxlint extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings/` plus targeted `pnpm tsgo:prod` are all clean on the touched files.
- Before evidence: not applicable for a feature add. The "before" is "this provider did not exist," and the operator pain it addresses is documented in the Summary above and in linked issue #80476.

## Root Cause

N/A. Feature addition, not a regression fix. (For the underlying operator pain that motivated the addition, see linked issue #80476.)

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts` (new), with the factory in `extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings/embedding-provider.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - The adapter's posture: `id: "openai-compatible"`, `transport: "remote"`, no `autoSelectPriority`, no `authProviderId`, `allowExplicitWhenConfiguredAuto: true`, no `shouldContinueAutoSelection`. This is what stops the adapter from accidentally being auto-selected over an unrelated cloud provider whose key happens to be configured.
  - No warmup or preload during `create`. The adapter must produce exactly one factory invocation per `create` call; nothing else.
  - The cache key includes the per-plugin `baseUrl` and `model` exactly as supplied, so two different local servers do not share a cache entry.
  - The Authorization header is stripped from the cache key so a rotated bearer does not invalidate cached embeddings.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the adapter is a thin facade over the generic `createRemoteEmbeddingProvider`. The risk surface is the posture (auto-select / auth / fallback) and the absence of any pre-call side effect. Both are testable in pure-TS with a mocked factory; no live server needed for the unit tests.
- Existi

[truncated]
```

per_label_rationale.label: reliability
per_label_rationale.rationale: Central failure mode: false timeouts, minutes-long fallback cascades, gateway unresponsiveness, and crash-loop class symptoms.

per_label_rationale.label: agent_runtime
per_label_rationale.rationale: The model-fallback/routing subsystem (src/agents/model-fallback.ts) misclassifies errors and orchestrates model-call fallback chains — internal runtime model-call orchestration.

per_label_rationale.label: self_hosted_inference
per_label_rationale.rationale: Healthy local vLLM endpoints are falsely timed out; the local/loopback/.local idle-timeout resolver behavior is central to the remaining bug.

per_label_rationale.label: gateway
per_label_rationale.rationale: sessions_spawn returns gateway timeouts and gateway RPC backpressure/unresponsiveness is an explicit remaining symptom.

excluded_label_rationale.label: inference_api
excluded_label_rationale.rationale: Remote/local providers are involved but the central concern is fallback routing orchestration and timeout policy, not provider API request/response integration.

excluded_label_rationale.label: cron_automation
excluded_label_rationale.rationale: Cron jobs are affected and amplify the issue but the root-cause surfaces are timeout/fallback paths, not cron scheduling mechanics.

excluded_label_rationale.label: sessions
excluded_label_rationale.rationale: Session model switch and continuity are mentioned, but the misclassification fix lives in fallback orchestration, not session lifecycle ownership.

ambiguity.why_not_hard: Core owning surfaces (reliability, model-fallback orchestration, local vLLM timeout, gateway RPC) are identifiable, but the bug spans multiple control-plane paths so boundary inclusion is debatable.

- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/63229
- Title: Bug: Gateway falsely marks healthy local vLLM endpoints as timed out/overloaded, causing 1–23 min fallback cascades
- State: OPEN
- Author: clawdia-lobster
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-info, impact:auth-provider, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🦐 gold shrimp

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

The gateway's model-fallback/routing subsystem incorrectly marks healthy, responsive local vLLM endpoints as "timed out" or "overloaded", causing cascading fallback chains that take **1–23 minutes** to resolve. The endpoints themselves respond in **0.27–0.93s** when tested directly via curl.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.5 (container, Linux 6.12.63 x64)
- Gateway: loopback bind, port 18789
- Local vLLM endpoints:
  - `vllm-8001` (gemma4, 27B) on jupiter.wg.local:8001 — dedicated GPU
  - `vllm-7002` (qwen3.5-27b) on jupiter.wg.local:7002 — dedicated GPU
- Remote providers: Novita (GLM-5, Kimi), DeepInfra (Kimi), Anthropic (Sonnet)
- Config: `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 1200`, `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds: 300`

## Observed Behaviour

### 1. Endpoints are fast (direct curl, concurrent)

Both GPUs idle, tested concurrently:

| Endpoint | Avg latency (5 reqs) |
|----------|---------------------|
| vllm-8001/gemma4 | **0.28s** |
| vllm-7002/qwen3.5-27b | **0.91s** |

### 2. Gateway marks them as timed out or overloaded

From gateway logs, model fallback decisions for today:

**Failure reasons:**
- `timeout`: 17 occurrences
- `unknown`: 8
- `overloaded`: 2

**Error previews:**
- `LLM request timed out.`: 12
- `Gateway is draining for restart; new tasks are not accepted`: 8
- `cron: job execution timed out`: 4
- `Live session model switch requested: <model>`: 2
- `Request was aborted.`: 1

### 3. Fallback chains take minutes

Example fallback chains from today's logs:

| Run ID | Chain | Total time |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| `7c914aae` | qwen→timeout → Kimi→timeout → gemma→timeout → **Sonnet✓** | **23.4 min** |
| `21ca97c0` | gemma→timeout → **qwen✓** | **4.1 min** |
| `0cb06206` | gemma→timeout → **Kimi✓** | **56.6s** |
| `66f5e9e5` | gemma→timeout → **GLM-5✓** | **80.6s** |

### 4. Gateway can't even spawn subagents

Attempting `sessions_spawn` returns:
```
gateway timeout after 10000ms
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
```

Meanwhile, direct curl to the same endpoints returns in <1s.

### 5. "Overloaded" misclassification

The gateway logs show `reason: "overloaded"` with `errorPreview: "Live session model switch requested: novita/zai-org/glm-4.7"`. A **session model mismatch** is being classified as a provider overload — the gateway is conflating an internal session state error with provider unavailability.

## Expected Behaviour

- Requests to healthy, sub-second local endpoints should not time out
- Session model switch errors should not be classified as "overloaded"
- Fallback chains should not take minutes when all providers are responsive
- `sessions_spawn` should not time out when the gateway is under normal load

## Root Cause Hypothesis

Two distinct bugs:

1. **Internal timeout too aggressive or misapplied**: The gateway's LLM request timeout fires before the endpoint responds, or the timeout is applied to an internal queue wait rather than the actual HTTP request. Endpoints respond in <1s but the gateway reports "LLM request timed out" 17 times today.

2. **LiveSessionModelSwitchError misclassified as "overloaded"**: When a cron job or isolated session requests a model different from the live session's current model, the gateway throws `LiveSessionModelSwitchError` and classifies this as `reason: "overloaded"` in the fallback system. This is semantically wrong and triggers unnecessary fallback cascading.

## Reproduction

1. Configure two local vLLM providers with fast endpoints (<1s response)
2. Configure 3+ agents with cron jobs using different model overrides
3. Observe gateway logs: endpoints will be marked as "timed out" despite being healthy
4. Run `curl` directly against the same endpoints to confirm sub-second response

## Impact

- Interactive sessions experience **minutes-long delays** for responses that should take seconds
- Cron jobs time out and fail unnecessarily
- Session continuity breaks (related to #63195)
- Gateway becomes unresponsive to `sessions_spawn` and CLI commands
- Users lose trust in the interface as a reliable work surface

## Additional Context

- Related: #63195 (sessions disappearing during normal use)
- The `LiveSessionModelSwitchError` appears 17 times in today's logs, suggesting this is the dominant failure mode
- `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent: 8` with 5 agents may amplify the issue but is not the root cause — the endpoints are idle when failures occur

```

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Crash (process/app exits or hangs)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

# Bug: Memory provider cutover to Ollama leaves production index in mixed OpenAI/Ollama vector state after live sync/reload

## Summary

Switching `agents.defaults.memorySearch` from OpenAI embeddings to Ollama embeddings can leave the live production memory index in an inconsistent state after the provider change is loaded.

In my repro, a clean isolated Ollama index completed successfully with 768-dimensional vectors, but after copying/cutting over to the production memory path and loading the config change, `openclaw memory status --deep` reported:

- `Provider: ollama (requested: ollama)`
- `Model: nomic-embed-text:latest`
- `Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks`
- `Dirty: no`
- `Vector dims: 1536`

That is inconsistent: `nomic-embed-text` via Ollama produced 768-dimensional vectors in the isolated build, while 1536 dimensions matches the previous OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` index.

A known-good canary search then returned no matches. Rolling back the config and SQLite index to the prior OpenAI backup restored canary search results.

This appears related to the memory provider-change / live sync / auto-reindex path, rather than the initial Ollama embedding build itself.

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: `2026.5.7`
- OS/runtime: Linux container, Node `24.14.0`
- Install/runtime style: npm/global OpenClaw CLI inside container
- Memory engine: built-in SQLite memory search (`memory-core` plugin)
- Previous provider/model: `openai` / `text-embedding-3-small` / 1536 dims
- New provider/model: `ollama` / `nomic-embed-text:latest` / 768 dims
- Ollama endpoint: configured local/container Ollama provider

## Configuration change

Production config was changed under:

```json5
agents.defaults.memorySearch = {
  enabled: true,
  provider: "ollama",
  model: "nomic-embed-text:latest",
  fallback: "none",
  sync: {
    embeddingBatchTimeoutSeconds: 600
  }
}
```

The setting location matches the docs: `agents.defaults.memorySearch`.





After cutover/reload, production memory reported an inconsistent state:

- Provider/model: Ollama / `nomic-embed-text:latest`
- Vector dims: `1536`, matching previous OpenAI index rather than Ollama's 768-dim isolated index
- Dirty: `no`
- Canary search: no matches

Rollback to the OpenAI backup restored search.

## Why this may be related to #60856 but distinct

This is adjacent to #60856 because it involves Ollama-backed memory search, gateway restart/reload, and background memory sync/indexing.

However, this is not the same symptom:

- #60856: indexing times out after 120s despite embeddings being ready.
- This issue: isolated Ollama indexing succeeds, but production provider cutover/reload leaves a mixed provider/vector-dimension state and search fails.

The fix for #60856 appears to have landed before `2026.5.7`, but this provider-change/index-metadata issue still reproduces on `2026.5.7`.


### Steps to reproduce

## Reproduction outline

1. Start from a healthy OpenAI-backed production memory index.

   Observed before cutover:

   ```text
   Provider: openai (requested: openai)
   Model: text-embedding-3-small
   Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks
   Dirty: no
   Vector dims: 1536
   ```

2. Create an isolated profile/config with memory search set to Ollama.

3. Force/build the isolated Ollama memory index.

   Isolated profile completed cleanly:

   ```text
   Provider: ollama (requested: ollama)
   Model: nomic-embed-text:latest
   Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks
   Dirty: no
   Embeddings: ready
   Vector dims: 768
   ```

4. Run a canary search against the isolated Ollama profile.

   Result: canary search returned relevant matches.

5. Back up production config and `~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`.

6. Change production config to Ollama and copy the clean isolated Ollama SQLite index into the production memory path.

7. Run production memory status/search after live reload/sync has had a chance to touch the index.

   Observed inconsistent production state:

   ```text
   Provider: ollama (requested: ollama)
   Model: nomic-embed-text:latest
   Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks
   Dirty: no
   Embeddings: ready
   Vector dims: 1536
   ```

8. Run a known-good canary search.

   Result: no matches.

9. Roll back config and production SQLite to the prior OpenAI backup.

   Verification after rollback:

   ```text
   Provider: openai (requested: openai)
   Model: text-embedding-3-small
   Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks
   Dirty: no
   Embeddings: ready
   Vector dims: 1536
   ```

   The same canary search returned matches again.

### Expected behavior

When `memorySearch.provider` / `model` changes from OpenAI to Ollama:

- The production index should be rebuilt or replaced cleanly in the new provider's vector space.
- `Provider: ollama` + `Model: nomic-embed-text:latest` should not report stale OpenAI-sized `1536` dimensions.
- A provider/dimension mismatch should be detected and treated as dirty/invalid, not `Dirty: no`.
- Search should not silently return no matches because the index metadata/vector store is in a mixed state.



### Actual behavior

After cutover/reload, production memory reported an inconsistent state:

- Provider/model: Ollama / `nomic-embed-text:latest`
- Vector dims: `1536`, matching previous OpenAI index rather than Ollama's 768-dim isolated index
- Dirty: `no`
- Canary search: no matches

Rollback to the OpenAI backup restored search.

### OpenClaw version

2026.5.7

### Operating system

Docker Container on Ubuntu

### Install method

docker

### Model

/openrouter/openai-codex/gpt-5.5

### Provider / routing chain

OpenClaw memorySearch -> Ollama embedding provider -> nomic-embed-text:latest

### Additional provider/model setup details

Ollama via container
OpenClaw via container
- OpenClaw version: `2026.5.7`
- OS/runtime: Linux container, Node `24.14.0`
- Install/runtime style: npm/global OpenClaw CLI inside container
- Memory engine: built-in SQLite memory search (`memory-core` plugin)
- Previous provider/model: `openai` / `text-embedding-3-small` / 1536 dims
- New provider/model: `ollama` / `nomic-embed-text:latest` / 768 dims
- Ollama endpoint: configured local/container Ollama provider

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
Isolated Ollama rebuild monitor:
[2026-05-18 08:21:41] size=211324928 procs=yes status=Provider: ollama (requested: ollama) Model: nomic-embed-text:latest|Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks Dirty: no Embeddings: ready|Vector dims: 768

Completion marker:
Ollama current rebuild completed: Provider: ollama (requested: ollama) Model: nomic-embed-text:latest|Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks Dirty: no Embeddings: ready|Vector dims: 768

Post-cutover failure observation, recorded immediately before rollback:
Production reported `Provider: ollama` but `Vector dims: 1536`; canary search returned no matches; `/home/node/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite` had been rewritten to 288M.

Rollback verification:
Provider: openai (requested: openai) Model: text-embedding-3-small|Indexed: 582/582 files · 6474 chunks Dirty: no Embeddings: ready|Vector dims: 1536

Current verification after rollback:
Provider: openai (requested: openai) Model: text-embedding-3-small|Indexed: 582/582 files · 6476 chunks Dirty: no Embeddings: ready|Vector dims: 1536
```

### Impact and severity

## Impact

Semantic memory appears healthy according to status, but known-good recall fails. This can silently degrade agent memory after a provider cutover and is hard to detect without a canary query.


### Additional information

## Hypothesis

The live production memory path may be reusing, rewriting, or validating SQLite/vector metadata insufficiently when provider/model/dimensions change. Possible areas to inspect:

- provider/model/dimension identity stored in the SQLite index metadata
- dirty detection after `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider` / `model` changes
- embedding cache keying across providers/models/dimensions
- auto-reindex/watch sync after gateway reload/restart
- handling of copied/replaced SQLite DBs with subsequent live sync

At minimum, `Provider: ollama` + `Vector dims: 1536` + `Dirty: no` should probably be considered invalid when the active Ollama model returns 768-dimensional embeddings.

```

**Summary**
Keep open: current main still has a source-reproducible path for the reported clean-but-mismatched memory status. The indexing path compares stored provider/model/providerKey metadata, but the status-only path can still read stale vector dimensions from existing metadata, initialize the new provider during `--deep`, and report `Dirty: no` without running that mismatch check.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence end-to-end reproduction was run, but source inspection gives a clear current-main reproduction path for the visible status inconsistency: status-only managers treat any existing metadata as clean while `--deep` can initialize the new provider and keep stale vector dimensions.

**Next step**
This is a focused repair candidate after maintainer promotion because the source path and likely files are clear, but landing should include a focused regression test and preferably a packaged Ollama/status smoke.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Make status, deep probes, and live reload use the same provider/model/providerKey/scope validation as the reindex path, marking mismatches dirty and guiding or triggering a safe rebuild before reporting the index clean.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

No high-confidence end-to-end reproduction was run, but source inspection gives a clear current-main reproduction path for the visible status inconsistency: status-only managers treat any existing metadata as clean while `--deep` can initialize the new provider and keep stale vector dimensions.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes, the narrow maintainable direction is to reuse the existing reindex metadata comparison for status and reload validation; closing as fixed would be premature because that comparison is not applied to the reported clean status path.

Acceptance criteria:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-status-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/cli.test.ts
- If feasible before landing, run a Docker or Crabbox smoke that builds an OpenAI-dimension test index, switches memorySearch to Ollama/nomic-embed-text, and verifies `openclaw memory status --deep` no longer reports stale dimensions with `Dirty: no`.

What I checked:

- **Issue report:** The provided GitHub context reports OpenClaw 2026.5.7 showing `Provider: ollama`, `Model: nomic-embed-text:latest`, `Dirty: no`, and stale `Vector dims: 1536` after an OpenAI-to-Ollama memory provider cutover; the related timeout report at https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60856 is closed and describes a different Ollama indexing timeout symptom.
- **Status dirty calculation ignores provider metadata:** `resolveInitialMemoryDirty` marks a status-only manager clean whenever index metadata exists; it does not compare the stored provider/model/providerKey against the active memorySearch settings. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-status-state.ts:26`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/17ab3b11cb85/extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-status-state.ts#L26), [17ab3b11cb85](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/17ab3b11cb85))
- **Status managers reuse persisted vector dimensions:** The manager constructor reads `meta.vectorDims` into `this.vector.dims` before computing status dirtiness from only `hasIndexedMeta`, which can preserve the old OpenAI dimension count in a status-only manager. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts:233`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/17ab3b11cb85/extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts#L233), [17ab3b11cb85](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/17ab3b11cb85))
- **Deep status probes but does not sync unless indexing is requested:** `memory status --deep` probes vector store and embeddings, but only calls `sync` under the `opts.index` branch, so a plain deep status can initialize the new provider without rebuilding or validating stale index metadata. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/cli.runtime.ts:683`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/17ab3b11cb85/extensions/memory-core/src/cli.runtime.ts#L683), [17ab3b11cb85](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/17ab3b11cb85))
- **Status output combines initialized provider with stale dimensions:** `status()` reports provider/model from `resolveStatusProviderInfo` after probe initialization while returning `vector.dims` from manager state, allowing active Ollama provider text and old metadata dimensions to appear together. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts:761`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/17ab3b11cb85/extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts#L761), [17ab3b11cb85](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/17ab3b11cb85))
- **Index path has the missing comparison:** `shouldRunFullMemoryReindex` already treats stored model, provider, and providerKey mismatches as requiring a full reindex, so the safer fix is to reuse or mirror that identity validation for status/live reload instead of inventing a second policy. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.ts:88`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/17ab3b11cb85/extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.ts#L88), [17ab3b11cb85](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/17ab3b11cb85))

(role: adjacent memory provider contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [ca26489fe882](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ca26489fe88227bbafc582f3be00680abdbc0803), [37f449d7e189](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/37f449d7e1891d3d1c3cd8fdd1492bf08831de45), [77e0e3bac5e6](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/77e0e3bac5e6eef0ff5ed98566976862027bf230); files: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts`, `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts`, `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host`)

input.title: OpenAI-compatible streaming with llama.cpp saves zero usage (stream closed before final usage chunk)
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #79897: OpenAI-compatible streaming with llama.cpp saves zero usage (stream closed before final usage chunk)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 79897
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/79897
- Title: OpenAI-compatible streaming with llama.cpp saves zero usage (stream closed before final usage chunk)
- State: OPEN
- Author: alexanderatkins
- Labels: (none)

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When using OpenClaw with a llama.cpp backend (`openai-completions` API, SSE streaming), token usage is always saved as `0/0/0`, breaking `/status` context display and compaction behavior.

## Root Cause (diagnosed)

llama.cpp emits a final **usage-only SSE chunk** after the `finish_reason: stop` chunk. OpenClaw appears to stop consuming the stream once it sees `finish_reason: stop`, so the trailing usage chunk is never processed.

Evidence from llama.cpp server log:
```
new prompt, n_ctx_slot = 120064, task.n_tokens = 17332
stop processing: n_tokens = 17553, truncated = 0
```
This confirms the model had a valid ~17.3k token prompt. But OpenClaw saves:
```json
"usage": {"input": 0, "output": 0, "cacheRead": 0, "cacheWrite": 0, "totalTokens": 0}
```

The llama.cpp log also shows:
```
reasoning-budget: activated, budget=2147483647 tokens
srv stop: cancel task, id_task = 112
```
The `cancel task` line suggests the client (OpenClaw) closes the connection around finalization, dropping the final usage chunk.

A direct `curl` of the same endpoint confirms the usage chunk **is** sent by llama.cpp — it's just not making it into OpenClaw's saved transcript.

## Expected Behavior

OpenClaw should either:
1. Continue consuming the SSE stream until the connection closes (not stop at `finish_reason: stop`), or
2. Fall back to llama.cpp's `timings` / token fields in the response when the usage chunk was missed

## Reproduction

- Provider: `studio-llamacpp` with `api: openai-completions`
- Any llama.cpp server with streaming enabled
- Model with `reasoning: true` (triggers `reasoning-budget: activated`)
- Run any agent turn → check saved transcript usage fields → all zeros
- `curl` the same endpoint directly → final SSE chunk contains correct usage

## Impact

- `/status` shows `?/?` context instead of actual token counts  
- Compaction/safeguard logic can't accurately track context usage
- Agents running on local llama.cpp backends effectively have no token visibility
```

input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #70790: fix(agents): strip empty tools/tool_choice from embedded runner payloads
input.github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 70790 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/70790 - Title: fix(agents): strip empty tools/tool_choice from embedded runner payloads - State: OPEN - Author: kiranvk-2011 - Labels: agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem When the `active-memory` plugin invokes sub-agents with `toolsAllow: ["memory_search", "memory_get"]`, these tools are unavailable in the embedded runner context and get filtered down to an empty array `tools: []`. Strict OpenAI-compatible providers (DashScope/GLM, Kimi, vLLM) reject this with: ``` 400 [] is too short - 'tools' ``` This affects any sub-agent flow (active-memory, cron, etc.) using DashScope/GLM or similar providers through the embedded runner. ## Root Cause Chain This bug has been tracked across multiple issues and partial-fix PRs: 1. **#53174** (closed) — Original bug report: `/btw` command fails with ModelStudio provider: `400 [] is too short - 'tools'` 2. **#59898** (open) — Attempted a system-prompt guard for empty tool lists 3. **#66710** (open) — Forwarding `toolsAllow` through the embedded runner call chain 4. **#47947** (open) — Stripping `tool_choice` when tools array is empty 5. **#66581** (open) — `toolsAllow` parameter not forwarded to `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend` 6. **#69892** (open) — Active Memory sub-agent receives all tools instead of `toolsAllow` filter (`toolsAllow` not forwarded in `runEmbeddedPiAgent`) ## Solution Consolidates all three open PRs (#59898, #66710, #47947) into a single, minimal payload interceptor at the stream-resolution layer — `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.ts`. Adds `wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools()` which: - **Strips `tools` entirely** when the array is empty (`length === 0`) — correct behavior for tool-less requests per the OpenAI API spec - **Strips `tool_choice`** simultaneously when `tools` is absent — some providers reject `tool_choice` without a corresponding `tools` array (#47947) - Wraps both the boundary-aware and custom stream function paths, covering all HTTP/embedded stream transports ## Why This Approach - **Payload-level intercept** — operates on the serialized HTTP payload after all filtering logic has run, so it is transport-agnostic and covers every code path that reaches the provider - **No core logic changes** — does not modify the tool filtering or `toolsAllow` forwarding; it is a defensive guard at the egress boundary - **Supersedes** the partial approaches in #59898 (system prompt guard), #66710 (call-chain forwarding), and #47947 (tool_choice guard) — all three are addressed by this single hook - **Verified** on a test container with `bailian/glm-5` via Telegram — active-memory queries succeed without the 400 error ## Change ```diff src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.ts + wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools() — payload interceptor that strips empty tools + arrays and orphaned tool_choice from outgoing HTTP payloads + // Wrap with empty-tools guard for boundary-aware stream + return wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools(boundaryAwareStreamFn); + + // Wrap with empty-tools guard for custom stream function as well. + return wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools(currentStreamFn); ``` ## Verification - Built and deployed via custom GitHub Actions runner (`ghcr.io/kiranvk-2011/openclaw:0.0.0-emptytools-fix`) - Active-memory plugin queries succeed with `bailian/glm-5` (DashScope provider) — no more 400 rejection - Gateway health check passes with all 8 plugins loaded (including `active-memory`) - Telegram connectivity verified on test instance --- 🤖 **AI-assisted PR** — lightly tested. Built and validated via CI + test container deployment. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-23T21:37:27Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds `wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools()` as a payload interceptor in `stream-resolution.ts` to strip `tools: []` and orphaned `tool_choice` from outgoing HTTP payloads, fixing 400 rejection errors from strict OpenAI-compatible providers (DashScope/GLM, Kimi, vLLM). - The `session-http-fallback` code path (line 113) returns `currentStreamFn` unwrapped when `shouldUseWebSocketTransport=true` but `wsApiKey` is absent — this branch is not protected and will still trigger provider 400 errors in that configuration.

Confidence Score: 4/5

Safe to merge with the understanding that the session-http-fallback path remains unprotected — a one-line fix covers the gap. The core fix is sound and well-targeted. One code path (shouldUseWebSocketTransport=true with no wsApiKey) escapes the wrapper and still exposes tools: [] to strict providers, which is an incomplete fix for the stated goal. src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.ts — the session-http-fallback branch at line 113 needs wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools.

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.ts`, line 105-113 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/e2f4ceeb52d5ddbe024befe9216fb40e5dc15cfb/src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.ts#L105-L113)) **`session-http-fallback` path not wrapped** When `shouldUseWebSocketTransport` is `true` but `wsApiKey` is absent, the function returns `currentStreamFn` at line 113 before reaching the `wrapStreamFnStripEmptyTools` call at line 131. The PR description claims to cover "all HTTP/embedded stream transports," but this `"session-http-fallback"` branch (as named in `describeEmbeddedAgentStreamStrategy`) is unprotected and will still send `tools: []` to any strict provider that happens to fall into this code path.

input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr

- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/82880
- Title: security: harden ACPX proxy and Firecrawl SSRF protection
- State: OPEN
- Author: thesomewhatyou
- Labels: size: M, extensions: acpx, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, extensions: firecrawl, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof

Body:
```markdown
## Worthy Security Hardening & Proof (Addressing Clawsweeper Feedback)

This update provides the "worthy proof" requested by the maintainers and addresses the gaps identified by Clawsweeper.

### 1. ACPX Proxy: Cryptographic Command Binding
- **Improvement:** Upgraded `mcp-proxy.mjs` to support HMAC-SHA256 payload signatures.
- **Proof:** Created `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy-security.test.ts` which provides the following deterministic evidence:
  - **Blocks unsigned payloads:** When `OPENCLAW_MCP_PROXY_SECRET` is set, unsigned payloads are rejected.
  - **Blocks tampered payloads:** Any modification to the command or arguments results in a signature mismatch and immediate rejection.
  - **Blocks unauthorized binaries:** Demonstrates that the `OPENCLAW_MCP_PROXY_ALLOWED_COMMAND_PREFIX` correctly restricts the execution surface.

### 2. Firecrawl: DNS Pinning (TOCTOU SSRF Protection)
- **Improvement:** Implemented DNS result pinning. The extension now resolves the hostname locally and **replaces the hostname in the outbound URL with the pinned IP address** before sending it to the Firecrawl API.
- **Proof:** Created `extensions/firecrawl/src/firecrawl-dns-pinning.test.ts` which provides the following evidence:
  - **Hostname Replacement:** Verifies that a request for `https://example.com/page` is transformed into `https://93.184.216.34/page` in the actual fetch body sent to Firecrawl.
  - **Policy Enforcement:** Ensures that if the resolved IP is private/internal, the request is blocked before it reaches the Firecrawl service.

### Real Behavior Proof (Terminal Logs)

#### Security Test Suite Execution
```text
$ npx vitest run extensions/firecrawl/src/firecrawl-dns-pinning.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy-security.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 C:/Users/Laptop/Projects/openclaw

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Duration  4.22s
```

#### Firecrawl DNS Pinning Evidence
From `firecrawl-dns-pinning.test.ts`:
```typescript
// Test Logic
await runFirecrawlScrape({ url: "https://example.com/page", ... });

// Observed Result
expect(fetchBody.url).toBe("https://93.184.216.34/page"); // PASS
```

#### ACPX Signature Evidence
From `mcp-proxy-security.test.ts`:
```typescript
// Test Logic (Secret Set, Invalid Signature)
const child = spawn(proxyPath, ["--payload", invalidPayload], ...);

// Observed Result
expect(stderr).toContain("signature verification failed"); // PASS
```

### Security Impact
- **Command binding:** Host-level execution is now cryptographically bound to the Gateway's intent.
- **SSRF Hardening:** TOCTOU/DNS-rebinding window is eliminated via pinning.

### REVIEW NOTE
I have added two new test files specifically to prove the security properties of these changes. These files provide the "worthy proof" required for merge.

```

<details>
<summary>What the crustacean ranks mean</summary>

- 🦀 challenger crab: rare, exceptional readiness with strong proof, clean implementation, and convincing validation.
- 🦞 diamond lobster: very strong readiness with only minor maintainer review expected.

Shiny media proof means a screenshot, video, or linked artifact directly shows the changed behavior. Runtime, network, CSP, and security claims still need visible diagnostics.

</details>

**Real behavior proof**
Needs real behavior proof before merge: The PR body includes copied Vitest output and mocked assertions, but no redacted live ACPX signed launch or real Firecrawl scrape/blocking proof; terminal output, logs, recordings, or linked artifacts should redact private data before posting. After adding proof, update the PR body; ClawSweeper should re-review automatically. If it does not, the PR author or someone with repository write access can comment `@clawsweeper re-review`.

**Risk before merge**
- Merging the Firecrawl rewrite can break existing HTTPS scrape targets behind CDNs, virtual hosts, SNI, or certificate routing because Firecrawl receives an IP URL instead of the user-requested hostname.
- The Firecrawl hardening does not prove a complete SSRF boundary unless Firecrawl exposes a host-preserving pinned connection contract or maintainers intentionally accept a documented strict/breaking mode.
- The ACPX secret gate can reject all current production proxy payloads when enabled because the PR adds verifier-side HMAC support without wiring a production signer.
- The command-prefix guard can create a false security boundary for interpreters or shells because it validates only the executable portion and not the canonical arguments or gateway-intended command.

**Maintainer options:**
1. **Rework The Security Boundary Before Merge (recommended)**  
   Wire ACPX signing into the real producer and replace Firecrawl IP rewriting with a host-preserving SSRF strategy or a documented opt-in strict mode with upgrade proof.
2. **Accept A Breaking Strict Mode Explicitly**  
   Maintainers could choose the Firecrawl and ACPX behavior changes deliberately, but the PR would need docs, fresh-install and upgrade proof, and clear operator guidance.
3. **Pause The Firecrawl Half**  
   If Firecrawl cannot support host-preserving pinning, pause or split this PR rather than landing an IP rewrite that changes user targets.

**Next step before merge**
Manual review is needed because the remaining blockers are security-boundary design, compatibility policy, and contributor real-behavior proof rather than a narrow mechanical repair.

**Security**
Needs attention: The diff attempts security hardening but leaves concrete ACPX payload-authentication and Firecrawl SSRF/compatibility concerns.

**Review findings**
- [P1] Preserve the Firecrawl scrape hostname — `extensions/firecrawl/src/firecrawl-client.ts:555-556`
- [P1] Wire ACPX signatures through the producer — `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy.mjs:12-14`
- [P2] Bind command policy to arguments too — `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy.mjs:118`

<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Land a complete, documented security boundary: preserve normal Firecrawl target semantics unless an explicit strict mode is approved, and wire ACPX payload signing through the real producer with full command/argument binding and upgrade proof.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, by source inspection. The PR head shows Firecrawl replacing the scrape hostname before posting to Firecrawl, and ACPX verifying OPENCLAW_MCP_PROXY_SECRET without any production signing path in current main.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

No. The current approach is not the best fix because it changes Firecrawl target semantics and adds only the ACPX verifier half; the maintainable path is a complete producer/consumer signing contract plus a host-preserving or explicitly opt-in Firecrawl SSRF strategy.

Label justifications:

- `P1`: The PR changes security-sensitive ACPX command execution and Firecrawl SSRF behavior in ways that can break real workflows or weaken the intended boundary.
- `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`: The Firecrawl IP rewrite can make existing hostname-based HTTPS scrape targets fail or fetch a different origin after merge.
- `merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary`: The ACPX verifier-only HMAC path and Firecrawl remote-fetch pinning claim do not yet establish the security boundary the PR advertises.

Full review comments:

- [P1] Preserve the Firecrawl scrape hostname — `extensions/firecrawl/src/firecrawl-client.ts:555-556`
  The patch rewrites `https://example.com/page` to an IP URL before sending it to Firecrawl. That changes HTTPS/SNI and virtual-host routing for normal scrape targets, so existing CDN or shared-host sites can fail or fetch the wrong origin; keep the original URL unless maintainers approve a documented strict mode.
  Confidence: 0.92
- [P1] Wire ACPX signatures through the producer — `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy.mjs:12-14`
  `OPENCLAW_MCP_PROXY_SECRET` now makes the proxy reject unsigned payloads, but the real ACPX launch path does not sign payloads. Enabling the secret would break current launches, while leaving it unset gives no command-binding boundary; add producer-side signing for the canonical payload or remove the opt-in verifier.
  Confidence: 0.9
- [P2] Bind command policy to arguments too — `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy.mjs:118`
  The allowed-prefix check only inspects `target.command`. A payload can keep an allowed interpreter or shell executable and move the dangerous behavior into `target.args`, so the policy should validate or sign the canonical full command and arguments rather than only the binary prefix.
  Confidence: 0.82

Overall correctness: patch is incorrect

Security concerns:

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 46740 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/46740 - Title: ACP: classify silent acpx exits as backend unavailable - State: OPEN - Author: clovericbot - Labels: size: S, extensions: acpx, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - classify silent non-zero acpx exits as `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` instead of generic `ACP_TURN_FAILED` - preserve the existing permission-denied guidance while improving the fallback used when the runtime backend exits without a structured ACP error - cover the runtime, manager, and user-facing error text regressions for `acpx exited with code 1` ## Testing - pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/acp/runtime/error-text.test.ts ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T02:23:08Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR reclassifies silent (no stderr) non-zero exits from the `acpx` backend as `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` rather than the generic `ACP_TURN_FAILED`, giving users more actionable guidance (`/acp doctor`) when the backend process disappears without a structured error. Permission-denied exits (code 5) are deliberately kept as `ACP_TURN_FAILED` to preserve the existing configuration guidance. The change is applied in two layers: - **`extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`** – `resolveSilentAcpxExitErrorCode` is called directly when the ACP runtime detects a silent exit in `runTurn` (streaming path) and in the private `runControlCommand` helper (control-command path). - **`src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts`** – A defensive regex-based fallback (`resolveTurnFailureFallbackCode`) catches the rare case where the runtime emits a plain `Error` (not `AcpRuntimeError`) with the `"acpx exited with code N"` message text. Key observations: - The `runControlCommand` change is broader than the PR description implies: it also affects session-init command paths that previously used `fallbackCode: "ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED"`. A silent exit during `ensureSession` (e.g. `sessions new`) will now surface as `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` instead of `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, changing both user-facing guidance and the Discord monitor's session-staleness classification (`classifyAcpStatusProbeError` in `src/discord/monitor/provider.ts` has an explicit branch for `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`). This may be intentional and arguably more correct, but it is undocumented. - The regex in `manager.core.ts` creates a soft coupling to the exact string produced by `formatAcpxExitMessage`; both are stable today, but the dependency is implicit. - Test coverage for the primary `runTurn` path (runtime test), the manager's fallback path (manager test), and the error-text routing (error-text test) is solid.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge with minor follow-up: the session-init reclassification side-effect should be acknowledged and ideally tested. - The core logic is correct and well-tested across three test files. No critical bugs found. The main concern is an undocumented behavioral change: `runControlCommand` now returns `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` for session-init silent exits (previously `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`), which has downstream effects on user guidance text and the Discord monitor's session classification. This is arguably better behavior but it is not mentioned in the PR description and lacks a dedicated test to lock in the expectation. - extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts (runControlCommand silent-exit path) and src/discord/monitor/provider.ts (classifyAcpStatusProbeError ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED branch)

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`, line 812-820 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/c50968f53cdd9787a3cd0dcbc8b74d75d3b935b2/extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts#L812-L820)) **Silent-exit reclassification also affects session-init callers** `runControlCommand` is a shared private method called both for turn operations (`fallbackCode: "ACP_TURN_FAILED"`) and for session initialisation (`fallbackCode: "ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED"`). By replacing `params.fallbackCode` with `resolveSilentAcpxExitErrorCode` at the silent-exit branch, a silent non-zero exit during session initialisation (e.g. `ensureSession` → `sessions new`) now throws `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` instead of `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`. Concretely, two things change for that path: 1. **User-facing guidance** – `formatAcpRuntimeErrorText` routes `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` to "If this session is stale, recreate it with `/acp spawn`…" and routes `ACP_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE` to "Run `/acp doctor`, install/enable the backend plugin, then retry." The latter guidance is arguably more appropriate for a backend that silently crashed, but it is a visible UX change not mentioned in the PR description. 2. **Discord monitor session classification** – `classifyAcpStatusProbeError` (`src/discord/monitor/provider.ts:167`) has an explicit branch for `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` that marks the binding as `"stale"`. With the new code that path is bypassed, and the fallback becomes `"uncertain"` (unless `isStaleRunning` is set), which could affect binding-teardown behaviour. If this broadening is intentional, it is worth documenting in the PR description and adding a test for the `ensureSession` silent-exit path (analogous to the new `runtime.test.ts` test) to lock in the expected code.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 74339 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/74339 - Title: fix(acpx): prevent duplicate -c config overrides for Codex ACP command - State: OPEN - Author: SymbolStar - Labels: size: S, extensions: acpx, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Root Cause When a user configures the ACPX Codex command with `-c model=gpt-5.5 -c model_reasoning_effort=medium`, and the runtime model override scope is active (e.g. via `sessions_spawn` with `model`/`thinking`), `appendCodexAcpConfigOverrides` blindly appended additional `-c` args to the command string without checking if those keys were already present. This caused duplicate `-c model=...` / `-c model_reasoning_effort=...` args to be passed to the codex-acp adapter. Additionally, on v2026.4.26 (the reported version), the older wrapper code and codex-acp 0.11.x dependency had different arg handling that contributed to the failure. Current main already improved the wrapper normalization and upgraded to codex-acp 0.12.0, but the duplicate-append issue remained. ## Changes - **`extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`**: Add `commandHasConfigKey()` that checks if a command string already contains a `-c =...` arg. Modified `appendCodexAcpConfigOverrides()` to skip appending a `-c` override if that config key is already present in the command string, preventing duplication. - **`extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`**: Added tests covering: - Configured command with both `-c model` and `-c model_reasoning_effort` is not duplicated - Partial overlap: only missing keys are appended - Wrapper command variant with pre-existing `-c` args - `commandHasConfigKey` utility behavior - The #74305 scenario: configured command with `-c` overrides, no runtime model passed Fixes #74305 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-29T12:56:52Z:

Greptile Summary

Adds a `commandHasConfigKey` helper that inspects an already-split command string for a given `-c` flag, then uses it in `appendCodexAcpConfigOverrides` to skip appending an override whose key is already present. This prevents the duplicate-arg breakage when a user has both a pre-configured model flag and a runtime model scope active simultaneously.

Confidence Score: 4/5

Safe to merge; fixes a real crash with no regressions on the reviewed path. No P0/P1 issues found. Two P2 style observations: (1) a silent precedence rule where a runtime model override is quietly dropped when the configured command already carries that key, and (2) a trivially redundant ?? guard. Neither affects correctness of the bug fix. No files require special attention.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts Line: 341-346 Comment: **Runtime override silently dropped when key already present** When a configured command already contains `-c model=` and the runtime scope (e.g. `sessions_spawn`) supplies a *different* model ``, the runtime value is silently discarded: the function returns the configured command unchanged. This is correct for the identical-value duplicate case, but a caller who genuinely wants to *switch* models at runtime will be surprised to find their override ignored with no log or error. Consider adding a comment documenting the precedence rule, or logging a warning when the configured and runtime values diverge. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts Line: 330-331 Comment: **Redundant nullish coalescing** `i + 1 < parts.length` is already verified by the outer condition, so `parts[i + 1]` is guaranteed to be a non-undefined `string` here. The `?? ""` fallback is unreachable. ```suggestion if (parts[i + 1]!.startsWith(`${key}=`)) { ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

No. Skipping already-present keys avoids identical duplicates, but it is not the best fix because it silently discards explicit runtime overrides that the docs define as child-session overrides.
