Body:
```markdown
## Summary
Fixes #78919

### Issue
[[Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78919)

### Changes
- fix(agents): route image attachments through sessions_spawn ACP path to Codex native vision

### Changed Files
```
CHANGELOG.md                                 |  1 +
 src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts                 | 72 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 src/agents/acp-spawn.ts                      | 24 ++++++++++
 src/agents/agent-command.ts                  |  3 ++
 src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts | 25 +++++++---
 src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts      |  9 +---
 6 files changed, 119 insertions(+), 15 deletions(-)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: When calling `sessions_spawn(runtime=acp)` with image attachments, the tool rejected them with "attachments are currently unsupported for runtime=acp". Meanwhile, `acpx codex exec` correctly forwarded images to Codex native vision. The images were silently dropped instead of being routed through the ACP gateway agent call.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw dev checkout on Linux (CentOS 8), Node 22, with `pnpm tsc` type checking and unit tests via `pnpm test`.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-78919
pnpm install
npx tsc --noEmit
pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts
pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:
Terminal output from tsc and test runs confirms the fix compiles and tests pass:
```
$ npx tsc --noEmit
(no errors)

$ pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > converts utf8 attachment to base64
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > preserves base64 attachment
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > forwards mimeType when present

$ pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
 ✓ sessions_spawn tool > ACP runtime > forwards attachments to spawnAcpDirect instead of rejecting
```
The code change removes the early-return error block that rejected attachments for `runtime=acp`, and instead forwards them through `spawnAcpDirect` → `acpManager.runTurn` via the new `toRpcAttachments` helper that encodes images to base64 for the RPC call.

**Observed result after fix**: `sessions_spawn(runtime=acp)` no longer rejects image attachments. Images are forwarded through the ACP path via `toRpcAttachments` and `acpManager.runTurn`, matching the working `acpx codex exec` image routing path. TypeScript compilation and all related unit tests pass cleanly.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end live gateway test with actual Codex vision model (tested via unit test coverage only).
```

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 43348 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/43348 - Title: feat(acp): add acp_send tool and sessions_cancel tool - State: OPEN - Author: t2wei - Labels: app: web-ui, gateway, agents, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Add two new agent tools for ACP session lifecycle management: - **`acp_send`**: Fire-and-forget message delivery to ACP sessions. Unlike `sessions_send`, skips the A2A ping-pong/announce flow and relies on ACP callback for result delivery. Follows the same session resolution, visibility, and A2A policy checks as `sessions_send`. - **`sessions_cancel`**: Cancel the active turn of an ACP session without closing it. Backed by a new `sessions.cancel` gateway RPC that delegates to `acpManager.cancelSession()`. ### Changes | File | Change | |------|--------| | `src/agents/tools/acp-send-tool.ts` | New `acp_send` tool implementation | | `src/agents/tools/sessions-cancel-tool.ts` | New `sessions_cancel` tool implementation | | `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` | Register both new tools | | `src/agents/tool-display-overrides.json` | Display config for streaming card history panel | | `src/gateway/protocol/schema/sessions.ts` | `SessionsCancelParamsSchema` | | `src/gateway/protocol/schema/protocol-schemas.ts` | Register schema | | `src/gateway/protocol/schema/types.ts` | Derive type | | `src/gateway/protocol/index.ts` | Validator + re-exports | | `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` | `sessions.cancel` handler | | `src/gateway/method-scopes.ts` | Register in `WRITE_SCOPE` | ### Design decisions - `acp_send` uses the same inline resolution pattern as `sessions_send` rather than extracting shared logic, keeping the PR self-contained and additive-only. - `sessions.cancel` is placed in `WRITE_SCOPE` (not `ADMIN_SCOPE`) since agents need to call it during normal operation. - The cancel is a no-op if no active turn exists — it does not error, matching the existing `acpManager.cancelSession()` semantics. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-11T16:50:39Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces the `acp_send` fire-and-forget tool for ACP multi-turn follow-ups and fixes callback channel routing for race-safe delivery. The implementation is well-structured — it correctly extracts the shared session resolution and permission logic into `sessions-send-core.ts`, reused by both `sessions_send` and the new `acp_send`. The A2A callback block in `agent.ts` fires for every ACP turn (spawn and follow-up), injecting the child's output into the parent session with explicit channel routing pulled from `extractDeliveryInfo`, addressing the race condition with `lastChannel` mutation. The removal of the `session requires thread=true` guard in `acp-spawn.ts` and the tightening of `hasDeliveryTarget` to require `requestThreadBinding` correctly separates H2A (thread-bound, inline delivery) from A2A (session-persistent, callback-only) spawn paths. Key changes: - **`sessions-send-core.ts`**: New shared core for session resolution and `sendParams` construction, reducing duplication between `sessions_send` and `acp_send`. - **`acp-send-tool.ts`**: Fire-and-forget tool that skips A2A flow entirely; relies on the ACP callback mechanism for result delivery. - **`agent.ts` A2A callback**: Placed before the ACP early-return to fire on all turns; uses `extractDeliveryInfo` for explicit, race-safe channel routing. - **`acp-spawn.ts`**: Suppresses external delivery for A2A spawns; passes `spawnedBy` on the `agent` dispatch as a safety net for first-turn callback resolution. - One minor issue: the error message in `sessions-send-core.ts` L109 hardcodes the string `"sessions_send"` but this function is now also called from `acp_send`, making the message misleading in that context. - One minor style issue: the fire-and-forget `callGateway` call in `agent.ts` should be prefixed with `void` to explicitly signal intentional promise detachment.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge — the core logic is sound, well-tested, and the two identified issues are minor style/wording concerns that don't affect runtime correctness. - The architectural design is solid and the critical code paths (A2A callback placement, explicit channel routing, delivery guard via `!opts.deliver`) are all correct. The only issues found are: (1) a hardcoded `"sessions_send"` string in a shared error message that now also surfaces for `acp_send`, and (2) a floating promise without `void` on the intentional fire-and-forget gateway call. Neither is a runtime bug. The PR author confirmed TypeScript type checks pass and deployed to dev with multi-turn Q&A validation. - `src/commands/agent.ts` (A2A callback block) and `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-core.ts` (shared error message) warrant a final look for the two style issues noted. Last reviewed commit: 16edefc - openclaw-barnacle at 2026-04-27T04:39:37Z: This pull request has been automatically marked as stale due to inactivity. Please add updates or it will be closed. - clawsweeper at 2026-04-27T05:19:26Z: Codex review: needs real behavior proof before merge. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.
How this review workflow works - ClawSweeper keeps one durable marker-backed review comment per issue or PR. - Re-runs edit this comment so the latest verdict, findings, and automation markers stay together instead of adding duplicate bot comments. - A fresh review can be triggered by eligible `@clawsweeper re-review` comments, exact-item GitHub events, scheduled/background review runs, or manual workflow dispatch. - PR/issue authors and users with repository write access can comment `@clawsweeper re-review` or `@clawsweeper re-run` on an open PR or issue to request a fresh review only. - Maintainers can also comment `@clawsweeper review` to request a fresh review only. - Fresh-review commands do not start repair, autofix, rebase, CI repair, or automerge. - Maintainer-only repair and merge flows require explicit commands such as `@clawsweeper autofix`, `@clawsweeper automerge`, `@clawsweeper fix ci`, or `@clawsweeper address review`. - Maintainers can comment `@clawsweeper explain` to ask for more context, or `@clawsweeper stop` to stop active automation.
**Summary** The branch adds model-facing ACP session send/cancel tools, display wiring, and a new `sessions.cancel` gateway schema/handler/scope that calls the ACP session manager. Reproducibility: yes. for the review blockers: source inspection of the PR diff against current-main contracts shows raw cancel-key forwarding, non-ACP fire-and-forget delivery loss, shared nested-lane use, an undefined error code, stale TypeBox imports, and missing generated protocol artifacts. I did not run tests because this review is read-only. **PR rating** Overall: 🧂 unranked krab Proof: 🧂 unranked krab Patch quality: 🧂 unranked krab Summary: The PR is not quality-ready because real behavior proof is missing and the patch has security, protocol, dependency, and message-delivery blockers. Rank-up moves: - Add redacted real ACP runtime proof showing `acp_send` callback delivery and `sessions_cancel` cancellation after the patch. - Rebase on current main and fix the visibility, ACP-only target, nested-lane, error-code, TypeBox, and generated protocol issues. - Ask for maintainer/API review of model-facing ACP cancellation semantics once the branch is source-correct.
What the crustacean ranks mean - 🦀 challenger crab: rare, exceptional readiness with strong proof, clean implementation, and convincing validation. - 🦞 diamond lobster: very strong readiness with only minor maintainer review expected. - 🐚 platinum hermit: good normal PR, likely mergeable with ordinary maintainer review. - 🦐 gold shrimp: useful signal, but proof or patch confidence is still limited. - 🦪 silver shellfish: thin signal; proof, validation, or implementation needs work. - 🧂 unranked krab: not merge-ready because proof is missing/unusable or there are serious correctness or safety concerns. - 🌊 off-meta tidepool: rating does not apply to this item. Shiny media proof means a screenshot, video, or linked artifact directly shows the changed behavior. Runtime, network, CSP, and security claims still need visible diagnostics.
**Real behavior proof** Needs real behavior proof before merge: The PR body and comments contain no inspectable after-fix ACP runtime proof; the contributor should add redacted terminal output, logs, screenshots, a recording, or linked artifact showing `acp_send` callback delivery and `sessions_cancel` cancellation, then update the PR body to trigger a fresh review or ask a maintainer to comment `@clawsweeper re-review`. **Risk before merge** - The PR still has no inspectable after-fix ACP runtime proof for `acp_send` callback delivery or `sessions_cancel` cancellation. - The branch is dirty against current main and predates current gateway method descriptors, generated protocol surfaces, `sessions.abort`, and per-session nested-lane behavior. - A model-facing `sessions_cancel` that forwards raw session keys can interrupt ACP session state across visibility boundaries unless it uses the same session visibility and A2A policy as existing session tools. - `acp_send` can silently lose replies for non-ACP targets because it dispatches with `deliver:false` while relying on an ACP-only callback path. - Protocol and dependency drift mean the branch is not source-correct against current main even before runtime behavior review. **Maintainer options:** 1. **Rework against current main (recommended)** Require the branch to rebase, route cancellation through explicit visibility/A2A policy, reject non-ACP `acp_send` targets, use per-session nested lanes, update generated protocol artifacts, and provide redacted real ACP proof. 2. **Replace with a narrower branch** Because the branch is stale and overlaps newer `sessions_send` and `sessions.abort` work, maintainers may prefer a fresh PR that only adds the approved missing tool surface. 3. **Accept only with owner signoff** Maintainers could intentionally expose model-facing ACP cancellation, but should explicitly approve the authorization semantics and user-visible failure modes before merge. **Next step before merge** Human review is needed because this external PR lacks required real behavior proof, is dirty/stale, and changes security-sensitive model-facing ACP cancellation semantics. **Security** Needs attention: Model-facing ACP cancellation needs attention because the PR does not preserve the existing session visibility and A2A authorization boundary. **Review findings** - [P1] Enforce visibility before cancelling sessions — `src/agents/tools/sessions-cancel-tool.ts:31-35` - [P1] Use a declared gateway error code — `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts:465` - [P2] Reject non-AC [truncated] ``` Changed files (10): ```text src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tool-display-overrides.json src/agents/tools/acp-send-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-cancel-tool.ts src/gateway/method-scopes.ts src/gateway/protocol/index.ts src/gateway/protocol/schema/protocol-schemas.ts src/gateway/protocol/schema/sessions.ts src/gateway/protocol/schema/types.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts ```

input.title: [Feature]: Add ACP support for Antigravity CLI (agy)
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84443: [Feature]: Add ACP support for Antigravity CLI (agy)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84443
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84443
- Title: [Feature]: Add ACP support for Antigravity CLI (agy)
- State: CLOSED
- Author: Openclaw-Metis
- Labels: P3

Body:
```markdown
## Feature request

Please consider adding first-class ACP support for the Antigravity CLI (`agy`) in OpenClaw.

## Why this would be useful

Antigravity CLI is already useful as an interactive coding and generation agent. In local testing, `agy` can:

- Run non-interactively with `agy -p`
- Resume conversations with `agy --conversation=<id>` or `agy -c`
- Switch models through `/model`
- Use its own tools, including image generation
- Access models such as Gemini 3.5 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6 Thinking, and GPT-OSS 120B

However, OpenClaw currently cannot treat `agy` as a proper ACP-capable agent in the same way it can work with other CLI agents. This makes it harder to use Antigravity as a durable OpenClaw backend for session orchestration, subagent work, inspection, and media/tool workflows.

## Desired behavior

Add an Antigravity CLI ACP integration or bridge so OpenClaw can:

- Start an `agy` session through the normal OpenClaw session/subagent system
- Send prompts and receive structured responses over ACP
- Preserve and resume `agy` conversation IDs
- Expose session status/history in OpenClaw tools
- Support model selection or model override where possible
- Forward attachments/reference images where possible
- Surface generated artifacts, especially image outputs, back to OpenClaw
- Handle long-running `agy` tasks without blocking the main OpenClaw session
- Normalize errors such as auth failure, model unavailability, and tool permission prompts

## Example use cases

- Use `agy` as a selectable subagent runtime from OpenClaw
- Run Antigravity CLI tasks from Discord/Telegram/WhatsApp through OpenClaw
- Generate images through `agy`, then automatically return the output attachment to the chat
- Compare Antigravity model behavior against Codex/Claude agents from the same OpenClaw session
- Let OpenClaw inspect, resume, or terminate stuck `agy` tasks cleanly

## Current workaround

The current workaround is to call `agy -p` or run interactive `agy` manually from a shell, then parse terminal output and locate generated files. This works for experiments, but it is brittle:

- Output paths and artifacts need manual parsing
- Interactive flows can hang
- Session state is not visible to OpenClaw
- No standard ACP-style status/history/tool event stream is available
- Model switching is manual

## Suggested implementation direction

An ACP bridge around `agy` could wrap:

- `agy -p <prompt>` for one-shot execution
- `agy --conversation=<id>` / `agy -c` for resumable sessions
- `agy -i` for interactive mode if needed
- Conversation metadata from `~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/`
- Generated artifacts under `~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/scratch/`

Even a minimal first version that supports one-shot execution, resumable conversation IDs, status reporting, and artifact discovery would make Antigravity much more useful inside OpenClaw.

Thanks for considering this.

```

Comments/context:

- clawsweeper at 2026-05-20T05:03:39Z:
Thanks for the idea. I checked the current extension path, and this is a better fit for [ClawHub.com](https://clawhub.ai/) than OpenClaw core.

This is a useful external harness idea, but current OpenClaw already exposes the plugin, ACP runtime backend, CLI backend, and ACPX custom-agent seams needed to host an Antigravity adapter outside core; no missing core API is identified.

So I’m closing this as a scope-fit item for the plugin/community path. Please upload or publish it through [ClawHub.com](https://clawhub.ai/) so it can live as an installable community skill instead of a bundled OpenClaw core change.

<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Publish an Antigravity ACP/CLI adapter through ClawHub or upstream it into ACPX; reopen core only if that work hits a specific missing OpenClaw plugin or ACP SDK contract.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Not applicable; this is a request for a new third-party ACP/CLI adapter rather than a bug in existing behavior. The relevant check is repository scope and whether current plugin seams can host it, which current main supports.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes for cleanup; the maintainable path is a plugin, ACPX adapter, or ClawHub package rather than adding Antigravity-specific behavior to OpenClaw core. If an adapter later proves a missing generic SDK seam, that should be filed as a focused core API issue.

Security review:

Security review: No patch is under review, and the issue is not itself reporting a security-sensitive defect.

What I checked:

- **Issue request scope:** The issue asks for a new Antigravity CLI ACP integration or bridge around `agy`, including one-shot execution, resumable conversations, model selection, attachments, artifacts, status, and normalized errors.
- **Core scope policy:** OpenClaw's [`VISION.md`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/VISION.md) says core stays lean, optional capability should usually ship as plugins, and missing generic plugin APIs should be discussed instead of adding one-off core behavior. ([`VISION.md:54`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/29f8715f05c8/VISION.md#L54), [29f8715f05c8](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/29f8715f05c8))

Public docs: [`docs/plugins/building-plugins.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/plugins/building-plugins). ([`docs/plugins/building-plugins.md:12`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/plugins/building-plugins), [29f8715f05c8](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/29f8715f05c8))
- **CLI and harness plugin path:** The CLI backend docs cover local AI CLIs and point runtimes that own full sessions, tool events, compaction, or background task state toward the agent-harness plugin surface. Public docs: [`docs/plugins/cli-backend-plugins.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/plugins/cli-backend-plugins). ([`docs/plugins/cli-backend-plugins.md:18`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/plugins/cli-backend-plugins), [29f8715f05c8](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/29f8715f05c8))

([`src/plugin-sdk/acp-runtime-backend.ts:12`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/29f8715f05c8/src/plugin-sdk/acp-runtime-backend.ts#L12), [29f8715f05c8](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/29f8715f05c8))

Public docs: [`docs/tools/acp-agents-setup.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents-setup). ([`docs/tools/acp-agents-setup.md:28`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents-setup), [29f8715f05c8](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/29f8715f05c8))

(role: feature-history owner; confidence: high; commits: [fb61986767c7](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/fb61986767c7ef8f3a2278db998ab3acdbd785c1), [6211e3dcd6cb](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/6211e3dcd6cbb88b31c714fda1089de94619290d), [38a673b688eb](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/38a673b688eb725c18e05914fecd0fcb4e045872); files: `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/config-schema.ts`, `extensions/acpx/openclaw.plugin.json`)
- **osolmaz:** Onur Solmaz authored the merged refactor that moved OpenClaw onto the shared acpx runtime library and touched the ACPX runtime/config path. (role: recent area contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [154a7edb7cad](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/154a7edb7cad3d427584eb977e43280f7afb7de7), [80711c70f007](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/80711c70f007e7a0451ad702f181ba0d2fc99cbc), [74af34cefbb2](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/74af34cefbb258307cc0b7b67af4037485e2931d); files: `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/config-schema.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/service.ts`)
- **amknight:** Alex Knight's recent merged PR is the blame source for the current ACP agents docs, custom acpx alias wording, and ACP target-resolution lines in the checked-out history. (role: recent docs and ACP configuration contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [c81271ee6e3a](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/c81271ee6e3a78e961ff4db7b26ff206b61653a8); files: `docs/tools/acp-agents.md`, `docs/tools/acp-agents-setup.md`, `src/agents/acp-spawn.ts`)

input.title: fix(agent): abort accepted gateway runs on signal
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #84381: fix(agent): abort accepted gateway runs on signal
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 84381
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84381
- Title: fix(agent): abort accepted gateway runs on signal
- State: OPEN
- Author: Kaspre
- Labels: docs, app: web-ui, gateway, commands, size: XL, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
Fixes #71710.

This makes `openclaw agent` signal handling abort Gateway-backed runs instead of only killing the local CLI process. When the CLI receives `SIGINT` or `SIGTERM`, it now aborts the active Gateway request, asks the Gateway to cancel the accepted agent run, retries that cancellation on the same connection, and falls back to a fresh authorized `chat.abort` call if the original connection closes before cancellation is confirmed.

The Gateway side now registers agent abort state before sending the accepted ack, supports pre-registration `chat.abort` and `/stop` races, preserves pending accepted agent dedupe entries until the actual run timeout, and keeps duplicate retries from spawning a second agent run. The CLI exits with the normal signal status (`130`/`143`) after cleanup, including the case where the Gateway run resolves just as a signal arrives.

Docs now call out the recommended hard-kill backstop for cron/systemd wrappers while describing the new graceful Gateway abort behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw agent` Gateway runs could leave the remote agent work alive after the local CLI received `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT`, especially after the Gateway had accepted the run. This patch makes the CLI request cancellation and makes the Gateway able to abort active and pre-registered accepted agent runs.

Real environment tested: GCP Crabbox Spot VM in `us-east1-b`, Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, branch head `ea5dfbaf324669c917dac22084889a602cca9c2e`, merge base `c8a953af9371f0c1e5980283abf554f89f641fea`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Remote validation ran `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --reporter=append-only`, a real loopback Gateway/CLI SIGTERM proof script against the source CLI through `tsx`, and focused Vitest coverage for the abort paths touched by the current-head CI fix. The earlier runtime-equivalent head also passed `pnpm build`, a loopback Gateway/CLI/provider SIGTERM proof, focused command/gateway suites, and `pnpm check:changed`; the follow-up current-head commit only updates test helper shape.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the remote proof included:

```text
proof.ok=true
remote.head=ea5dfbaf324669c917dac22084889a602cca9c2e
remote.base=c8a953af9371f0c1e5980283abf554f89f641fea
gateway.url=ws://127.0.0.1:39041
connect.requests=1
agent.message=live SIGTERM proof
agent.sessionKey=agent:main:main
accepted.runId=proof-run-84381
signal.sent=SIGTERM
abort.runId=proof-run-84381
abort.sessionKey=agent:main:main
child.exit.code=143
child.stderr=
Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 12 passed (12)
```

Earlier runtime-head suites passed before the test-helper-only follow-up: `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts` `29 passed`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts` `112 passed`, `src/gateway/call.test.ts` plus `src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts` `97 passed`, and `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts` `14 passed`. `pnpm check:changed` passed all selected lanes, including typecheck, lint shards, and runtime import cycles.

Observed result after fix: The Gateway-backed CLI run receives `SIGTERM`, confirms the accepted Gateway agent run is aborted, closes the provider request, and exits as a signal termination (`143`) instead of leaving the provider stream/run alive. The model-override path preserves backend/admin authority for the fallback abort.

What was not tested: No live third-party provider was called; the real proof used loopback Gateway/provider surfaces so abort/close behavior was deterministic. The full loopback provider-close proof and `pnpm check:changed` were run on `736a39082690389e0b25b6baae089a7f03af5534`; the current head `ea5dfbaf324669c917dac22084889a602cca9c2e` refresh reran the source-CLI accepted-run SIGTERM proof and the focused abort tests after rebasing onto `c8a953af9371f0c1e5980283abf554f89f641fea`.

## Review gates

- Codex review accepted five issues during development; all were fixed and the final Codex review reported no actionable regressions.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 48433 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48433 - Title: feat(exec): per-host allowlists via tools.exec.allowedHosts - State: OPEN - Author: ImNotAVirus - Labels: docs, gateway, commands, agents, size: XL, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Problem: `tools.exec.host` is a hard boundary — an agent configured with `host: "gateway"` cannot run commands in the sandbox, and vice versa. - Why it matters: agents that need restricted gateway exec (only approved binaries) + open sandbox exec (arbitrary commands in Docker) are forced to choose one. - What changed: new optional `tools.exec.allowedHosts` field, per-host allowlist map format in `exec-approvals.json`, `openclaw doctor` migration for legacy format, docs. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): absent `allowedHosts` = current behavior unchanged. No change to default security model or existing single-host agents. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #12405 ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New `tools.exec.allowedHosts` config field — optional array of hosts an agent may request at runtime. - `exec-approvals.json` allowlist supports per-host map format: `{ "gateway": [...], "sandbox": [{ "pattern": "*" }] }`. - Legacy array format backward compatible, one-time warning at startup, `openclaw doctor --fix` auto-migrates to `{ "default": [...] }`. - Invalid `allowedHosts` combinations rejected at config-load with clear error messages. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) Yes - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: `allowedHosts` only allows going more isolated, never less. Validated at both config-load (Zod `superRefine`) and runtime (defense in depth): - `host: "sandbox"` cannot include `"gateway"` or `"node"` (container escape prevention) - `host: "gateway"` cannot include `"node"` (unknown trust boundary) - `host: "node"` can only include `"node"` Sandbox exec only enters allowlist processing when explicitly requested via `allowedHosts`. Default sandbox-without-sandbox-runtime (agents with no exec config) is unaffected. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Debian 12 - Runtime/container: Node 22, OpenClaw 2026.3.13 gateway, Docker sandbox - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel: Telegram - Relevant config: agent with `host: "gateway"`, `allowedHosts: ["gateway", "sandbox"]`, per-host allowlist map (gateway restricted, sandbox wildcard `*`) ### Steps 1. Configure agent with `allowedHosts: ["gateway", "sandbox"]` 2. Set per-host allowlist: gateway = `[gh, xurl]`, sandbox = `[{ "pattern": "*" }]` 3. Agent calls `exec(command="gh auth status", host="gateway")` 4. Agent calls `exec(command="echo hello", host="sandbox")` 5. Agent calls `exec(command="python3 --version", host="gateway")` 6. Set invalid config `host: "sandbox"`, `allowedHosts: ["sandbox", "gateway"]`, restart gateway ### Expected Steps 3-4 succeed, step 5 blocked by allowlist, step 6 rejected at config-load. ### Actual All cases pass as expected. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: gateway allowlist (gh ✅, python3 ❌), sandbox wildcard (echo ✅, curl ✅, Paperclip API ✅), main agent not broken ✅, Zod validation rejects all 3 invalid combos ✅, `openclaw doctor` detects legacy format ✅, `openclaw doctor --fix` migrates array → `{ "default": [...] }` ✅ - Edge cases checked: agent with no exec config (default sandbox fallback) still works, legacy array format backward compatible, wildcard `*` in sandbox allowlist, `allow-always` node-host call sites updated - What you did not verify: node host end-to-end (no node configured in test env), `allow-always` approval flow on gateway/sandbox (agent configured with `ask: "off"`) ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) Optional `allowedHosts` field - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No (legacy format works, warning + doctor migration available) ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `allowedHosts` from config — behavior reverts to single-host mode - Files/config to restore: `exec-approvals.json` — switch map back to array if needed - Known bad symptoms: `exec host not allowed` errors when agent requests a host not in `allowedHosts`, `allowlist miss` if per-host resolution returns wrong bucket ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: per-host allowlist map format could confuse existing tooling reading `exec-approvals.json` - Mitigation: legacy array format fully supported, auto-detection via `isAllowlistByHost()`, doctor migration available, one-time startup warning ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-16T18:47:10Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces `tools.exec.allowedHosts` — a per-host allowlist feature allowing agents to request a more-isolated exec host (e.g. sandbox) while remaining configured with a more-permissive default (e.g. gateway). The new per-host map format for `exec-approvals.json`, Zod validation, `openclaw doctor --fix` migration, and docs are all well-structured. **Key concerns:** - **Data corruption via unupdated `node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` call sites** — `addAllowlistEntry` (line 500) and `recordAllowlistUse` (lines 513–519) were not updated to pass the new `host` parameter. After `openclaw doctor --fix` migrates a node-host agent's flat array to `{ "default": [...] }`, a subsequent "allow-always" approval on the node host triggers the `addAllowlistEntry` path without `host`. Because `isAllowlistByHost` returns `true` but `host` is `undefined`, the function falls into the `else` branch where `Array.isArray(perHostMap)` is `false`, initializes `allowlist = []`, and saves `[newEntry]` — silently **overwriting the entire per-host map**. This is the most critical bug in the PR. - **`mergeLegacyAgent` silently drops per-host map data** — both `currentEntries` and `legacyEntries` are coerced to `[]` when the allowlist is an object (not an array). If one side of the merge was already doctor-migrated to map format, that data is lost. - **`resolveAllowlistForHost` empty-array semantics** — `??` only falls back on `null | undefined`, not on `[]`. An explicit `"gateway": []` bucket shadows the `"default"` fallback, contrary to the documented "falls back to default" behavior. This is worth a note in the docs or a code-level fix.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge — a pre-existing call site in `invoke-system-run.ts` was not updated and can corrupt per-host allowlist maps for any agent after a doctor migration. - The feature design and the gateway/sandbox paths are well-implemented, but the `addAllowlistEntry` call in `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` omits the new `host` parameter. For node-host agents migrated from legacy flat-array format (the intended migration path via `openclaw doctor --fix`), this call will silently overwrite the entire per-host map with a single-entry flat array on the first "allow-always" approval. This is a concrete, reproducible data-loss bug on the node-host path. The secondary concerns (mergeLegacyAgent, empty-array fallback semantics) are lower severity but add to the overall risk. - `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` (unupdated callers of `addAllowlistEntry` and `recordAllowlistUse`) and `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` (`mergeLegacyAgent` data loss and `resolveAllowlistForHost` empty-array semantics).

Comments Outside Diff (4)

 1. `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`, line 500 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts#L500)) **`addAllowlistEntry` missing `host` corrupts per-host allowlist map** This call omits the new `host` parameter introduced in this PR. When an agent uses the per-host map format (including any agent whose legacy flat array was migrated by `openclaw doctor --fix` to `{ "default": [...] }`), `isAllowlistByHost(existing.allowlist)` will return `true` but `host` will be `undefined`. Execution falls into the `else` branch of `addAllowlistEntry`, where `Array.isArray(existing.allowlist)` is `false`, so `allowlist` is forced to `[]`. The agent's entire per-host map is then **overwritten** with a flat single-entry array on the next save: ```ts // existing.allowlist = { "default": [{ pattern: "curl" }] } // After addAllowlistEntry(file, agentId, "gh") without host: // existing.allowlist = [{ id: "...", pattern: "gh" }] ← map is gone ``` This is triggered whenever a node-host agent with per-host allowlist format grants an "allow-always" approval. The fix is to pass `"node"` as the host: 2. `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`, line 513-519 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts#L513-L519)) **`recordAllowlistUse` missing `host` parameter** `recordAllowlistUse` was updated in this PR to accept an optional `host` parameter so it can update the correct per-host bucket. This call site in `invoke-system-run.ts` was not updated, so for agents with a per-host map allowlist (e.g. post-migration `{ "default": [...] }`), the use record will always be written to the `"default"` bucket regardless of which host actually ran the command. In the common migrated case this is harmless, but it's inconsistent with the gateway path which now passes `effectiveHost`. 3. `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`, line 831-839 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L831-L839)) **Empty-array host bucket silently shadows `"default"` fallback** `resolveAllowlistForHost` uses the nullish-coalescing operator (`??`), which only falls back when the left-hand operand is `null` or `undefined`. An explicitly empty array (`[]`) is neither, so a configuration like `{ "gateway": [], "default": [{ "pattern": "curl" }] }` would resolve to an **empty** allowlist for `"gateway"` (blocking all gateway commands) rather than falling back to `"default"`. The docs describe the resolution order as `allowlist[host]` → `allowlist["default"]` → empty, and the sentence "when no host-specific entry exists" could reasonably be interpreted as "when the array is empty OR the key is absent." Users who write `"gateway": []` intending "no special overrides, use the default" will be silently blocked instead. Consider documenting this edge case explicitly, or treating an empty array identically to an absent key: ```ts export function resolveAllowlistForHost( resolved: ExecApprovalsResolved, host: ExecHost, ): ExecAllowlistEntry[] { if (resolved.allowlistByHost) { const hostEntries = resolved.allowlistByHost[host]; // Treat an empty or absent host bucket as "fall back to default" if (hostEntries && hostEntries.length > 0) return hostEntries; return resolved.allowlistByHost["default"] ?? []; } return resolved.allowlist; } ``` 4. `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`, line 209-239 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L209-L239)) **`mergeLegacyAgent` silently drops per-host allowlist when merging** Both `currentEntries` and `legacyEntries` are set to `[]` when the respective allowlist is a per-host map (because `Array.isArray(obj)` is `false` for objects). Any per-host map data is therefore silently discarded if `mergeLegacyAgent` is ever called for an agent that has already been migrated to the map format. `mergeLegacyAgent` is called on line 331 when a `"default"` key in `agents` is being merged into the main agent entry during `normalizeExecApprovals`. If the primary agent entry uses a per-host map but the legacy `agents.default` still uses the flat array (or vice versa), the map data will be lost. While the current code path may make this rare in practice, the silent data loss is risky given that `openclaw doctor --fix` can upgrade agents to map format at any time. At minimum, consider logging a warning if either `current` or `legacy` has a per-host map allowlist when this merge function is invoked.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts Line: 500 Comment: **`addAllowlistEntry` missing `host` corrupts per-host allowlist map** This call omits the new `host` parameter introduced in this PR. When an agent uses the per-host map format (including any agent whose legacy flat array was migrated by `openclaw doctor --fix` to `{ "default": [...] }`), `isAllowlistByHost(existing.allowlist)` will return `true` but `host` will be `undefined`. Execution falls into the `else` branch of `addAllowlistEntry`, where `Array.isArray(existing.allowlist)` is `false`, so `allowlist` is forced to `[]`. The agent's entire per-host map is then **overwritten** with a flat single-entry array on the next save: ```ts // existing.allowlist = { "default": [{ pattern: "curl" }] } // After addAllowlistEntry(file, agentId, "gh") without host: // existing.allowlist = [{ id: "...", pattern: "gh" }] ← map is gone ``` This is triggered whenever a node-host agent with per-host allowlist format grants an "allow-always" approval. The fix is to pass `"node"` as the host: ```suggestion addAllowlistEntry(phase.approvals.file, phase.agentId, pattern, "node"); ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, pleas [truncated] ``` Changed files (19): ```text docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md docs/gateway/security/index.md src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-request.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-types.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/commands/doctor-exec-approvals.ts src/commands/doctor.ts src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts src/config/zod-schema.exec-allowed-hosts.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-allowed-hosts.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts ```

input.title: Remove skill prelude exec allowlist
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #84570: Remove skill prelude exec allowlist
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 84570
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84570
- Title: Remove skill prelude exec allowlist
- State: MERGED
- Author: jesse-merhi
- Labels: agents, maintainer, size: M, clawsweeper:automerge, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 🚀 automerge armed

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- Remove the `skillPrelude` exec allowlist compatibility path for `cat SKILL.md && printf ... && <skill-wrapper>` chains.
- Keep direct trusted skill wrapper execution allowed while making old skill prelude chains go through the normal approval flow.
- Add a changelog entry for the exec approvals behavior change.

This removes behavior introduced by https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/57839.

## Why

The old compatibility path existed for agents that displayed a skill file through shell before running the skill wrapper, for example:

```sh
cat SKILL.md && printf '---CMD---' && <skill-wrapper> ...
```

That pattern is no longer the contract. Current skill instructions tell agents to load `SKILL.md` with the read tool, then run the actual skill executable. Keeping an exec-approval exception for the display prelude means allowlist evaluation has to recognize adjacent `cat`/`printf` commands and prove they reach a later trusted wrapper. That is extra command-chain policy for a legacy presentation pattern, not trust in the executable itself.

After this change, `autoAllowSkills` and explicit allowlist entries apply to the real skill command only. If an agent still emits the old prelude chain, the prelude commands are evaluated like any other shell commands instead of inheriting trust from the wrapper.

## Verification

- Title: Feat/browser screenshot vision
- State: OPEN
- Author: scotthuang
- Labels: docs, size: XL, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: ⏳ waiting on author, proof: 🎥 video

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- Problem: When the main model is text-only (e.g. `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview`), browser screenshots return image content blocks that the model cannot read — the agent sees `[image omitted]` and cannot reason about the page.
- Why it matters: Browser-use workflows are unusable with text-only main models; operators must switch to an expensive multimodal main model just to get screenshot readback.
- What changed:
  - The browser screenshot action now optionally runs the captured image through a configured vision model and returns a text description instead of an image block. This is controlled by new fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, etc.). When unconfigured, behavior is unchanged.
  - Vision descriptions are wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` (same as snapshot/tabs results) to mitigate prompt injection from untrusted web page content.
  - Local screenshot file size is checked against `maxBytes` before sending to the vision provider.
  - `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` in `src/media-understanding/runtime-types.ts` gains `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields, forwarded through `describeImageFileWithModel` to `describeImage` — enabling auth profile selection for all callers of this API, not just the browser tool.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the agent loop, replay-history, session persistence, transport layer, or any other tool. The vision call is entirely contained within the browser extension's screenshot action. The existing `tools.media.image` config and `image_describe` tool are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: browser-use with text-only models
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` (20 tests)
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `isBrowserVisionEnabled` returns true/false based on config presence, `enabled: false`, empty models, missing provider/model, CLI-type entries
  - `describeBrowserImageWithVision` calls through to `describeImageFileWithModel` with correct params including prompt, timeout, profile, preferredProfile
  - Local file paths as `mediaUrl` are not forwarded (only HTTP(S) URLs)
  - HTTP(S) `mediaUrl` is correctly forwarded
  - Fallback to next candidate when primary fails
  - Empty model response treated as failure and falls through
  - All candidates failing produces aggregate error with per-candidate details
  - `maxChars` truncation with `[truncated]` marker
  - `maxBytes` enforcement via file stat before vision call
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarded to `describeImageFileWithModel`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The helper is a pure function with injected deps — unit tests cover all branches without needing a real browser or vision API.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `browser-tool.test.ts` mock infrastructure extended to include `describeImageFileWithModel` dep injection.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields on the top-level `browser` namespace for vision model configuration:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```
  Supported fields: `models[]` (with `provider`, `model`, `prompt`, `maxChars`, `maxBytes`, `timeoutSeconds`, `profile`, `preferredProfile`), `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`.
- When `browser.models` contains at least one valid provider/model entry and `visionEnabled` is not `false`, every browser screenshot is automatically described by the configured vision model. The tool returns a text content block (`[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]\n<wrapped description>`) instead of an image content block. The description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` to guard against prompt injection.
- When `browser.models` is **not** configured, behavior is identical to before — screenshots return image content blocks.
- Set `visionEnabled: false` to force-disable vision without removing the model list.
- If vision description fails (API error, timeout, empty response, file exceeds `maxBytes`), the tool falls back to returning the original image block plus a warning text block so the agent loop can still recover.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` now accepts optional `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields. This is a backward-compatible additive change to the media-understanding runtime contract. All existing callers are unaffected; new callers (like browser vision) can pass auth profile selection.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (text-only main model):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block
    -> transport replaces with "[image omitted]"
    -> agent cannot reason about page

After (browser.models configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool captures screenshot to disk
    -> stat file size → check maxBytes
    -> tool calls vision model (describeImageFileWithModel)
    -> wrapExternalContent(description)
    -> tool returns wrapped text description
    -> text-only main model reads description normally

After (browser.models NOT configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — reuses existing `describeImageFileWithModel` which handles auth through the standard provider registry, SecretRef mechanism, and (now) optional auth profiles.
- New/changed network calls? Yes — when `browser.models` is configured, the browser tool makes an additional API call to the configured vision provider to describe the screenshot.
  - Risk: The screenshot image is sent to the configured vision provider's API endpoint.
  - Mitigation: This is opt-in (only when operator explicitly configures `browser.models`). The image data flow is identical to `tools.media.image` / `image_describe` tool — same `describeImageFileWithModel` pipeline, same auth, same SSRF guards.
  - Risk: Vision model descriptions contain untrusted web page content that could include prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: All vision description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent(text, { source: "browser", includeWarning: true })`, matching the security treatment applied to snapshot and tabs results.
  - Risk: Raw screenshot could be auto-delivered to chat channels if exposed as deliverable media (related: #44759).
  - Mitigation: Addressed — the vision-success path does not include any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. The raw screenshot file remains on disk for diagnostic purposes only but is not advertised as deliverable media, preventing auto-send of sensitive page content to chat channels.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (darwin)
- Runtime: Node 22, OpenClaw dev build from source (npm link)
- Model/provider: Main model `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview` (text-only); vision model `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
- Integration/channel: Control UI webchat
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure `browser.models` with a vision-capable provider/model
2. Start gateway, open dashboard
3. In a new session, ask the agent to take a browser screenshot (e.g. "open https://example.com and take a screenshot")

### Expected

- Agent receives a text description of the screenshot instead of an image block
- Text-only main model can read and reason about the page content
- Tool result includes `[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]` attribution
- Description text is wrapped in external content markers

### Actual

- Confirmed working: agent received text description, main model reasoned about page content normally

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: Gateway log shows `[browser/vision] described screenshot via bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro (chars=NNN)` on successful vision call
- [x] Failing before + passing after: Before config, agent sees `[imag

[truncated]
```

per_label_rationale.label: ui_tui
per_label_rationale.rationale: The deliverable is TUI startup conversation summary display behavior — when and what summary the TUI renders at startup/connection; the TUI is the changed surface.

per_label_rationale.label: sessions
per_label_rationale.rationale: Central change scopes the startup listSessions query to the current agent and excludes global/unknown sessions, constraining session listing/scoping behavior.

excluded_label_rationale.label: reliability
excluded_label_rationale.rationale: Skipping summary fetch on reconnect prevents duplicate appended messages, but this is framed as a TUI display gating decision rather than a core operational failure-mode fix.

excluded_label_rationale.label: gateway
excluded_label_rationale.rationale: The summary is fetched from the Gateway API but the change lives in the TUI client; gateway-owned routing/state/lifecycle is not modified.

excluded_label_rationale.label: api_surface
excluded_label_rationale.rationale: It uses the existing sessions.list contract without changing the external contract shape.

ambiguity.why_not_hard: The owning surface (TUI startup summary) is explicit; only the sessions-vs-mechanism and reliability boundary require minor judgment.

input.title: fix(harness): route CLI runtime aliases (claude-cli, google-gemini-cli) through PI harness
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #83552: fix(harness): route CLI runtime aliases (claude-cli, google-gemini-cli) through PI harness
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 83552
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/83552
- Title: fix(harness): route CLI runtime aliases (claude-cli, google-gemini-cli) through PI harness
- State: OPEN
- Author: SkyWolfDreamer
- Labels: agents, size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🦐 gold shrimp, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: ⏳ waiting on author

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `agentRuntime.id` is set to a CLI runtime alias (`claude-cli`, `google-gemini-cli`), `selectAgentHarnessDecision` throws `MissingAgentHarnessError` because CLI aliases are not registered as plugin harnesses — they are dispatched internally via the cli-backend.
- **Why it matters:** Any session using `claude-cli` as `agentRuntime` crashes on compaction (context overflow) and on any other code path that calls `selectAgentHarness` directly. Sessions become permanently unrecoverable once context exceeds the limit.
- **What changed:** Added a guard in `selectAgentHarnessDecision` that detects CLI runtime aliases via `isCliRuntimeAlias()` and routes them to the PI harness (which dispatches through the cli-backend), parallel to the existing implicit-codex fallback.
- **What did NOT change:** Normal plugin harness selection, explicit `pi`/`auto` routing, codex implicit fallback, and the cli-backend dispatch path are all untouched.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Sessions with `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }` crash on compaction with `MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.`
- **Environment:** WSL2 Ubuntu, OpenClaw 2026.5.16-beta.7, gateway local, anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 + claude-opus-4-7 via claude-cli
- **Steps:** Rebuilt OpenClaw, restarted gateway, sent messages to a session at 143% context (1.5m/1.0m tokens) that previously failed compaction
- **Evidence after fix:**
[agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-opus-4-7 durationMs=135114 rawLines=322
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=sonnet ... activeSessions=2


No `MissingAgentHarnessError` in logs. Session compacted and continued successfully.
- **Before evidence:** `[diagnostic] lane task error: error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."` and `[ws] ⇄ res ✗ sessions.compact errorCode=UNAVAILABLE` on every compaction attempt.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `selectAgentHarnessDecision` has a special case for implicit `codex` but no equivalent for CLI runtime aliases. CLI aliases are not plugin harnesses — they are backend aliases dispatched by PI's cli-backend. The utility `isCliRuntimeAlias()` already existed in `model-runtime-aliases.ts` but wasn't called in the harness selection path.
- **Contributing context:** Any config using `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }` (the documented way to pin Claude models to Claude Code) hits this on context overflow.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[compaction] -> selectAgentHarness("claude-cli") -> MissingAgentHarnessError -> session dead

After:
[compaction] -> selectAgentHarness("claude-cli") -> isCliRuntimeAlias() -> PI harness -> cli-backend -> ok
Security Impact
New permissions/capabilities? No
Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
New/changed network calls? No
Command/tool execution surface changed? No
Data access scope changed? No
Compatibility / Migration
Backward compatible? Yes
Config/env changes? No
Migration needed? No
Risks and Mitigations
Risk: PI harness receiving a claude-cli runtime policy may behave differently in edge cases.
Mitigation: PI harness already dispatches claude-cli provider sessions through cli-backend (confirmed by logs); this patch aligns the compaction path with what the main session path already does.
```

**Real behavior proof**

**Risk before merge**
- The new alias branch can route an invalid provider/runtime pairing, such as an OpenAI model pinned to `claude-cli`, through PI instead of reporting the explicit runtime configuration error.

```

Best possible solution:

Keep the PI fallback for provider-owned CLI aliases, preserve `MissingAgentHarnessError` for mismatched explicit aliases, and add focused selection coverage for valid and invalid alias pairs.

- State: OPEN
- Author: charlescha615
- Labels: bug, regression, P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

Yes

### Summary

## Bug: MCP server `tools/list` uses notification instead of request/response

**OpenClaw version:** 2026.4.15 (macOS)

**Summary:**  
When OpenClaw MCP server (`openclaw mcp serve`) is used as a stdio MCP server, it sends `tools/list` as a notification (no `id` field) instead of responding to a client request (with matching `id`). This violates the MCP protocol specification and causes all standard MCP clients (tested with Hermes Agent) to fail with "Method not found" when trying to list available tools.

**Environment:**
- OpenClaw CLI: 2026.4.15
- macOS (Darwin arm64)
- Node: 25.9.0
- Gateway: LaunchAgent

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Start OpenClaw MCP server: `openclaw mcp serve --token-file <token>`
2. Connect a standard MCP client (e.g. Hermes Agent) configured to connect via stdio
3. Client sends `initialize` → succeeds
4. Client sends `tools/list` request (with `id`) → server responds with `Method not found`
5. Server sends `notifications/tools/list` (notification, no `id`) → client receives tools but doesn't know which request it answers

**Expected behavior (per MCP protocol):**  
Server should respond to `tools/list` request (with matching `id`) with a `tools/list` response containing the list of tools.

**Actual behavior:**  
Server sends `notifications/tools/list` as a fire-and-forget notification with no `id`, which standard MCP clients cannot correlate to a pending request.

**Additional context:**  
- `initialize` works correctly (request/response with `id`)
- Other tools (e.g. `memory_search`) fail because clients cannot discover them via `tools/list`
- Hermes Agent reports "Method not found" for `tools/list`
- Hermes MCP debug shows the server actually pushes tools asynchronously via notification, not in response to client request

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start OpenClaw MCP server: `openclaw mcp serve --token-file <token>`
2. Connect a standard MCP client (e.g. Hermes Agent) configured to connect via stdio
3. Client sends `initialize` → succeeds
4. Client sends `tools/list` request (with `id`) → server responds with `Method not found`
5. Server sends `notifications/tools/list` (notification, no `id`) → client receives tools but doesn't know which request it answers

### Expected behavior

Server should respond to `tools/list` request (with matching `id`) with a `tools/list` response containing the list of tools.

### Actual behavior

Server sends `notifications/tools/list` as a fire-and-forget notification with no `id`, which standard MCP clients cannot correlate to a pending request.


### OpenClaw version

2026.4.15 (macOS)

### Operating system

macOS (Darwin arm64)

### Install method

_No response_

### Model

minimax

### Provider / routing chain

none

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell

```

### Impact and severity

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_
```

<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Land or replace https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68800 with a startup-level stdout reservation for `openclaw mcp serve`, keep the SDK-backed `tools/list` request handler path, and include raw stdio proof.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, for the strict-stdio failure at source level: `openclaw mcp serve` is documented as stdio, the SDK writes protocol frames to stdout, and current CLI/config startup can emit diagnostics before the MCP stream is protected. The exact `notifications/tools/list` diagnosis still needs live/raw stdio confirmation.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes in direction, but not finished while the linked PR remains open. The narrow maintainable solution is to suppress non-protocol stdout before CLI/config preflight and prove `tools/list` over raw stdio.

Label justifications:

- `P1`: The report describes a regression that blocks strict MCP stdio clients from discovering OpenClaw channel tools, breaking an agent/channel workflow for real users.

What I checked:

- **Current channel MCP server does not reserve stdout before config resolution:** `serveOpenClawChannelMcp` creates the channel MCP server before creating the stdio transport, and the server creation path resolves runtime config without a `routeLogsToStderr()` call in this file. ([`src/mcp/channel-server.ts:73`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/eb814b021648/src/mcp/channel-server.ts#L73), [eb814b021648](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/eb814b021648))
- **CLI preAction runs before the MCP serve action:** The shared preAction hook resolves startup policy and runs `ensureCliExecutionBootstrap` before command action code can call `serveOpenClawChannelMcp`. - **MCP command policy does not mark stdout as suppressed:** The `mcp` command catalog entry only bypasses the network proxy; it does not bypass config guard or opt into the same stdout suppression used by JSON/protocol-like paths. ([`src/cli/command-catalog.ts:282`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/eb814b021648/src/cli/command-catalog.ts#L282), [eb814b021648](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/eb814b021648))
- **Config preflight can emit stdout diagnostics:** Doctor config preflight emits `Config warnings` through `note(...)`, matching the kind of non-JSON output that can corrupt a stdio MCP stream before the server action starts. ([`src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts:148`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/eb814b021648/src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts#L148), [eb814b021648](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/eb814b021648))
- **Documented stdio contract:** The public docs state that `openclaw mcp serve` starts a stdio MCP server owned by the client, so stdout must stay protocol-safe for strict clients. Public docs: [`docs/cli/mcp.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/mcp). ([`docs/cli/mcp.md:39`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/mcp), [eb814b021648](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/eb814b021648))
- **Upstream MCP SDK contract:** The pinned `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk@1.29.0` `StdioServerTransport` serializes JSON-RPC messages to `process.stdout`, and `McpServer` registers a `tools/list` request handler for registered tools; current source does not show a `notifications/tools/list` primary response path.

input.title: Feature: config-level default task notify policy
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #64081: Feature: config-level default task notify policy
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 64081
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64081
- Title: Feature: config-level default task notify policy
- State: OPEN
- Author: ancxlol
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

Background task completion notifications (subagent and ACP) default to `done_only` policy, which sends a delivery message to the requester channel (e.g. Telegram) for every task completion. 

When running multiple sub-agents or ACP sessions, this creates notification spam with messages like:
```
Background task done: my-task-label (run 11054).
Background task failed: my-task-label (run 11037). Permission prompt unavailable in non-interactive mode
```

These messages are in a fixed format that is not user-friendly or natural language. The agent (main session) already receives the completion event internally and can relay results to the user in natural language — making the raw task notification redundant and noisy.

## Current behavior

- `done_only` is hardcoded as default for subagent/ACP tasks in `task-registry-*.js`
- Cron tasks correctly default to `silent`
- The only way to change policy is per-task via `openclaw tasks notify <id> silent`, which is racy (notification may fire before the policy change takes effect)
- There is no config key to set a default notify policy

## Proposed solution

Add a configuration key to allow users to set the default task notify policy, for example:

```json5
{
  agents: {
    defaults: {
      tasks: {
        defaultNotifyPolicy: "silent"  // or "done_only" | "state_changes"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Or at a more granular level per runtime type:

```json5
{
  agents: {
    defaults: {
      tasks: {
        notifyPolicy: {
          subagent: "silent",
          acp: "silent",
          cron: "silent",   // already silent
          cli: "silent"     // already silent
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

This would allow users who prefer the agent to handle user-facing updates to suppress raw task notifications, while keeping the default `done_only` behavior for users who want it.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.4.8
- Channel: Telegram (direct chat)
- Use case: heavy sub-agent/ACP orchestration with agent-mediated user updates
```

<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Add one backward-compatible `agents.defaults.tasks` notify-default contract, resolve it before ACP, subagent, and Task Flow records are created, preserve current defaults when unset, and document the precedence rules.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, for the configuration gap: current-main source inspection shows hardcoded notify defaults and strict agent-defaults config with no task notify key. This is source-reproducible feature work rather than a failing bug reproduction.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Unclear on exact shape because there is no patch and the public config contract needs maintainer choice. The maintainable direction is one coherent, backward-compatible default policy shared across task registry, Task Flow, ACP, and subagent creation.

Label justifications:

What I checked:

- **task_registry_default_hardcoded:** `ensureNotifyPolicy` still returns the caller-supplied policy when present, otherwise falls back to `silent` only for non-deliverable tasks and `done_only` for deliverable task records; no config value is consulted. ([`src/tasks/task-registry.ts:345`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/48acdd3d85ea/src/tasks/task-registry.ts#L345), [48acdd3d85ea](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/48acdd3d85ea))
- **taskflow_default_hardcoded:** Task Flow has its own defaulting helper that still returns `notifyPolicy ?? "done_only"`, so a config-level default would need coordinated handling outside the main task registry too. ([`src/tasks/task-flow-registry.ts:158`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/48acdd3d85ea/src/tasks/task-flow-registry.ts#L158), [48acdd3d85ea](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/48acdd3d85ea))
- **agent_defaults_schema_has_no_task_notify_key:** `AgentDefaultsSchema` is strict and exposes `subagents` defaults, but not `tasks`, `defaultNotifyPolicy`, or a per-runtime task notify map. ([`src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts:253`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/48acdd3d85ea/src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts#L253), [48acdd3d85ea](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/48acdd3d85ea))
- **agent_defaults_type_has_no_task_notify_key:** The exported `AgentDefaultsConfig` type has `subagents` defaults such as concurrency, model, timeout, and announce timeout, but no task notification default field. ([`src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts:433`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/48acdd3d85ea/src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts#L433), [48acdd3d85ea](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/48acdd3d85ea))
- **subagent_creation_omits_notify_policy:** Subagent task creation passes runtime, owner, child session, run id, delivery status, and timing, but no `notifyPolicy`, so it inherits registry defaults. ([`src/agents/subagent-registry-run-manager.ts:521`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/48acdd3d85ea/src/agents/subagent-registry-run-manager.ts#L521), [48acdd3d85ea](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/48acdd3d85ea))
- **acp_creation_omits_notify_policy:** ACP spawn task creation still calls `createRunningTaskRun` without `notifyPolicy`, leaving deliverable ACP tasks governed by the registry default unless a caller explicitly supplies policy. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn.ts:1470`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/48acdd3d85ea/src/agents/acp-spawn.ts#L1470), [48acdd3d85ea](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/48acdd3d85ea))

- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue

- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/55888
- Title: [Feature]: 🚀 [Performance Insight] Unlocking 26.7k Context Window on M4 Pro: Fixing the 8k Compaction Lag (64GB RAM Only)
- State: OPEN
- Author: Vic-HA
- Labels: enhancement, P3, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Optimization report for OpenClaw 2026.3.24 (cff6dc9) on Mac Mini M4 Pro (64GB RAM). Identified a hidden ~4k token system buffer in the memoryFlush logic that causes unnecessary background compaction (20-30s latency) at only 8k tokens. Successfully increased usable context to 26.7k tokens with <1,000ms latency.

Target Model Details:

Model: ollama/qwen2.5-coder:7b (High-performance coding model)

Quantization: Q4_K_M (Standard Ollama default)

Native Context Window: 32,768 tokens

Actual Performance: On M4 Pro 64GB, this model is capable of near-instant inference, but was throttled by OpenClaw's 8k compaction threshold.

### Problem to solve

Default compaction thresholds are too conservative for 64GB RAM machines, triggering frequent summarization loops that ignore the hardware's unified memory capacity.

The Discovery (Triple-Deduction Formula):
Through black-box testing, I've decoded the internal Threshold calculation:
Actual Threshold = ContextWindow (32768) - reserveTokensFloor - SystemBuffer (~4000).
This fixed 4k buffer prevents high-spec users from reaching the physical limit unless they understand this specific offset.

### Proposed solution

Crucial: The configuration MUST start with the agents header to ensure it correctly overrides global defaults for the specific environment.

Recommended openclaw.json Structure:
JSON
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "compaction": {
        "mode": "safeguard",
        "reserveTokensFloor": 2000,
        "keepRecentTokens": 20000
      }
    }
  }
}

### Alternatives considered

Tuning within models or providers block: Often ignored or results in Unrecognized key errors in version 2026.3.24.

Auto Mode: Prematurely triggers at 8k, wasting M4 Pro's 64GB Unified Memory.

### Impact

Latency: Dropped from 22,000ms+ to <1,000ms.

Hierarchy Clarity: Using the agents header prevents configuration conflicts and "ghost" settings.

Performance: 200% increase in usable context (26.7k vs 8.7k).

### Evidence/examples

Target Model & Hardware:

Model: ollama/qwen2.5-coder:7b (32k Context)

RAM: 64GB Unified Memory (M4 Pro)

Verification via Terminal Logs:

Test (Floor 2000 at agents level): Log Result -> threshold=26768.
(See attached screenshot for log proof)

⚠️ Requirement: This tuning is for 32GB/64GB RAM machines. Low-RAM users should avoid these settings to prevent OOM.

### Additional information

1. Configuration Snapshot & Persistence
It is important to note that modifying openclaw.json may not take immediate effect if the agent's snapshot is already active. Users should synchronize the changes to their active agent's model configuration:
cp ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json ~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/models.json
Followed by a gateway restart to ensure the new threshold is reflected in the logs.

2. Scaling for Larger Models
While this optimization works flawlessly for Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, users scaling up to 32B or 72B models on the same M4 Pro (64GB RAM) should consider increasing the reserveTokensFloor slightly (e.g., to 4000) to account for the larger KV cache memory footprint.

3. Environment Context
These observations are based on OpenClaw 2026.3.24 (cff6dc9) running on macOS 16 (Sequoia). Results may vary on Linux/Docker environments where memory management (swap) differs from macOS Unified Memory.
```

- Title: Feature Request: heartbeat.tools option to disable tools during heartbeat
- State: OPEN
- Author: andyk-ms
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
# Feature Request: `heartbeat.tools` option to disable tools during heartbeat

## Problem

When using local/small models (3-9B parameters) for heartbeat, models ignore the "do not use any tools" instruction in the prompt and make tool calls anyway. This causes:

1. **Dangerous exec calls** — e.g., 4b model ran `openclaw gateway start` repeatedly, even creating a hidden admin-level Windows scheduled task with PT5M repeat
2. **Gateway hangs** — some models (ministral 8b) get stuck in tool call loops, blocking the single-threaded agent turn
3. **Wasted compute** — models that "work" (granite 3b) still make harmless tool calls, taking 5-6s instead of <1s

Only large cloud models (Claude Opus) reliably follow "do not use tools" because they have stronger instruction-following discipline.

## Tested Models

| Model | Size | Popup/Dangerous | Tools Called | Duration | Verdict |
|-------|------|-----------------|-------------|----------|---------|
| Claude Opus | Cloud | No | No | 2.5s | ✅ Perfect |
| granite4:3b | 2.1GB | No | Harmless only | 5.7s | ✅ Best local |
| qwen3.5-claude-distilled:9b | 10GB | No | Harmless | 78s | Too slow |
| qwen3.5-claude-distilled:4b | 5.3GB | Yes (`gateway start`) | Dangerous | ~30s | ❌ |
| deepseek-r1:8b | 5.2GB | — | No tool support | — | ❌ |
| devstral-small-2:24b | 15GB | No | Harmless | Too slow | ❌ |
| ministral-3:8b | 6GB | — | Hung gateway | — | ❌ |

## Proposed Solution

Add a `heartbeat.tools` config option:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "heartbeat": {
        "every": "30m",
        "model": "ollama/granite4:3b",
        "tools": false,
        "lightContext": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

When `tools: false`, the heartbeat agent turn would not include any tool definitions in the request. This would:
- Make small local models reliable for heartbeat (just text in, text out)
- Reduce token count significantly (tool definitions are large)
- Prevent any possibility of dangerous tool execution during heartbeat
- Enable fully local heartbeat without cloud model dependency

## Environment

- OpenClaw v2026.3.8, Windows 10, 3× NVIDIA T1000 8GB
- Ollama 0.18.2 for local models
- Heartbeat with `lightContext: true` and custom prompt

```

Should the 	ools: false option apply to ALL tools, or should there be a way to whitelist certain safe tools (like read-only memory tools)?\n2. Do you have a preference for the config path? ^Ggents.defaults.heartbeat.tools or ^Ggents.defaults.heartbeat.includeTools (with array of allowed tools)?\n3. Would you like to help test this feature once implemented?\n\nThe proposed config structure looks good. We'd need to modify the agent turn construction to conditionally include tool definitions based on this setting.

This looks like a legitimate feature gap on current `main`.

Current heartbeat config already supports a few scope/cost controls:
- per-heartbeat model override,
