Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When a user is in an existing Discord thread and asks the agent to start a coding session, the correct UX is `/acp spawn <agent> --bind here` — which attaches the ACP session to the current thread in place.

However, this slash command can only be run by a human. If the user asks the **agent** to spawn the session, the agent has no programmatic equivalent. `sessions_spawn` only supports `thread: true`, which always **creates a new thread** — which is the wrong behavior here.

## Use case

1. User and agent are having a discussion in an existing Discord thread
2. User says: _"Start a Codex session on this"_
3. Expected: agent binds an ACP session to the current thread — conversation continues in place
4. Actual: agent has to ask the user to manually run `/acp spawn <agent> --bind here`, breaking the flow

## Proposed solution

Add a `bindTo: "current"` option to `sessions_spawn` that maps to `--bind here` semantics — attaches the ACP session to the current thread without creating a new one:

```json
{
  "runtime": "acp",
  "mode": "session",
  "bindTo": "current",
  "task": "..."
}
```

## Related

- #41004 — same slash command vs `sessions_spawn` asymmetry on Telegram
```

- Maintainers can comment `@clawsweeper explain` to ask for more context, or `@clawsweeper stop` to stop active automation.

</details>

**Summary**
Keep open: current `main` still has no agent-callable ACP current-conversation bind option for `sessions_spawn`; `thread: true` still follows the Discord child-thread path while `/acp spawn --bind here` remains a separate slash-command-only current-bind path.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows the tool schema and ACP params expose no current-bind field, execution forwards only `thread`, and tests still distinguish Discord child placement from slash-command current placement.

**Next step**
A focused repair can add the missing ACP current-bind tool parameter and reuse existing current-conversation binding logic without changing the child-thread default.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Add an ACP-only current-conversation binding mode to `sessions_spawn` that reuses existing `/acp spawn --bind here` validation and binding behavior while preserving `thread: true` child-thread placement.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes. Source inspection shows the tool schema and ACP params expose no current-bind field, execution forwards only `thread`, and tests still distinguish Discord child placement from slash-command current placement.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes. An additive `bindTo: "current"` or equivalent ACP-only option is the narrow maintainable fix because changing `thread: true` would break existing child-thread semantics.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: The request is a narrow agent/channel workflow improvement with a clear implementation path and limited blast radius.
- `impact:message-loss`: The current tool path can force ACP follow-up into a new Discord thread instead of the user’s active thread.
- `impact:session-state`: The issue concerns which channel conversation an ACP session remains bound to for continued interaction.

Acceptance criteria:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/acp-bind-here.integration.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/acp-spawn.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts docs/tools/acp-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md docs/channels/discord.md CHANGELOG.md

What I checked:

- **Live issue context:** The issue is still open, has no protected labels or closing pull requests, and the prior ClawSweeper review also found this same source-level gap on an older main.
- **Tool schema lacks a current-bind parameter:** `sessions_spawn` exposes `thread`, ACP `resumeSessionId`, and `streamTo`, but no `bindTo`, `bind`, or current-conversation placement parameter. ([`src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts:151`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ddeaebfc6807/src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts#L151), [ddeaebfc6807](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ddeaebfc6807))
- **Tool execution forwards only thread:** The tool normalizes `params.thread === true` and passes only `thread` into `spawnAcpDirect`, so agents have no way to request current placement. ([`src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts:357`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ddeaebfc6807/src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts#L357), [ddeaebfc6807](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ddeaebfc6807))
- **ACP params and guard still require thread for persistent sessions:** `SpawnAcpParams` has `thread?: boolean` but no current-bind field, and `spawnAcpDirect` rejects ACP `mode: "session"` unless `thread` is true. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn.ts:106`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ddeaebfc6807/src/agents/acp-spawn.ts#L106), [ddeaebfc6807](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ddeaebfc6807))
- **Discord thread spawn still defaults to child placement:** Discord advertises current-conversation binding support but keeps `defaultTopLevelPlacement: "child"`, and the ACP thread-binding preparation uses the channel default placement for `thread: true`. ([`extensions/discord/src/channel.ts:442`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ddeaebfc6807/extensions/discord/src/channel.ts#L442), [ddeaebfc6807](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ddeaebfc6807))
- **Slash command has the requested semantics:** `/acp spawn --bind here` validates current-placement support, resolves the active conversation, checks ownership, and binds with `placement: "current"`. ([`src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts:185`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ddeaebfc6807/src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts#L185), [ddeaebfc6807](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ddeaebfc6807))

(role: recent ACP/session binding contributor; confidence: high; commits: [8612af754b4d](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/8612af754b4d3ed4e0b3369e081b37f20913763a), [c42ec81e37f5](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/c42ec81e37f570250937dd3be64451bd7c5a1919), [21136238cecc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/21136238cecc657b2a37593223b8992ff8e4e546); files: `src/agents/acp-spawn.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts`, `extensions/discord/src/monitor/acp-bind-here.integration.test.ts`)

(role: recent sessions_spawn contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [efc3a52947e9](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/efc3a52947e9f0909569bf23dc9c5d1544f6aec3), [1427c3a78d80](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1427c3a78d80550e8edb26570e32856448edc9e9); files: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts`, `src/agents/acp-spawn.ts`)

input.title: Feature: Inter-session progress streaming for sessions_send (ACP/long-running session support)
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #54895: Feature: Inter-session progress streaming for sessions_send (ACP/long-running session support)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 54895
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/54895
- Title: Feature: Inter-session progress streaming for sessions_send (ACP/long-running session support)
- State: OPEN
- Author: Proita
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When `sessions_send` targets a long-running session (e.g. an ACP/Codex binding session), the caller receives a `timeout` status even though the message was successfully delivered and the target session is actively working. The caller has **no visibility** into the target session's progress (tool calls, assistant deltas, etc.) until the run completes and the announce flow posts a final summary.

This is a fundamental gap in the inter-session messaging model: it currently only supports `send → wait → done`, with no `send → subscribe → stream progress → done` pattern.

## Problem

### 1. Timeout misreports delivery status

`sessions_send` dispatches the agent run via `callGateway({ method: "agent" })` (succeeds immediately with `accepted`), then synchronously waits via `callGateway({ method: "agent.wait" })` with a default timeout of **30 seconds**.

For ACP/Codex sessions that routinely take 1–5+ minutes, `agent.wait` times out. The tool returns `{ status: "timeout" }`, which the caller interprets as failure — even though the message was already delivered and the target session is actively processing.

### 2. No progress visibility for inter-session runs

The gateway event system routes events as follows:

| Event type | Routing | Visible to caller session? |
|------------|---------|---------------------------|
| `tool` (start/end) | `toolEventRecipients.get(runId)` → registered websocket connIds only | ❌ No registration |
| `assistant` (text delta) | `broadcast("agent")` + `nodeSendToSession(targetSessionKey)` | ❌ Sent to target session only |
| `lifecycle` (end/error) | `broadcast("agent")` + cleanup | ❌ Caller is not a websocket client |
| `chat` (delta/final) | `broadcast("chat")` + `nodeSendToSession(targetSessionKey)` | ❌ Sent to target session only |

When `sessions_send` triggers a run, the caller agent uses internal RPC (`callGateway`), not a websocket connection. There is no `connId` to register for tool event receipts. All events are routed to the target session's key, never back to the originating caller session.

### 3. Announce flow is final-only

The `startA2AFlow` / `runSessionsSendA2AFlow` path is:
- Fire-and-forget (not awaited before returning the tool result)
- Only runs after the target run completes
- Posts a single final summary to the target channel
- Does **not** stream intermediate progress (tool calls, partial results, etc.)

## Impact

- Users sending work to ACP/Codex sessions via `sessions_send` see `timeout` and have no way to know the session is actively working
- No real-time visibility into tool calls, file operations, or other progress
- Users must manually poll with `sessions_history` or re-ping the session to discover status
- Particularly impactful for Telegram topic-bound sessions where the expected UX is live progress updates

## Proposed solution

### Option A: Event forwarding in announce flow (incremental)

Modify `runSessionsSendA2AFlow` (or add a parallel progress reporter) to:

1. After `startAgentRun`, subscribe to `onAgentEvent` for the dispatched `runId`
2. On `tool:start` events, forward a progress message to the caller session's channel via `callGateway({ method: "send" })`
3. On `lifecycle:end`, proceed with the existing final announce

This keeps the current architecture intact while adding a progress bridge.

### Option B: `sessions_send` with `internalEvents` subscription

Allow `sessions_send` to opt into an event stream:

```json
{
  "sessionKey": "...",
  "message": "...",
  "streamProgress": true
}
```

When `streamProgress` is true:
1. Dispatch the run as normal
2. Instead of `agent.wait`, subscribe to `onAgentEvent(runId)`
3. Forward tool events and assistant deltas back to the caller
4. Return a structured result including progress items and final reply

### Option C: New `sessions_subscribe` tool

Add a dedicated tool that lets an agent subscribe to another session's run events:

```json
sessions_subscribe(sessionKey, runId, { events: ["tool", "assistant"] })
```

This would provide real-time visibility without modifying `sessions_send`.

## Reproduction

1. Configure an ACP binding session (e.g. Codex via `acpx`)
2. From a main agent session (e.g. Telegram topic), use `sessions_send` to send a task to the ACP session
3. Observe: `sessions_send` returns `timeout` after 30 seconds
4. The ACP session is actively working (verifiable via `sessions_history` later)
5. No progress is visible in the caller session until the ACP run finishes and announce fires

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: latest (`2026.3.x`)
- Channel: Telegram (group forum with topic-bound sessions)
- ACP backend: `acpx` (Codex)

## Relevant code paths

- `createSessionsSendTool` — tool entry point, default 30s timeout
- `startAgentRun` → `callGateway({ method: "agent" })` — 10s dispatch
- `agent.wait` handler — races lifecycle + dedupe, returns `timeout` if neither resolves
- `runSessionsSendA2AFlow` — fire-and-forget announce, final-only
- `createAgentEventHandler` — event routing, tool events only to registered connIds
- `toolEventRecipients` — connId-based registry, no inter-session support

```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Proita at 2026-03-26T04:48:38Z:
## Design Proposal: `sessions_subscribe` tool for inter-session progress streaming

Following up on this issue with a concrete design proposal for **Option C** — a new `sessions_subscribe` tool that gives agents real-time visibility into another session's run progress.

---

### Motivation

When agent A uses `sessions_send` to delegate work to agent B (especially an ACP/Codex session), agent A currently has two options:

1. **Wait synchronously** (`timeoutSeconds > 0`) — but long-running ACP tasks exceed the timeout, returning a misleading `timeout` status even though the message was delivered.
2. **Fire-and-forget** (`timeoutSeconds = 0`) — avoids the timeout error, but agent A has zero visibility into what agent B is doing. The user sees nothing until the announce flow posts a final summary.

Neither option lets the caller observe progress. `sessions_subscribe` fills this gap.

---

### Proposed tool interface

```typescript
// Tool: sessions_subscribe
// Purpose: Poll or subscribe to another session's active run progress

const SessionsSubscribeToolSchema = Type.Object({
  sessionKey: Type.Optional(Type.String()),
  label: Type.Optional(Type.String({ minLength: 1, maxLength: 64 })),
  runId: Type.Optional(Type.String()),
  
  // What events to include in the result
  include: Type.Optional(Type.Array(
    Type.Union([
      Type.Literal("tool"),        // tool call start/end
      Type.Literal("assistant"),   // assistant text deltas
      Type.Literal("lifecycle"),   // run start/end/error
    ])
  )), // default: ["tool", "lifecycle"]
  
  // How long to wait for events before returning
  timeoutSeconds: Type.Optional(Type.Number({ minimum: 0, maximum: 120 })),
  // default: 30
  
  // Max events to return
  maxEvents: Type.Optional(Type.Number({ minimum: 1, maximum: 100 })),
  // default: 20
});
```

### Example usage

Agent A delegates work, then subscribes to progress:

```
// Step 1: Fire-and-forget send
sessions_send(sessionKey: "agent:codex:acp:...", message: "Refactor the auth module", timeoutSeconds: 0)
// Returns: { status: "accepted", runId: "abc-123" }

// Step 2: Subscribe to progress
sessions_subscribe(runId: "abc-123", include: ["tool", "lifecycle"], timeoutSeconds: 60)
// Returns: {
//   status: "active" | "completed" | "timeout",
//   events: [
//     { seq: 1, stream: "tool", data: { phase: "start", name: "read_file", args: { path: "src/auth.ts" } } },
//     { seq: 2, stream: "tool", data: { phase: "end", name: "read_file" } },
//     { seq: 3, stream: "tool", data: { phase: "start", name: "write_file", args: { path: "src/auth.ts" } } },
//     { seq: 4, stream: "lifecycle", data: { phase: "end" } }
//   ],
//   reply: "I've refactored the auth module..."
// }
```

### Implementation approach

This design deliberately avoids modifying the gateway's event system or `callGateway`'s request-response model. Instead, it uses a **poll-based** approach leveraging existing primitives:

#### 1. New gateway method: `agent.events` (read-only, request-response)

```typescript
// Gateway handler: "agent.events"
// Returns buffered events for a given runId

interface AgentEventsParams {
  runId: string;
  afterSeq?: number;    // only return events after this sequence number
  timeoutMs?: number;   // long-poll: wait up to N ms for new events
  include?: string[];   // event stream filters
  limit?: number;       // max events to return
}

interface AgentEventsResponse {
  runId: string;
  status: "active" | "completed" | "error" | "unknown";
  events: AgentEvent[];
  lastSeq: number;
}
```

This method reads from an **event ring buffer** that the existing `createAgentEventHandler` already processes. The key change: instead of discarding tool events when there are no websocket recipients, buffer them (bounded, e.g. last 200 events per runId) so they can be read by `agent.events`.

#### 2. Event ring buffer (new, small addition)

```typescript
// In gateway runtime state, alongside existing agentRunSeq / chatRunState:

class AgentEventBuffer {
  private buffers = new Map<string, { events: AgentEvent[]; maxSize: number }>();
  
  append(runId: string, event: AgentEvent): void { /* ring buffer append */ }
  read(runId: string, afterSeq?: number, limit?: number): AgentEvent[] { /* read from seq */ }
  cleanup(runId: string): void { /* called on lifecycle end, with delay for late readers */ }
}
```

This is a small, bounded, in-memory buffer. Events are already flowing through `createAgentEventHandler` — the buffer just retains them temporarily instead of dropping them when no websocket recipient is registered.

#### 3. Tool implementation

```typescript
// src/agents/tools/sessions-subscribe-tool.ts

export function createSessionsSubscribeTool(opts?: {
  agentSessionKey?: string;
  sandboxed?: boolean;
  config?: OpenClawConfig;
}): AnyAgentTool {
  return {
    name: "sessions_subscribe",
    label: "Session Subscribe",
    description: "Observe progress of an active run in another session. Returns tool calls, status, and final reply when available.",
    parameters: SessionsSubscribeToolSchema,
    execute: async (_toolCallId, args) => {
      // 1. Resolve session key (same pattern as sessions_send)
      // 2. Resolve runId (from args or lookup latest active run for session)
      // 3. Call gateway: agent.events(runId, afterSeq, timeoutMs, include, limit)
      // 4. If run completed, also fetch final reply via chat.history
      // 5. Return structured result with events + status + reply
    },
  };
}
```

#### 4. Changes to existing code

| File | Change | Risk |
|------|--------|------|
| `src/gateway/agent-event-handler.ts` | Add `eventBuffer.append(evt.runId, evt)` call in the event handler | Low — additive only |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` | Add `agent.events` handler | Low — new method, no existing changes |
| `src/agents/tools/sessions-subscribe-tool.ts` | New file | None — additive |
| `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` | Register `createSessionsSubscribeTool` | Low — additive |
| Tool visibility / access control | Reuse existing `sessionVisibility` + `a2aPolicy` guards | None — reuse |

### Why poll-based instead of streaming

1. **`callGateway` is request-response** — no need to add streaming/websocket plumbing to the internal RPC layer
2. **LLM tool calls are inherently request-response** — the model calls a tool and gets a result back; long-lived streams don't fit the tool-call model
3. **Long-poll gives near-real-time UX** — with `timeoutSeconds: 60`, the tool waits for events and returns as soon as new ones arrive
4. **The agent can call `sessions_subscribe` in a loop** — using `afterSeq` to paginate through events, similar to how chat polling works
5. **Bounded memory** — ring buffer per runId with auto-cleanup means no unbounded growth

### Security / access control

- Same visibility guards as `sessions_send` / `sessions_history`
- `a2aPolicy` check for cross-agent subscription
- `sandboxed` mode restricts to spawned sessions only
- Events are read-only — no mutation

### Alternative considered: Enhancing `sessions_send` directly

Adding a `streamProgress: true` flag to `sessions_send` was considered but rejected because:
- It would make `sessions_send` sometimes return quickly (fire-and-forget) and sometimes block for minutes (streaming mode) — inconsistent API surface
- Streaming within a single tool call doesn't work well with LLM tool-call semantics (models expect a single response)
- Separating "send" and "subscribe" gives the agent more control over when/how to check progress

---

### Open questions

1. **Should `sessions_subscribe` support subscribing by `label` in addition to `sessionKey`?** — Probably yes, for consistency with `sessions_send`.
2. **Event buffer size / retention** — What's a reasonable default? Suggestion: 200 events per runId, auto-cleanup 60s after run end.
3. **Should `agent.events` support a `sinceTs` (timestamp) filter in addition to `afterSeq`?** — Useful for late subscribers who missed early events.
4. **Should this be gated behind a config flag initially?** — e.g. `tools.sessions.subscribeEnabled: true`

Body:
```markdown
Right now, when I spawn a Codex or Claude Code session from Discord, I can see what the agent is doing (especially with `/reasoning on`), but I can't talk to it while it's working. If it goes off track, my only option is to kill it and start over.

In a terminal, Claude Code and Codex let you type corrections mid-task, answer questions the agent asks, and redirect it on the fly. The output streaming already works in Discord. The missing piece is the input side.

**Proposed behavior:**

When an ACP session is running in a Discord thread, messages sent in that thread should go to the running agent as input while it's working, not queue up for after it finishes.

Specifically:
- Send a message, the agent gets it while it's working (not after the current turn ends)
- Users can correct, redirect, or answer questions without killing the session
- Works for both ACP (Codex, Claude Code) and sub-agent sessions
- Output streaming already works today via `/reasoning on`. This just adds the input path

**Current workarounds:**

- Having the orchestrating agent relay messages back and forth using `process submit`, which is clunky and adds latency
- Using `openclaw tui` in a terminal, which works but defeats the point of having Discord integration
- Killing and re-spawning when something goes wrong, losing all progress

**Related issues:**

- #23580 (ACP thread-bound agents, merged)
- #28511 (ACP runtime plugin)
- #28484 (ACP file write bug in Discord threads)

The thread-bound session infrastructure and output streaming already exist. This is about completing the loop by adding interactive input.
```

**Summary**
Keep open: current main and the latest release still lack a live ACP active-turn input primitive, while sub-agent steering is implemented as abort-and-restart. The related Discord queue PR is adjacent, but it does not provide the requested ACP/sub-agent live-input contract.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: start one ACP runTurn and submit a bound reply or /acp steer for the same session; current main serializes that second turn until the active one exits. Native sub-agent steering is also source-visible as abort-and-restart rather than same-turn input.

**Next step**
Maintainers need to choose the ACP live-input contract and sub-agent live-vs-restart semantics before a safe implementation lane can proceed.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Define a backend-advertised ACP live-input capability with documented fallback behavior, then explicitly decide whether native sub-agent chat steering should become same-turn input or remain restart-based.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, source-level: start one ACP runTurn and submit a bound reply or /acp steer for the same session; current main serializes that second turn until the active one exits. Native sub-agent steering is also source-visible as abort-and-restart rather than same-turn input.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Unclear until maintainers choose the product/API boundary. The maintainable path is capability-gated ACP live input with documented fallback semantics, plus an explicit decision on sub-agent live input versus restart steering.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a normal-priority chat/agent control improvement with clear source evidence, limited blast radius, and a required product/API decision.
- `impact:message-loss`: Messages intended to steer a running chat-surface agent are delayed into later turns or restart flows instead of reaching the active session.
- `impact:session-state`: The requested behavior is about whether active ACP and sub-agent session state can be corrected while the run is still in progress.

Acceptance criteria:

- Live Discord or Telegram thread proof showing an ACP or sub-agent steering message reaches the active run before the prior turn exits, with private details redacted
- Docs verification for https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents, https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/steer, and https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents after any contract change

- **acp_runtime_has_no_live_input_method:** The ACP runtime interface exposes ensureSession, startTurn/runTurn, status/config controls, cancel, and close, but no active-turn input injection method or live-input capability flag. input.title: ACP parent session stuck until refresh when yielded waiting for child completion
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #52249: ACP parent session stuck until refresh when yielded waiting for child completion
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 52249
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/52249
- Title: ACP parent session stuck until refresh when yielded waiting for child completion
- State: OPEN
- Author: dapzthelegend
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
When an ACP child session completes while a parent session is yielded waiting for the result, the parent session remains stuck/non-responsive until the user manually refreshes the UI.

Root cause: The ACP parent-stream relay was using transcript inspection (`isYieldedParentAwaitingResume()`) and direct gateway re-entry (`resumeYieldedParent()`) instead of the normal system-event + heartbeat wake path. This ad-hoc resumption path can leave parent session state wedged.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open control-ui or web chat
2. Spawn ACP child: `sessions_spawn({ runtime: "acp", agentId: "codex", task: "..." })`
3. Yield parent: `sessions_yield()`
4. Wait for child to complete
5. **Observe:** Parent session appears stuck, not responsive
6. **Verify:** Manual refresh required to unblock chat

## Expected Behavior

Parent session automatically resumes when child completes, without manual intervention.

## Root Cause

In `src/acp/parent-stream-relay.ts` (compiled to `dist/auth-profiles-CCgh0vEZ.js`):

The `emitOrResume(...)` helper was doing:
```
if (isYieldedParentAwaitingResume(transcript)) {
  resumeYieldedParent(...)  // ← direct gateway re-entry
} else {
  enqueueSystemEvent() + requestHeartbeatNow()
}
```

This transcript-sniffing + direct-re-entry approach bypasses the normal session wake scheduler, causing state inconsistency.

## The Fix Applied

We deployed a Phase A stabilization patch that changed the relay to:
```
always: enqueueSystemEvent() + requestHeartbeatNow()
never: direct resumeYieldedParent() call
```

This forces all ACP completion follow-ups back onto the existing system-event + heartbeat wake path.

## Files Affected

- `src/acp/parent-stream-relay.ts` (source)
- `dist/auth-profiles-CCgh0vEZ.js` (compiled, where patch was applied)

## Workaround Status

**Phase A patch deployed locally.** Tests show parent now auto-resumes on child completion (without manual refresh), but relies on heartbeat wake machinery rather than dedicated yield-resume path.

This is a stabilization patch. The proper long-term fix is:

## Proper Fix (Phase B)

Implement explicit `yieldWait` state tracking in the runtime:
1. Persist parent yield-wait state when `sessions_yield` is called
2. Emit formal `ParentYieldWaitLifecycleEvent` on child completion
3. Use dedicated `resumeAcpYieldWaitingParent()` path (code already exists but unused)
4. Clear wait state on completion
5. Do not rely on transcript inspection or ad-hoc gateway calls

## System Information

- **OpenClaw:** 2026.3.13 (61d171a)
- **Node:** 25.8.1
- **Affected:** All ACP spawn + yield workflows in browser UIs

## Impact

**Severity:** High

- Makes ACP tasks in threads/web chat appear frozen
- User must manually refresh to proceed
- Degrades UX for code review, long-running tasks in browser
- Affects: control-ui, web chat, any browser-based ACP spawn

## Current Status

Phase A patch has been applied and partially tested. Parent auto-resume now works, but the architecture still couples to heartbeat wake machinery rather than having proper yield-resume semantics.

Full proper fix requires implementing the Phase B architecture with explicit yield-wait state and lifecycle events.

```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Ryce at 2026-03-22T17:20:20Z:
The root cause here — an ad-hoc resumption path that bypasses the normal system-event + heartbeat wake path — is a classic silent state-divergence pattern. The parent session's internal state says "waiting for child" while the actual state is "child done, result available." No notification fires, so the session stays wedged until a manual UI refresh corrects the view.

KeepMyClaw (keepmyclaw.com) addresses this category of problem through external state snapshots at defined boundaries. If the parent session had an independent encrypted snapshot taken at yield-time, and another snapshot when the child completes, the discrepancy between "internal belief" and "actual state" would be detectable without UI refresh.

The ad-hoc resume path is doing the same job as a snapshot boundary — capturing state at a transition point — but it's doing it in-memory only. When the gateway restarts mid-yield, that in-memory state is gone. An external encrypted snapshot at the yield boundary survives the restart and enables proper resumption instead of manual refresh.

- Artyomkun at 2026-03-22T22:37:11Z:
@Ryce This is a clean example of state-divergence — parent session's internal state never receives the child completion event.

@steipete 

**The Phase A fix (system-event + heartbeat) works** but it's a workaround, not a proper fix.

**Phase B (yieldWait state) is the right direction:**
1. Persist `yieldWait` state when `sessions_yield` is called
2. Child completion triggers `ParentYieldWaitLifecycleEvent`
3. Dedicated `resumeAcpYieldWaitingParent()` path handles resumption
4. No transcript inspection, no ad‑hoc gateway calls

**Why this matters:**
- Without it, ACP workflows in web UI feel broken
- Users have to refresh to continue — kills flow for long tasks
- The architecture should support this without heartbeat coupling

**Impact:** High. ACP is core to complex workflows (code review, multi‑step tasks). This fix would make it reliable and feel native.

Thanks for the detailed root cause — looking forward to Phase B landing.

- GMTekAI at 2026-03-22T22:51:03Z:
Current main does not appear to match the root-cause theory in the latest comments.

The old path called out in the report (`src/acp/parent-stream-relay.ts`) is gone. In current source, the ACP parent relay lives in `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`, and the relay path already goes through `enqueueSystemEvent(...)` plus `requestHeartbeatNow(...)` before waking the parent session (`src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts:181-194`). There also is not a current `resumeYieldedParent` / `resumeAcpYieldWaitingParent` path in-tree that I can point to as the active seam.

The tests line up with that newer behavior too: `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts:51-89` explicitly asserts that child progress/completion are relayed into the parent session and that a heartbeat wake is requested.

So I would separate two things here:

- The symptom may still be real if the browser UI can stay stale until refresh.
- But the latest “main still bypasses the normal system-event + heartbeat path” explanation looks stale against current main.

If this is still reproducible on a current build, the remaining seam is more likely in the browser/control-ui subscription or refresh lifecycle after those relayed system events land, or in some other yielded-parent state transition outside this relay file, rather than the old direct-resume branch described above.


- liizfq at 2026-04-02T16:46:01Z:
Confirmed on OpenClaw latest (2026.4.x), with **heartbeat disabled**.

## Reproduction

1. Main session on Matrix (no heartbeat configured)
2. `sessions_spawn({ runtime: "acp", agentId: "claude", mode: "run", streamTo: "parent", task: "..." })`
3. `sessions_yield()` — parent yields, waiting for child
4. Claude Code completes the task (confirmed via acp-stream.jsonl log)
5. **Parent never wakes up.** No message delivered to Matrix until user sends a new message manually.

## Root Cause

I traced through the source (`dispatch-acp.runtime-O2mHRwjn.js` + `pi-embedded-CbCYZxIb.js`):

The relay path correctly calls `enqueueSystemEvent()` + `requestHeartbeatNow()`. But `requestHeartbeatNow()` (in `heartbeat-wake.ts`) queues a wake request that is consumed by a **handler registered via `setHeartbeatWakeHandler()`**. When heartbeat is not enabled, no handler is registered — the timer fires, sees `handler === null`, and returns immediately.

```
ACP complete → enqueueSystemEvent() → requestHeartbeatNow()
  → schedule(250ms) → timer fires
  → if (!handler) return;  ← nothing happens
  → system events sit in queue until next manual inbound message
```

So the Phase A fix (system-event + heartbeat wake) **only works when heartbeat is enabled**. Without heartbeat, the parent session has no delivery mechanism for ACP completion events at all.

## Impact

This makes ACP `streamTo: "parent"` fundamentally broken for any deployment that does not use heartbeat polling — which includes many lightweight / privacy-conscious setups where heartbeat is intentionally disabled.

## Suggested Fix

Phase B should not couple to heartbeat. ACP child completion should have an **independent notification path** that works without heartbeat — e.g., directly injecting an inbound message into the parent session, or using a dedicated wake primitive that does not require the heartbeat infrastructure.

Related: #54690

- GMTekAI at 2026-04-03T00:02:18Z:
Maintainer triage update: this still looks real on current `main`, but the active seam is narrower than the original root-cause writeup.

What current source shows:

- The old direct-resume theory is stale. The ACP parent relay now lives in `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`, and it already does the session-scoped path: `enqueueSystemEvent(..., { sessionKey: parentSessionKey })` followed by `requestHeartbeatNow(scopedHeartbeatWakeOptions(...))`.
- So the relay is no longer bypassing system-event + wake.

But the new heartbeat-disabled repro in this thread does match the current implementation:

- `src/infra/system-events.ts` keeps events in a session-scoped in-memory queue.
- `src/infra/heartbeat-wake.ts` only delivers those wakes via the registered heartbeat handler.
- If no heartbeat wake handler is registered, the scheduled timer returns immediately and nothing drains the queued event until some later inbound activity happens.

So the current scope looks like:

- symptom: still real
- old root-cause theory: outdated
- current active seam: ACP parent completion still depends on heartbeat wake infrastructure, and that wake path becomes a no-op when heartbeat is disabled

That means the latest report here is meaningful: `streamTo: "parent"` is not reliably self-sufficient today when heartbeat is off.

I’d treat the smallest correct next step as:

- keep this issue open
- add a regression for ACP child completion with heartbeat disabled
- decouple ACP parent completion delivery from the optional heartbeat handler, or add a dedicated wake/delivery path that works even when heartbeat polling is off

So this is not "fixed on main" yet; it is more accurately "rerouted onto heartbeat, which still leaves a gap when heartbeat is disabled."

- madders26 at 2026-04-27T03:27:00Z:
Hitting this same thing on `2026.4.23` with a native `sessions_spawn` (not ACP). Parent hangs after yield. Completion lands in session log as a `[Internal task completion event]` user message but model never reinvokes.

Looks like `subagent-spawn-DrLN9wlV.js` doesn't call `requestHeartbeatNow` the way `acp-spawn-Bs2TViGe.js:178` does. And even if it did, my `main` has no `heartbeat:` block so `runOnce` skips it (`heartbeat-runner-CRPuOpNx.js:601` + `isHeartbeatEnabledForAgent` returning false in workspaces with split heartbeat agents).

If the Phase B `yieldWait` path lands on announce-delivery instead of spawn, that'd cover both ACP and native and not depend on the agent having heartbeat configured.

Have session+trajectory JSONL if useful.

input.title: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #53319: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 53319
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/53319
- Title: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
- State: OPEN
- Author: bluk1020
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-info, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦐 gold shrimp

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When spawning two ACP sessions (`sessions_spawn runtime:"acp"`) in rapid succession (~37s apart), the first agent's CC process never starts while the second eventually works fine.

## Environment

- OpenClaw `2026.3.22`
- acpx `0.1.16`
- ACP backend: `acpx`, defaultAgent: `claude`
- Node 22.22.1, macOS (arm64)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. From a main session, call `sessions_spawn` twice in quick succession:
   - Agent 1 spawned at `02:22:28 UTC`
   - Agent 2 spawned at `02:23:05 UTC` (37s later)
2. Both return `status: "accepted"` with valid `childSessionKey` and `streamLogPath`

## Observed Behavior

**Agent 1** (`0a73e5e1`):
- `lifecycle:start` emitted at 02:22:28
- `stall` warning at 02:23:28 (60s, no output)
- **No `assistant_delta` events ever appear** — CC process never produced any output
- `ps aux | grep claude.*reachfar` shows no matching process

**Agent 2** (`ebf86699`):
- `lifecycle:start` emitted at 02:23:05
- `stall` warning at 02:24:05 (60s cold start)
- `assistant_delta` appears at 02:25:17 — CC **starts working normally**
- Progress updates continue ("Now let me read the specific files...")

## Expected Behavior

Both agents should launch successfully. A ~37s gap between spawns should not cause the first to silently fail.

## Stream Log Evidence

**Agent 1 stream log** (3 lines total, never progresses):
```
lifecycle:start → system_event:stall → (nothing)
```

**Agent 2 stream log** (resumes after cold start):
```
lifecycle:start → system_event:stall → assistant_delta ("Now let me read...") → system_event:resumed → ...
```

## Workaround

Falling back to direct `claude --print --permission-mode bypassPermissions` via exec works reliably for parallel spawns (tested 3 concurrent agents successfully).

## Analysis

The acpx backend appears to have a race condition during concurrent session initialization. The first session's CC process is either:
1. Never spawned (acpx loses the session init in concurrent handling)
2. Spawned but immediately crashes without emitting any output
3. Blocked waiting on a resource the second session acquires first

Since the session is "accepted" and `lifecycle:start` fires, the issue is downstream of session registration — likely in the acpx CLI launch path.

## Related

- #52878 — ACP backend registration regression
- #53256 — ACP completion relay fix (different bug, addresses result routing)
- #49782 — RFC: ACP completion relay
```

**Next step**
Needs maintainer-run live ACPX/Claude validation before a safe automated fix can choose the correct source boundary.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Run a live current-main smoke that starts two Claude ACP `mode:"run"` children in rapid succession and verifies both streams reach assistant output; if it still fails, fix the narrow OpenClaw/ACPX launch-health boundary with focused regression coverage.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Label justifications:

- `P2`: The report describes a real ACP subagent launch failure with limited surface area, but current-main repro status is still unclear after adjacent fixes.
- `impact:message-loss`: The reported first child session is accepted but never emits assistant output to the stream.
- `impact:session-state`: The ACP child lifecycle reaches started/stalled state without a corresponding launched process or terminal progress, indicating session-state drift.

Acceptance criteria:

- Live current-main smoke: spawn two `sessions_spawn({ runtime: "acp", agentId: "claude", mode: "run" })` children in rapid succession and verify both streams reach assistant output and terminal lifecycle.
- If code changes follow, run focused ACP manager/ACPX runtime tests around the chosen launch, runtime-control, or process-lease seam.
- For broad ACPX/runtime changes, run the relevant ACPX plugin tests plus changed checks on Testbox/Crabbox.

What I checked:

- **Historical repro context:** The issue body gives a concrete OpenClaw 2026.3.22/acpx 0.1.16 macOS repro: two ACP children were accepted 37 seconds apart, the first stream had only lifecycle start plus stall, and the second later emitted assistant output.
- **Accepted spawn is still not output-gated:** `spawnAcpDirect` dispatches the child turn through `callGateway` and later returns `status: "accepted"`; the acceptance path is not conditioned on seeing an `assistant_delta` from the ACP child. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn.ts:1413`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1c3ff34d752f/src/agents/acp-spawn.ts#L1413), [1c3ff34d752f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1c3ff34d752f))
- **Current tests still allow cross-session ACP parallelism:** The manager test expects turns for different ACP sessions to enter the runtime concurrently and reach `maxInFlight` 2, so current main has not solved this class by globally serializing ACP launches. ([`src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts:856`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1c3ff34d752f/src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts#L856), [1c3ff34d752f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1c3ff34d752f))
- **ACPX launch path now has process leases but still delegates launch:** `AcpxRuntime.ensureSession` wraps new launches with `runWithLaunchLease` and delegates to `delegate.ensureSession(...)`; the lease records ownership and cleanup metadata, but this source path is not evidence that two rapid launches both produce assistant output. ([`extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts:963`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1c3ff34d752f/extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts#L963), [1c3ff34d752f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1c3ff34d752f))
- **Adjacent Claude ACP timeout fixes are present:** Current main suppresses Claude ACP timeout wire controls and tolerates unsupported optional timeout hints, with focused tests; those fixes address later single-spawn `ACP_TURN_FAILED` reports but do not specifically validate the concurrent first-spawn launch failure. ([`extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts:1170`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1c3ff34d752f/extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts#L1170), [1c3ff34d752f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1c3ff34d752f))
- **Dependency stack materially changed since the report:** The ACPX plugin on current main is version 2026.5.19 and pins `acpx` 0.7.0 plus `@agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp` 0.33.1, much newer than the reported acpx 0.1.16 stack, but dependency age alone is not proof of a concurrent-spawn fix. ([`extensions/acpx/package.json:11`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1c3ff34d752f/extensions/acpx/package.json#L11), [1c3ff34d752f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1c3ff34d752f))

Likely related people:

- **steipete:** Authored ACPX session lifecycle cleanup near the launch/runtime boundary and contributed adjacent ACP error redaction and timeout-hint follow-up work. (role: recent ACPX lifecycle contributor; confidence: medium; commits: [42ecd5d95eae](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/42ecd5d95eae8b9fbe155a2e5bb20f32080826e3), [b11a515803a2](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b11a515803a2bb6a62cd53f295f19ea8164fc47d), [494dedbf5fef](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/494dedbf5fef7f2b2c560d62afeec3dd8de36183); files: `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-controls.ts`)

input.title: [Feature]: Visible agent-to-agent messaging for ACP thread-bound sessions (proxy-only delivery without main session creation)
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #50798: [Feature]: Visible agent-to-agent messaging for ACP thread-bound sessions (proxy-only delivery without main session creation)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 50798
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/50798
- Title: [Feature]: Visible agent-to-agent messaging for ACP thread-bound sessions (proxy-only delivery without main session creation)
- State: OPEN
- Author: ruan330
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## What

A way for a coordinator agent to send **visible** messages into an ACP thread-bound session's Discord thread, **without creating a `agent:main:discord:channel:<thread_id>` session** that causes route pollution.

## Why

### The gap

OpenClaw currently offers two paths to communicate with ACP sessions:

| Path | Visibility | Side effects |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| `sessions_send` (A2A) | ❌ Invisible — human cannot see the instruction in the thread | None |
| Human types in 🤖 thread | ✅ Visible | None |

There is no supported way for a **coordinator agent** to send a visible instruction to an ACP worker session while the human observes.

### Our workaround and its consequences

We built a dual-Discord-account proxy channel:

1. Coordinator agent calls `message(accountId:"acp")` → Bot B posts in the 🤖 thread
2. Bot A (main, `allowBots: true`) sees it → routes to the ACP session ✅
3. Human can see the instruction in the thread ✅

**But every `message(accountId:"acp")` also creates an `agent:main:discord:channel:<thread_id>` session as a side effect.** This causes:

- **Route pollution**: The main/coordinator agent gets routed into the 🤖 thread and responds there (mixing coordinator replies with worker replies)
- **Stub accumulation**: Over time, dozens of orphaned main sessions pile up
- **Discord 503 overflow**: Accumulated stubs hitting archived threads trigger Discord rate limits → gateway crash → binding adapter fails to reinitialize

### Scale context

We run 100+ concurrent ACP sessions. The proxy workaround generates stubs at scale, making the side effects significantly worse.

## Proposed solution (either would work)

### Option A: `sessions_send` with `visible: true`

```json
sessions_send({
  "key": "<acp session key>",
  "message": "...",
  "visible": true
})
```

The worker's bound thread receives the message as a visible post. No main session created.

### Option B: `message()` with `proxyOnly: true`

```json
message({
  "channelId": "<thread_id>",
  "accountId": "acp",
  "proxyOnly": true
})
```

Deliver the message but skip `agent:main:discord:channel:<thread_id>` session creation.

### Option C: ACP-bound thread exclusion rule

If a thread is bound to an ACP session, the gateway should **never** create an `agent:main:discord:channel:<thread_id>` session for it, regardless of how messages arrive.

## Related issues

- #46645 — `sessions_send` replies not mirrored into worker's bound thread (same underlying gap)
- #40325 — Supervised Agent-to-Agent Collaboration Sessions (broader version of this request)
- #41562 — Thread session delivery leaks to main session target
- #41823 — Discord Thread Session Fork Causes Context Pollution

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.3.13
- @zed-industries/claude-agent-acp: 0.22.0
- Platform: Discord dual-account (default + acp)
```

Comments/context:
```markdown

input.title: [Feature] Allow per-binding tool restrictions (tools.deny) in route/ACP bindings
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #49567: [Feature] Allow per-binding tool restrictions (tools.deny) in route/ACP bindings
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 49567
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/49567
- Title: [Feature] Allow per-binding tool restrictions (tools.deny) in route/ACP bindings
- State: OPEN
- Author: semiok
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

There is currently no way to restrict specific tools on a per-channel or per-group basis through bindings. `RouteBindingSchema` and `AcpBindingSchema` use Zod `.strict()`, which rejects any fields beyond `agentId`, `comment`, and `match`. Attempting to add `tools.deny` or similar fields causes Gateway startup to fail with `must NOT have additional properties`.

## Use Case

In a multi-agent Telegram setup, I need to prevent a specific tool (`sessions_spawn`) from being used in certain groups while allowing it in DMs. Currently the only workaround is implementing tool filtering at the MCP server level or model proxy level, which adds unnecessary complexity.

**Example desired config:**

```json
{
  "bindings": [
    {
      "agentId": "main",
      "match": { "channel": "telegram", "accountId": "default" },
      "tools": {
        "deny": ["sessions_spawn"]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Current behavior:** Gateway fails with JSON schema validation error.

## Workaround

Currently achieving this by passing `OPENCLAW_DENY_TOOLS=sessions_spawn` as an environment variable to the MCP server, which filters `tools/list` responses. This works but is fragile and invisible to the Gateway's tool management.

## Proposal

Extend `RouteBindingSchema` to accept an optional `tools` field:

```typescript
const RouteBindingSchema = z.object({
  agentId: z.string(),
  comment: z.string().optional(),
  match: MatchSchema,
  tools: z.object({
    deny: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
    allow: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
  }).optional(),
}).strict();
```

This would enable fine-grained, declarative tool control per binding without external workarounds.

## Related Context

Multi-agent setups with shared tools across channels increasingly need per-channel tool restrictions. The current all-or-nothing approach at the agent level is too coarse for production use cases.
```

input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 47596
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/47596
- Title: [Feature Request] Add tool call visibility to streamTo="parent" for ACP runtime
- State: OPEN
- Author: uf-hy
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
# [Feature Request] Add tool call visibility to streamTo="parent" for ACP runtime

## Current Behavior

When using `runtime="acp"` with `streamTo="parent"`, only the following events are streamed back to the parent session:

1. **Assistant text output** - Text deltas from the child agent
2. **Lifecycle events** - Start, end, error states

**What's missing:**
- Tool calls (which tools are being invoked)
- Tool call arguments (what parameters are passed)
- Tool results (what the tools returned)
- Thinking process (if enabled)

This makes the child agent a "black box" - you can see what it says, but not what it's doing.

## Desired Behavior

When `streamTo="parent"` is enabled, the parent session should receive progress updates including:

- 🔧 **Tool calls**: `"🔧 Calling tool: exec (command: 'ls -la')"`
- ✅ **Tool results**: `"✅ Tool result: [truncated output]"`
- 💭 **Thinking**: `"💭 Thinking: [summary]"` (if thinking is enabled)

This provides full transparency into what the child agent is doing.

## Implementation Suggestion

Modify `startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay()` in `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts` to subscribe to additional event types:

```javascript
// Current implementation only handles:
if (event.stream === "assistant") { ... }
if (event.stream === "lifecycle") { ... }

// Add support for:
if (event.stream === "tool_call") {
    emit(`🔧 ${event.data.name}(${summarizeArgs(event.data.arguments)})`, ...);
}
if (event.stream === "tool_result") {
    emit(`✅ Result: ${truncate(event.data.content, 200)}`, ...);
}
if (event.stream === "thinking") {
    emit(`💭 ${truncate(event.data.thinking, 150)}`, ...);
}
```

## Verbose Control

**Question for maintainers:** Should this behavior respect the parent session's verbose setting?

- Option A: Always show tool calls when `streamTo="parent"` is set
- Option B: Inherit parent session's `/v` (verbose) setting
- Option C: Add a new parameter like `streamVerbose: "full" | "minimal" | "inherit"`

I personally prefer **Option B** (inherit verbose setting) for consistency with existing UX.

## Use Cases

- Debugging child agent behavior in real-time
- Understanding why a task is taking long (e.g., waiting for a slow API call)
- Monitoring resource usage (e.g., seeing which files are being read)
- Educational purposes (learning how agents work)

## Related

This is a prerequisite for #[TBD] (Add streamTo support for runtime="subagent")

```

**Summary**

**Next step**
Needs maintainer product/security decision on ACP parent-stream visibility, redaction, and event structure before a fix PR is safe to queue.

**Security**
Needs attention: The requested feature is security-sensitive because it would surface child execution details into parent-session system events.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Add concise, redacted, structured ACP parent-relay progress/audit events with stable correlation IDs, explicit visibility policy, and focused relay/docs coverage.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes for source-level behavior. Start the ACP parent relay for a child run and emit `tool`, `command_output`, `approval`, `patch`, or `thinking` for the same run id; current main returns before enqueueing a parent update for those streams.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Unclear until maintainers choose the visibility and redaction policy. The implementation likely belongs in `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`, but raw string forwarding would be weaker than structured, redacted summaries aligned with the existing parent relay trust boundary.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a normal-priority ACP visibility improvement with a clear affected path but no urgent regression or outage.
- `impact:security`: The request would expose child execution details across a parent-session boundary, including potentially sensitive args, outputs, approvals, patches, and reasoning.

Security concerns:

- [medium] Define redaction before relaying child streams — `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts:233`
  Parent relay updates are requester-visible system events, so a fix should not forward raw tool args, command output, approvals, patches, results, or thinking without agreed redaction, truncation, and visibility rules.
  Confidence: 0.9

Acceptance criteria:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts

What I checked:

- **Live issue state:** The issue is still open with P2, source-repro, product-decision, and security-review labels, and it has no assignees.
- **Current ACP parent relay filters out requested streams:** `startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay()` handles assistant text and ACP/lifecycle metadata, then returns for any stream that is not lifecycle; this excludes `tool`, `command_output`, `approval`, `patch`, `item`, `plan`, and `thinking` from parent-visible updates. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts:373`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts#L373), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **Parent relay crosses a visible session boundary:** Relayed text is enqueued as a parent-session system event with the original delivery context, `forceSenderIsOwnerFalse`, and `trusted: false`, so adding execution details needs an explicit visibility/redaction policy. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts:233`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts#L233), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))

([`src/infra/agent-events.ts:5`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/src/infra/agent-events.ts#L5), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))
- **Tool and command events are emitted elsewhere:** The embedded tool handler emits sanitized `tool` start/update events and `command_output` deltas/end events, which confirms the requested data exists before the ACP parent relay filters it out. ([`src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts:913`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/1fb09069c342/src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts#L913), [1fb09069c342](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/1fb09069c342))

input.github_context:
GitHub item:

- Type: github_issue
- Number: 45841

- Title: [Feature]: Sandboxing + ACP
- State: OPEN
- Author: Thinkscape
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Sandboxed OpenClaw sessions should be able to spawn ACP sessions.
It's currently a [documented limitation](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents#sandbox-compatibility) that breaks the use of 2 very important openclaw features - sandboxing and ACP.

ACP is great for better interop with coding harnesses, while sandboxing reduces blast radius when they make mistakes!

Continuation of #32935

### Problem to solve

Users running OpenClaw inside Docker containers (sandboxed) cannot use ACP at all. Sandboxed sessions cannot spawn ACP sessions because runtime='acp' runs on the host. This forces a binary choice: give up sandboxing (weakening security) or give up ACP orchestration (losing the ability to control Claude Code, Codex, etc. from within OpenClaw). For users who run OpenClaw in Docker for security isolation but want to orchestrate coding agents on the host, there's no middle ground.


### Proposed solution

Options:
1. Allow running ACP boxes in their own sandboxes, similar to sub-agents - re-use the same docker-based spawning method.
2. Allow running ACP INSIDE the session/bot sandboxes - they're already sandboxed, but ACP provides the neat interop layer.
3. Introduce a controlled bridge/proxy mechanism that lets sandboxed sessions request ACP spawns through a narrow, auditable API surface  - ACP proxy daemon on the host that accepts spawn/steer/cancel requests from the sandboxed container over a Unix socket or localhost port, with allowlisting by agent ID and rate limiting.  - sandbox: "bridge" mode for sessions_spawn that explicitly opts into host-side ACP execution with logged privilege escalation, so operators can audit what crossed the boundary.  - Config flag like acp.allowFromSandbox: true (with appropriate warnings) that relaxes the current blanket block for users who accept the tradeoff. A proxy/bridge approach would preserve the security boundary while enabling the orchestration use case. The key constraint: the bridge should be opt-in, auditable, and narrowly scoped (only ACP spawn/steer/cancel, not general host access).- borrowed from: #32935


### Alternatives considered

• Running OpenClaw unsandboxed: works but removes the security benefits of containerization entirely.
• Ditching ACP - I keep the security of sandboxes, and run coding harnesses inside existing sandbox, but no ACP interop.
• Using runtime: "subagent" instead: works for OpenClaw-native tasks but doesn't support external coding harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI), which is the whole point of ACP.
• Manual workarounds (file-based queues, cron polling): fragile, token-wasteful, and defeats the purpose of having ACP built in.


### Impact

• Affected: Any user running OpenClaw in Docker/sandbox who wants to orchestrate external coding agents via ACP. This is likely a large portion of self-hosted users since Docker is the recommended deployment method.
• Severity: Blocks workflow. ACP is unusable from sandboxed sessions.
• Frequency: Always. Not intermittent, it's a hard block.
• Consequence: Users must either compromise security (unsandbox) or build custom polling/queue systems outside OpenClaw to achieve what ACP was designed to do natively.


### Evidence/examples

ACP docs explicitly state the limitation: https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents (Sandbox compatibility section)

• Community interest in ACP is high: @bilbeny's tweet (https://x.com/bilbeny/status/2028134377696448999) got 1k bookmarks
• @elvissun's architecture post (https://x.com/elvissun/status/2025920521871716562) shows the orchestrator pattern (OpenClaw agent controlling coding agents) that ACP enables, with 1,550 RTs and 35k bookmarks
• Our setup: OpenClaw in Docker container, Claude Code on host VPS. Currently using a bash cron polling system as a workaround, which burned significant tokens due to error handling issues.


### Additional information

_No response_
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Thinkscape at 2026-03-14T07:29:01Z:
@willmhawkins you've mentioned https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/32935#issuecomment-3990375044 but you haven't explained how you've worked around the sandboxed agent interop. Do you just tell your agents to `exec+elevate` to run the ai coding cli on the gateway?

- dsantoreis at 2026-03-14T07:37:16Z:
This is a real architectural tension. The sandbox boundary is process-level isolation (Docker), but ACP sessions need host access to spawn coding harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, etc.).

Potential approaches:

1. **Socket forwarding**: expose a Unix socket from the host ACP runtime into the sandbox container via volume mount. The sandboxed session sends spawn requests over the socket, the host-side daemon executes them. This preserves sandbox isolation for the agent's own operations while allowing controlled ACP orchestration.

2. **Sidecar pattern**: run the ACP harness manager as a sidecar container with host-level access, communicating with the sandboxed OpenClaw container over a private network. The sandboxed session gets ACP capability without breaking its own isolation.

3. **Nested sandbox**: spawn ACP sessions in their own isolated containers (rather than on the host), with a shared workspace volume. This keeps everything containerized but requires Docker-in-Docker or a container runtime accessible from within the sandbox.

Option 1 is probably the most pragmatic path — minimal surface area, clear trust boundary, and doesn't require architectural changes to how ACP harnesses run.

- Maintainers can comment `@clawsweeper explain` to ask for more context, or `@clawsweeper stop` to stop active automation.

</details>

**Summary**
Keep open: current main and the latest shipped release still intentionally block ACP spawns from sandboxed requester sessions, and the remaining request is a security/product design change rather than stale cleanup or a duplicate. The related closed issue documents a user workaround, while the newer execution-backend issue and ACP RFC are broader adjacent work, not a merged replacement for this targeted sandboxed-ACP compatibility request.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: a sandboxed requester calling sessions_spawn with runtime="acp" is blocked by availability and runtime-policy guards before ACP initialization. Existing tests cover the denial path, but this review did not run live Docker/acpx proof.

**Next step**
Needs maintainer security/product approval for the sandbox-to-host ACP bridge or placement contract before a fix PR is safe to queue.

**Security**
Needs attention: This issue is security-sensitive because it asks sandboxed sessions to request host-side ACP harness execution.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Define an explicit sandbox-to-ACP placement or bridge contract with operator opt-in, allowlisting, audit and rate-limit controls, config/schema/docs/tests, and live Docker/acpx proof before relaxing the fail-closed guard.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, source-level: a sandboxed requester calling sessions_spawn with runtime="acp" is blocked by availability and runtime-policy guards before ACP initialization. Existing tests cover the denial path, but this review did not run live Docker/acpx proof.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Unclear; the issue lists plausible bridge, sidecar, and nested-sandbox directions, but current main has no accepted sandbox-to-host ACP contract. The narrowest maintainable next step is a maintainer security/product decision for that boundary before code changes.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a normal-priority feature/security design request with a real blocked workflow but no current regression or unusable core runtime.
- `impact:security`: The request directly changes sandbox boundaries by letting sandboxed sessions request ACP harness execution that currently runs on the host.

Security concerns:

- [high] Define the ACP bridge as a constrained privilege boundary — `src/agents/acp-spawn.ts:200`
  Relaxing the current guard would let sandboxed requesters initiate host-side coding harness processes, so any implementation needs explicit operator opt-in, requester/agent allowlisting, logged spawn/steer/cancel verbs, rate limits, and clear failure modes.
  Confidence: 0.92

Acceptance criteria:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts
- Live Docker/acpx proof showing a sandboxed requester can spawn, steer, and cancel only the allowed ACP session through the approved bridge or placement contract
- Docs verification for https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents and https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents after any contract change

What I checked:

- **Current ACP availability fails closed for sandboxed requesters:** isAcpRuntimeSpawnAvailable returns false when params.sandboxed is true, so sandboxed sessions do not advertise ACP runtime spawn availability. ([`src/acp/runtime/availability.ts:10`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/06a39015f21c/src/acp/runtime/availability.ts#L10), [06a39015f21c](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/06a39015f21c))
- **Current ACP spawn policy rejects sandboxed requesters:** resolveAcpSpawnRuntimePolicyError treats requesterSandboxed as forbidden and returns the documented host-runtime sandbox error before ACP setup can proceed. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn.ts:188`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/06a39015f21c/src/agents/acp-spawn.ts#L188), [06a39015f21c](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/06a39015f21c))
- **sessions_spawn blocks runtime acp before dispatch:** createSessionsSpawnTool computes ACP availability with opts.sandboxed and returns the sandboxed-session unavailable message for runtime="acp" instead of dispatching to spawnAcpDirect. ([`src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts:236`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/06a39015f21c/src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts#L236), [06a39015f21c](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/06a39015f21c))
- **Regression tests cover the denial path:** ACP spawn tests assert sandboxed requesters and sandbox="require" fail before gateway/session initialization, and sessions_spawn tests assert sandboxed ACP calls do not reach spawnAcpDirect. ([`src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts:2112`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/06a39015f21c/src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts#L2112), [06a39015f21c](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/06a39015f21c))
- **Public docs document the current limitation:** The ACP agents guide says ACP sessions run on the host runtime, not inside the OpenClaw sandbox, and lists sandboxed requester ACP spawns plus sandbox="require" as current limitations. Public docs: [`docs/tools/acp-agents.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents). ([`docs/tools/acp-agents.md:721`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents), [06a39015f21c](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/06a39015f21c))
- **Latest release still ships the limitation:** v2026.5.12 contains the same docs and runtime guards blocking ACP from sandboxed requesters, so this is not implemented in the latest release. Public docs: [`docs/tools/acp-agents.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents). ([`docs/tools/acp-agents.md:721`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents), [f066dd2f31c2](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/f066dd2f31c2))

input.title: Adding ACP agent to agents.list silently hijacks all routing from implicit main agent
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #44375: Adding ACP agent to agents.list silently hijacks all routing from implicit main agent
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 44375
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/44375
- Title: Adding ACP agent to agents.list silently hijacks all routing from implicit main agent
- State: OPEN
- Author: barronlroth
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:security, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Adding a single ACP-bound agent to `agents.list` (e.g. for a persistent Codex binding on a Telegram topic) silently takes over as the default agent for **all** traffic — DMs, every group topic, heartbeats, crons — replacing the implicit `main` agent. No warning is shown. The user has no indication that their primary agent identity has changed.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Start with a working single-agent setup (no `agents.list` entries, implicit `main` agent with 596+ sessions of history).
2. Want to bind a persistent ACP Codex session to a single Telegram forum topic.
3. Run: `openclaw config set agents.list '[{"id":"codex-cubby","runtime":{"type":"acp","acp":{"agent":"codex","backend":"acpx","mode":"persistent","cwd":"/path/to/project"}}}]'`
4. Add a binding for that specific topic.
5. Restart gateway.

## Expected Behavior

- Only the bound Telegram topic routes to `codex-cubby`.
- All other traffic (DMs, other topics, heartbeats, crons) continues routing to `main`.
- If the implicit `main` agent would be displaced, show a warning.

## Actual Behavior

- **All** traffic routes to `codex-cubby` because it is the first (and only) entry in `agents.list`, making it the default per the routing fallback rule.
- The implicit `main` agent is completely abandoned — 596 sessions, QMD index, auth profiles, all orphaned.
- New sessions are created under `~/.openclaw/agents/codex-cubby/` with fresh history.
- Session keys change from `agent:main:...` to `agent:codex-cubby:...`, breaking any hardcoded references (e.g. in HEARTBEAT.md `sessions_history` calls).
- No warning or confirmation is shown during the config change.

## Impact

- Complete loss of conversational continuity (weeks/months of session history orphaned).
- QMD memory search index orphaned (built under `main`, now unused).
- All cron jobs, heartbeats, and topic sessions silently re-keyed.
- User discovers the problem days later when debugging unrelated issues.

## Suggested Fixes

1. **Warn when implicit `main` would be displaced.** If `agents.list` is being set for the first time and does not include an entry with `id: "main"` or `default: true`, warn the user that the implicit main agent will no longer receive traffic.
2. **Auto-include implicit main.** When `agents.list` is populated, auto-add `{"id": "main", "default": true}` if no default is specified and `main` agent state exists on disk.
3. **Require explicit `default: true`.** Instead of falling back to "first list entry," require at least one entry to have `default: true` when multiple agents exist (or when any agent is added).

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.3.8 → 2026.3.11
- macOS (Apple Silicon)
- Single Telegram bot account
- Config change made via `openclaw config set` from an agent topic session

## Workaround

Delete the ACP agent from `agents.list`, merge its sessions back into `main`, and restart. Requires manual session file migration and sessions.json re-keying.
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- newcodebook at 2026-03-12T21:14:39Z:
This is expected with the current routing fallback, but I agree it’s a foot-gun:

- Once `agents.list` exists, the default agent becomes **`agents.list[].default`**, else **the first entry in `agents.list`**, else fallback to implicit `main`.
- With a single entry (`codex-cubby`) and no explicit default, that ACP agent becomes the default for everything that doesn’t match a binding.

### Minimal, safe workaround (no session migration needed)
1) **Make `main` explicit and default**, and keep the ACP agent as a second entry.

Example shape:

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "main", "default": true },
      {
        "id": "codex-cubby",
        "runtime": {
          "type": "acp",
          "acp": { "agent": "codex", "backend": "acpx", "mode": "persistent", "cwd": "/path/to/project" }
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

2) Keep your **topic-specific binding** so *only* that peer routes to `codex-cubby`.
3) Restart the gateway. Your `~/.openclaw/agents/main/...` sessions/index should still be on disk; they were displaced by routing, not deleted.

When `agents.list` is written for the first time:
- Warn if there’s no `default: true`, *and* an implicit `main` store exists on disk (very likely continuity expectation).
- Consider requiring an explicit default when `agents.list` is non-empty.

- `openclaw config get agents.list`

input.title: Preserve structured ACP backend error kinds instead of mapping all errors to `end_turn`
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #44294: Preserve structured ACP backend error kinds instead of mapping all errors to `end_turn`
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 44294
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/44294
- Title: Preserve structured ACP backend error kinds instead of mapping all errors to `end_turn`
- State: OPEN
- Author: aniruddhaadak80
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

In `src/acp/translator.ts`, ACP events with `state === "error"` are currently finished as `end_turn` because the protocol lacks a structured error kind.

That means timeouts, rate limits, backend crashes, and deliberate refusals are all effectively collapsed into the same client-visible stop reason.

## Why It Matters

- Clients cannot distinguish transient backend failures from normal completion.
- Retry logic and UX become less accurate.
- Refusal semantics and operational failures are conflated.

## Suggested Direction

Introduce or consume a structured error classification such as:

- `refusal`
- `timeout`
- `rate_limit`
- `server_error`

Then map those cases intentionally instead of defaulting all ACP errors to `end_turn`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- ACP translator can preserve backend error class information.
- Clients can distinguish transient failures from normal end-of-turn behavior.
- The mapping is covered by targeted tests.

```
