GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44523 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44523 - Title: fix(session): preserve model override across daily freshness resets - State: OPEN - Author: hchen13 - Labels: size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem When a user sets a per-session model via the `/model` command, the override is lost every day after the 4 AM freshness reset. The user has to re-run `/model` every morning to restore their preference. ## Root Cause In `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`, the `else` branch that creates a new session only preserves `modelOverride` and `providerOverride` inside the `if (resetTriggered && entry)` guard: ```typescript } else { sessionId = crypto.randomUUID(); isNewSession = true; // ... if (resetTriggered && entry) { // ← daily resets have resetTriggered=false // ... persistedModelOverride = entry.modelOverride; persistedProviderOverride = entry.providerOverride; // ... } } ``` Daily freshness resets (4 AM boundary) go through this `else` branch with `resetTriggered = false`, so `modelOverride` and `providerOverride` are silently dropped. ## Fix Move `modelOverride`/`providerOverride` preservation outside the `resetTriggered` guard, to a broader `if (entry)` check. Behavior overrides (verbose, thinking, reasoning, ttsAuto, label) remain gated behind `resetTriggered` — they are intentionally only carried over for explicit `/reset` or `/new` commands. ```typescript } else { sessionId = crypto.randomUUID(); isNewSession = true; // Always preserve model/provider overrides across session resets // (including daily freshness resets, not just explicit /reset or /new) if (entry) { persistedModelOverride = entry.modelOverride; persistedProviderOverride = entry.providerOverride; } if (resetTriggered && entry) { persistedThinking = entry.thinkingLevel; persistedVerbose = entry.verboseLevel; persistedReasoning = entry.reasoningLevel; persistedTtsAuto = entry.ttsAuto; persistedLabel = entry.label; } } ``` ## Impact - **Daily/idle freshness reset path**: `modelOverride` and `providerOverride` now survive — this is the fix. - **First-ever session creation**: `entry` is `undefined`, so the new `if (entry)` block is a no-op — no change. - **Explicit `/reset` or `/new`**: Both guards fire, behavior unchanged. ## Test Added a test case `"preserves modelOverride and providerOverride across daily freshness resets"` to the existing `initSessionState preserves behavior overrides` describe block, using `vi.useFakeTimers()` to simulate a 5 AM arrival with the session last updated at 3 AM (stale). All 51 tests pass. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T01:48:25Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where a user's per-session model override (set via `/model`) was silently dropped every time the daily 4 AM freshness boundary triggered a new session. The root cause was that `persistedModelOverride` and `persistedProviderOverride` were only preserved inside the `resetTriggered` guard, which is `false` for freshness-based resets. The fix is a minimal, well-targeted two-line move: both fields are now preserved under a broader `if (entry)` check so they survive any type of session rollover, while the intentionally ephemeral behavior overrides (verbose, thinking, reasoning, ttsAuto, label) remain gated behind `resetTriggered`. A regression test using `vi.useFakeTimers()` is added to cover the exact failing scenario. Key changes: - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`: `modelOverride`/`providerOverride` preservation extracted from the `resetTriggered && entry` guard into a standalone `if (entry)` block, placed just before it. - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts`: New test case simulates a 5 AM arrival with a session last active at 3 AM, confirming model/provider overrides survive the freshness reset while behavior overrides do not.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — the change is a minimal, surgical fix to a clearly documented regression with a matching regression test. - The diff is only ~10 lines of production code change. The logic is straightforward: two lines are moved out of one `if` guard into a broader `if (entry)` check directly above it. All three code paths (first-ever session, explicit reset, daily freshness reset) are correctly handled both before and after the change. A dedicated regression test is added that precisely exercises the previously broken path and verifies that model/provider overrides survive while behavior overrides correctly do not. - No files require special attention. GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44015 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44015 - Title: gateway: enforce 512 KB max length for chat.send message - State: OPEN - Author: maweibin - Labels: docs, app: web-ui, gateway, size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ### Summary Add a **512 KB** (UTF-8 byte length) maximum for the `chat.send` message body in the gateway. This makes chat behavior predictable for clients, prevents extremely large messages from putting unnecessary memory and CPU pressure on the gateway, and aligns chat payload limits with other content-safety and size-guard rails in the system. ### Changes - **Implementation** (`src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`) - Define and export `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES = 512 * 1024` (512 KB). - In `sanitizeChatSendMessageInput`, enforce a UTF-8 byte length check via `Buffer.byteLength(normalized, "utf8")` after NFC normalization and null-byte checks. - If the message exceeds 512 KB, return: - `{ ok: false, error: "message exceeds maximum length (max 512 KB). Shorten or split your message." }` - Otherwise, keep the existing control-character stripping and return `{ ok: true, message }`. - **Protocol schema** (`src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts`) - Add `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH = 512 * 1024`. - Change `ChatSendParamsSchema.message` from `Type.String()` to: - `Type.String({ maxLength: CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH })`. - **Tests** (`src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`) - Import `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES` alongside `sanitizeChatSendMessageInput`. - Add tests that: - Accept a message at exactly 512 KB (ASCII). - Reject a message over 512 KB and assert the error includes `"512"`, `"maximum length"`, and `"Shorten or split"`. - Verify enforcement based on UTF-8 byte length using multi-byte characters. - **Docs** (`docs/gateway/protocol.md`) - Add a **Chat methods** subsection stating: - `chat.send` message body is limited to **512 KB** (UTF-8 byte length). - Oversized messages are rejected and clients should shorten or split them. ### Rationale - Prevents unbounded `chat.send` payloads from causing excessive memory usage or slow processing on the gateway. - Gives clients a clear and documented upper bound so they can split or truncate messages proactively. - Keeps chat behavior consistent with other gateway size and safety limits, reducing surprise for operators and users. ### Testing - `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts --run` - Lint/format checks via `scripts/committer` (includes `pnpm check`). ### Checklist - [x] Chat message size limit enforced at both schema and server sanitization layers. - [x] Limit is based on UTF-8 byte length (multi-byte characters handled correctly). - [x] Documentation updated to reflect the new limit and its rationale. - [x] No version bump or release flow changes; commit only (no push). ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T12:12:54Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a 512 KB (UTF-8 byte length) cap on `chat.send` message bodies, enforced in `sanitizeChatSendMessageInput` via `Buffer.byteLength`, along with a matching `maxLength` constraint on the TypeBox schema and updated documentation. **Key findings:** - The server-side byte check (`Buffer.byteLength(normalized, "utf8") > CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES`) is correct and well-tested. Boundary, over-limit (ASCII), and multi-byte (CJK) cases are all covered. - There is a unit mismatch between the schema guard and the sanitizer: `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH = 512 * 1024` is used as a JSON Schema `maxLength`, which counts **characters**, not bytes. For multi-byte BMP scripts (e.g., CJK at 3 bytes/char), a string of exactly 524,288 characters represents ~1.5 MB of UTF-8 — roughly 3× the intended limit — and will pass schema validation before being caught by the sanitizer. This means the schema layer offers little protection against multi-byte heavy payloads, and all actual enforcement relies on the downstream sanitizer. - The comment `"aligns with CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES for ASCII"` is technically accurate but may mislead future maintainers into treating the two constants as equivalent; a clarifying comment on the character vs. byte distinction would help. - Documentation is clear and well-placed.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the actual 512 KB enforcement is correct and the schema mismatch is a minor gap in defense-in-depth, not a security hole. - The byte-based sanitizer check is correct, well-placed, and thoroughly tested. The only issue is that the schema-level `maxLength` guard uses character count rather than byte count, so multi-byte payloads (CJK, Arabic, etc.) up to ~1.5 MB can pass schema validation and be fully received before the sanitizer rejects them. This is a defense-in-depth gap rather than a bypass — no message over 512 KB will ultimately be accepted — but it slightly weakens the schema as a first-line size guard for non-ASCII content. - src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts — the CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH constant and its comment should more clearly document the character-vs-bytes distinction.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/gateway/protocol/schema/logs-chat.ts Line: 35-41 Comment: **Schema `maxLength` is in characters, not bytes — inconsistent with the byte-level limit** `maxLength` in JSON Schema (and TypeBox) counts Unicode characters (BMP code units), while `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES` in the sanitizer counts UTF-8 bytes. For multi-byte BMP scripts (e.g., CJK at 3 bytes/char), a string of exactly `512 * 1024` characters is ~1.5 MB of UTF-8, which is 3× the intended limit. The comment acknowledges "aligns with CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_BYTES for ASCII", but in practice the schema guard is too permissive for non-ASCII content: such messages pass schema validation, are fully loaded into memory, and are only rejected later by the sanitizer. This weakens the first-line DoS protection that the schema layer is presumably meant to provide. Consider making `CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH` significantly smaller than `512 * 1024` characters — or, if the schema check is only for client-facing documentation purposes, add a clear comment making that intent explicit so future maintainers don't rely on it as a server-side size guard. ```suggestion /** Max character length for chat.send message. * Note: this is a character-count limit (schema layer); actual byte enforcement * (512 KB UTF-8) happens in sanitizeChatSendMessageInput. * For pure ASCII, 1 char == 1 byte, so the values coincide. * For multi-byte scripts (CJK, etc.) a string at this limit can be ~3× larger in bytes. */ const CHAT_SEND_MESSAGE_MAX_LENGTH = 512 * 1024; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

input.title: fix(sessions): reduce session-store memory retention
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #84693: fix(sessions): reduce session-store memory retention
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 84693
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84693
- Title: fix(sessions): reduce session-store memory retention
- State: OPEN
- Author: efpiva
- Labels: docs, agents, size: L, plugin: file-transfer, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

This PR addresses the dominant session-store memory-retention issue observed in a high-load CodeClaw/OpenClaw triage deployment. It keeps `loadSessionStore()` compatibility intact, adds safer immutable read APIs, bounds serialized cache state, and migrates several read-only paths away from mutable full-store clones.

Changes:

- Intern repeated large `SessionEntry.skillsSnapshot.prompt` strings after session-store parse/clone.
- Add immutable session-store read APIs:
  - `readSessionStoreSnapshot(storePath)`
  - `readSessionEntry(storePath, sessionKey)`
  - `readSessionEntries(storePath)`
- Export the snapshot read APIs through plugin SDK/runtime session helpers.
- Bound `SESSION_STORE_SERIALIZED_CACHE` by store-path count and total bytes.
- Migrate safe read-only paths to snapshot/entry reads:
  - cron isolated delivery target lookup
  - delivery-info lookup
  - `readSessionUpdatedAt()`
  - auto-reply verbose progress refresh
  - cron timer delivery context lookup
  - subagent announce output token/stat lookup
- Isolate small agent-attempt lifecycle callbacks to reduce unnecessary active-run closure capture.

## Problem / RCA

I have been dealing with an unstable OpenClaw instance under a high load of sessions, tasks, and `sessions_spawn` activity. After several memory dump analyses, I traced the dominant issue to session-store clone amplification: repeated full-store reads were duplicating large `skillsSnapshot.prompt` strings across hundreds of retained session-store clones.

The important workload characteristic is not raw request volume; it is session-store shape plus repeated access. CodeClaw had accumulated hundreds of session entries, especially `agent:main` subagent/topic sessions, each carrying a large `skillsSnapshot.prompt`. Before this fix, every compatible `loadSessionStore()` clone parsed/cloned the full store and created fresh copies of those large prompt strings. Active runs and gateway/session paths then retained enough clones for V8 old-space to fill.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: reduce retained session-store clone/prompt memory in a real high-session OpenClaw deployment while preserving `loadSessionStore()` compatibility and adding immutable read APIs for safer read paths.
- Real environment tested: live `codeclaw-triage` container running OpenClaw/CodeClaw with triage cron enabled, real persistent session stores, and normal cron/subagent review activity.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: captured and analyzed pre-fix OOM/near-OOM heap snapshots, deployed the session-store fix stack into `codeclaw-triage`, let triage cron run for about 20 minutes with the real session registry loaded, then captured a fresh SIGUSR2 heap snapshot and analyzed exact prompt strings/session-store map retainers.
- Evidence after fix: copied live heap-analysis output from the CodeClaw triage container is below; artifacts are saved under `/tmp/openclaw-session-store-evidence/sigusr2-capture-20260520T171954Z`.

  ```text
  Pre-fix captured heaps reached 4.1-4.5 GB.
  First analyzed OOM heap:
    heap nodes: ~50.6M
    exact skills prompt string nodes: ~389,465
    exact skills prompt bytes: ~1.5 GiB
    full-store clone-like maps: ~925

  Post-fix live codeclaw-triage SIGUSR2 heap after ~20 min cron/subagent load:
    heap snapshot size: 387,801,524 bytes (~370 MiB)
    heap nodes: 3.63M
    exact skills prompt string nodes: 8
    exact skills prompt bytes: 55,312 bytes
    full-store clone-like maps: 25 fingerprint / 14 strong candidates
    container restart=0
    OOMKilled=false
    serialized session-cache strings: ~11.35 MiB under 64 MiB cap
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the container stayed healthy during validation (`restart=0`, `OOMKilled=false`), and the catastrophic `skillsSnapshot.prompt` clone multiplier was gone; serialized session-cache strings were ~11.35 MiB under the new 64 MiB default cap.
- What was not tested: this does not claim all OpenClaw memory pressure is gone; remaining pressure includes active-run state, task registry retention, serialized session-state strings, and normal Node/runtime caches. Further locking down/deprecating mutable full-store reads is left for follow-up API discussion.

## Compatibility / open question

This PR intentionally preserves `loadSessionStore()` as a mutable-clone compatibility API. The new snapshot APIs provide a safer read path, and this PR migrates several known hot read-only consumers, but existing callers and plugins can still call `loadSessionStore()`.

Open question for reviewers: should we keep this non-breaking compatibility path and migrate call sites gradually, or start a deprecation/guardrail plan for mutable full-store reads? The stricter long-term model would reserve mutable access for explicit writer/maintenance paths and require snapshot/entry APIs for reads.

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where expired `.deleted.*` and `.reset.*` session archive artifacts could accumulate indefinitely in a session store running in `enforce` maintenance mode. The root cause was that archive cleanup only ran when the same save operation had also archived at least one live session — if no live sessions were pruned, stale archives were never touched. Additionally, older filename variants using `+HH-MM` timezone offsets or `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` compact format were not recognized by the timestamp parser, making them ineligible for retention-based cleanup even when they should have been removed. Key changes: - **`store.ts`**: The archive cleanup gate is changed from `archivedDirs.size > 0 || resetArchiveRetentionMs != null` to `pruneAfterMs >= 0 || resetArchiveRetentionMs != null`. Since `pruneAfterMs` is always positive in practice, cleanup now runs unconditionally during every enforced-maintenance save. The `targetDirs` list always includes the store directory alongside any additional archived directories. - **`artifacts.ts`**: `ISO_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` is expanded to accept `±HH-MM`-style timezone suffixes, and a new `COMPACT_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` (`/^\d{8}-\d{6}$/`) handles `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` legacy filenames. `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` now normalizes both formats to valid ISO 8601 strings before `Date.parse`. Note: the original split-on-`T` fallback branch at the bottom of `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` is now unreachable by any timestamp accepted by `isArchiveTimestamp` and can be treated as dead code. - **Tests**: A new integration test covers the exact bug scenario (cleanup fires even when no live sessions were pruned), and unit tests verify round-trip parsing for both new legacy formats.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the fix correctly addresses the cleanup gap with no risk of over-deletion for recent archives. - The logic change is sound and well-tested. The new gate condition (`pruneAfterMs >= 0`) is effectively always true, which is intentional — the `cleanupArchivedSessionTranscripts` function's own guard (`olderThanMs < 0` early return) ensures no unsafe deletion occurs. The timestamp parsing additions are carefully scoped and validated by new unit tests. The main minor concern is that the always-true condition is slightly misleading, and the original fallback branch in `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` is now dead code — neither is a bug. - `src/config/sessions/store.ts` lines 482–483 (always-true gate condition worth clarifying) and `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts` lines 63–67 (now-unreachable fallback branch).

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts`, line 63-67 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/9d14a02307762e4420cde3f99df7ac0c863c1f22/src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts#L63-L67)) **Fallback branch is now dead code** After adding the two new branches (`compact` and `iso`), the original fallback path (split on `"T"` and replace `-` with `:`) can no longer be reached by any timestamp that passes `isArchiveTimestamp()`. The `ISO_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` requires the zone suffix `(Z|[+-]\d{2}-\d{2})`, and both new branches exhaustively handle every format that regex accepts. A raw value like `2026-02-23T12-34-56` (no zone) wouldn't pass `isArchiveTimestamp`, so it would never arrive here. The code is harmless, but it gives a false impression that there's a third format to handle. If this is intentional as a final safety net, a brief comment would help; otherwise it's a candidate for removal to reduce surface area.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 49112 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/49112 - Title: add doctor coverage for stale gateway locks - State: OPEN - Author: biefan - Labels: commands, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown AI-assisted: Codex Fixes #49036. ## Summary This change teaches `openclaw doctor` to inspect the gateway lock file, report stale lock ownership, and repair stale gateway locks with `--fix`. ## Root Cause The CLI already had doctor coverage for stale session locks, but the gateway lock had no matching doctor entry point even though the lock subsystem already encoded enough state to detect dead owners and stale files. When the gateway lock became stale, operators had no built-in visibility or guided repair path. ## Fix The gateway lock module now exposes a small inspection helper that reports pid liveness, age, stale reasons, and optional stale-file removal. `doctor` calls a new `noteGatewayLockHealth` helper so stale gateway locks appear in normal doctor output and are automatically removed when the operator runs `openclaw doctor --fix`. ## Validation - `pnpm exec vitest run "src/commands/doctor-gateway-locks.test.ts" "src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts" "src/infra/gateway-lock.test.ts"` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T16:54:36Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR extends `openclaw doctor` to detect and repair stale gateway lock files, mirroring the existing session-lock health checks. The implementation is well-structured and follows established patterns in the codebase. **Key issues found:** - **False-negative stale detection on Linux** (`src/infra/gateway-lock.ts`, line 208): `inspectGatewayLock` uses only `isPidAlive` for PID liveness, while `acquireGatewayLock` uses the more thorough `resolveGatewayOwnerStatus` which additionally validates PID start times and cmdline on Linux to detect recycled PIDs. A dead gateway whose PID was reused by another process will be reported as alive by the new inspector, meaning the doctor will not flag (or offer to repair) the stale lock — the exact scenario this feature is designed to address. - **Incorrect "dead" display for `pidAlive: null`** (`src/commands/doctor-gateway-locks.ts`, line 27): `formatGatewayLockLine` uses `lock.pidAlive ? "alive" : "dead"`, but `pidAlive` is typed as `boolean | null`. A `null` value (unknown status) would silently display as "dead", potentially misleading operators into thinking a lock owner is confirmed dead when the status is actually undetermined. - **Missing test for stale-without-repair path** (`src/commands/doctor-gateway-locks.test.ts`): The test suite covers the healthy-lock and stale-plus-repair cases, but not the stale-plus-no-repair path that generates the `"Gateway lock is stale."` warning and the `"Run openclaw doctor --fix"` hint — the primary user-visible diagnostic output of this feature.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Mostly safe to merge but the PID liveness divergence from `acquireGatewayLock` on Linux is a real functional gap that reduces the doctor's effectiveness in the scenario it was designed to fix. - The feature is additive (read-only unless `--fix` is passed) and the `--fix` path only deletes a file with `{ force: true }`. However, the PID liveness check in `inspectGatewayLock` is weaker than the check used by `acquireGatewayLock`, which means recycled PIDs on Linux will produce false-negative stale reports. There is also a subtle formatter bug where `null` pidAlive is displayed as "dead", and test coverage for the primary diagnostic path (stale + no repair) is missing. - `src/infra/gateway-lock.ts` (PID liveness check) and `src/commands/doctor-gateway-locks.ts` (formatter null handling) need attention before merge.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/commands/doctor-gateway-locks.ts Line: 27 Comment: **`null` pidAlive silently displays as "dead"** The `GatewayLockInspection` type declares `pidAlive: boolean | null`, so `lock.pidAlive` can legally be `null` even when `lock.pid` is not null (e.g. if a future code path constructs the inspection object that way, or if a test does). Because `null` is falsy, the conditional `lock.pidAlive ? "alive" : "dead"` will evaluate to `"dead"` for that case, which is misleading — operators would see a lock reported as "dead" and potentially take manual action on a lock whose owner status is actually unknown. ```suggestion lock.pid === null ? "pid=missing" : `pid=${lock.pid} (${lock.pidAlive === null ? "unknown" : lock.pidAlive ? "alive" : "dead"})`; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/infra/gateway-lock.ts Line: 208 Comment: **PID liveness check weaker than `acquireGatewayLock` on Linux** `inspectGatewayLock` uses `isPidAlive(pid)` (a simple `kill(pid, 0)` signal check), while the existing `acquireGatewayLock` path uses the much more thorough `resolveGatewayOwnerStatus`, which on Linux additionally: 1. Compares the lock payload's `startTime` against `/proc//stat` to detect PID reuse. 2. Falls back to reading `/proc//cmdline` and verifying it looks like a gateway process. If a gateway dies and its PID is reused by an unrelated process, `isPidAlive` returns `true`, so `inspectGatewayLock` reports `pidAlive=true` and `stale=no`. The lock would be left in place and operators would see no stale warning — exactly the scenario this doctor check is meant to catch. `acquireGatewayLock` would immediately detect the PID mismatch and reclaim the lock. Consider reusing `resolveGatewayOwnerStatus` here (exporting it or inlining the relevant subset) so the inspection results are consistent with what the acquisition path would decide. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/commands/doctor-gateway-locks.test.ts Line: 69-89 Comment: **Missing test for stale-but-no-repair path** The two existing tests cover (a) a healthy lock and (b) a stale lock with `shouldRepair: true`. There is no test for the case where the lock is stale but `shouldRepair: false`, which is the path that adds the `"Gateway lock is stale."` line and the `"Run \"openclaw doctor --fix\""` suggestion. Without this test, a regression in that branch (e.g., the hint disappearing, or the file being incorrectly removed) would go undetected. Consider adding: ```ts it("reports stale gateway lock with repair hint when shouldRepair is false", async () => { const { lockPath, configPath } = resolveLockPath(process.env); await fs.writeFile( lockPath, JSON.stringify({ pid: -1, createdAt: new Date(Date.now() - 120_000).toISOString(), configPath, }), "utf8", ); await noteGatewayLockHealth({ shouldRepair: false, staleMs: 30_000 }); expect(note).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1); const [message, title] = note.mock.calls[0] as [string, string]; expect(title).toBe("Gateway lock"); expect(message).toContain("Gateway lock is stale."); expect(message).toContain("openclaw doctor --fix"); // file should NOT be removed await expect(fs.access(lockPath)).resolves.toBeUndefined(); }); ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

per_label_rationale.rationale: The embedding fix is about graceful degradation, retry-once, fallback, and avoiding hard failure (plus resource-leak concerns) — operational failure-mode behavior.

excluded_label_rationale.rationale: Tests are added alongside the change but test infrastructure is not the subject.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 62733 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/62733 - Title: Fix local memory embedding VRAM fallback and logging file resolution - State: OPEN - Author: nSPIR3D - Labels: size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary This fixes two issues in local OpenClaw setups: 1. Logging could ignore a valid configured `logging.file` path because the logging runtime depended on a fragile built-module require path. 2. Local memory embeddings could fail hard on VRAM-fit initialization instead of retrying and degrading gracefully. ## Changes - switch logging config reads to the real source config loader - expand `YYYY-MM-DD` placeholders in configured log file paths before writing - add GPU-fit local embedding init with one retry on VRAM-style failure - fall back to CPU-only for that embedding operation if the second GPU-fit attempt also fails - add targeted tests for both behaviors ## Reproduction ### Logging Configure: `logging.file = /home/user/.openclaw/logs/openclaw-YYYY-MM-DD.log` Before: - runtime could miss the configured path and fall back elsewhere - placeholder expansion was not honored at write time After: - the configured path is resolved through the normal config loader - `YYYY-MM-DD` expands to the real local date before file writes ### Local embeddings Use local memory embeddings with `node-llama-cpp` on a machine where GPU-fit init can fail with: `A context size of 24 is too large for the available VRAM` Before: - the operation failed unless GPU was disabled globally After: - retry once with a fresh GPU-fit init - if it still fails, fall back to CPU-only for that operation - the next operation starts fresh on the normal GPU-fit path ## Validation Ran: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/logging/config.test.ts src/logging/logger-settings.test.ts src/memory-host-sdk/host/embeddings.test.ts` Result: - 3 test files passed - 28 tests passed ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-07T22:24:38Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes two independent local-setup issues: logging config is now read via the real `loadConfig()` import (dropping the fragile `resolveNodeRequireFromMeta` indirection) and `YYYY-MM-DD` placeholders are expanded at write time; local embedding initialization now retries once with a fresh GPU-fit calculation on VRAM errors and falls back to a temporary CPU-only context if the retry also fails. - The temporary CPU-mode `Llama` instance created in `createTemporaryCpuContext` is never disposed — `cpuContext` and `cpuModel` are cleaned up, but `cpuLlama` is dropped, leaking CPU thread-pool resources on every fallback operation.

Confidence Score: 4/5

Safe to merge after fixing the cpuLlama disposal leak in the CPU fallback path. The logging fixes are clean and well-tested. The embedding retry/fallback logic is correct in the sequential case and the tests are solid, but the missing cpuLlama.dispose() call is a genuine resource leak that will accumulate on any machine that repeatedly triggers the CPU fallback. The concurrent VRAM race is a real but lower-probability concern. Score of 4 reflects the single clear P1 that should be fixed before landing. src/memory-host-sdk/host/embeddings.ts — createTemporaryCpuContext dispose function is missing cpuLlama.dispose()

Vulnerabilities

 No security concerns identified. The logging path change reads config through the standard loader (wrapped in try/catch) and writes to user-configured file paths. The embedding fallback uses the same trusted `node-llama-cpp` APIs as before with no new network or IPC surfaces.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/memory-host-sdk/host/embeddings.ts Line: 197-217 Comment: **`cpuLlama` instance leaked after CPU fallback** `createTemporaryCpuContext` disposes `cpuContext` and `cpuModel`, but never calls `cpuLlama.dispose()`. Every time the CPU fallback path is exercised, a new CPU-mode `Llama` instance (thread pools, allocator state) is abandoned. The test at line 721 confirms `getLlama` is called twice with distinct options, so these are separate instances — not a shared singleton. ```suggestion const createTemporaryCpuContext = async (): Promise<{ context: LlamaEmbeddingContext; dispose: () => Promise; }> => { const cpuLlama = await getLlama({ logLevel: LlamaLogLevel.error, gpu: false }); const cpuModel = await cpuLlama.loadModel({ modelPath: await getResolvedModelPath(), gpuLayers: 0, }); const cpuContext = await cpuModel.createEmbeddingContext(); return { context: cpuContext, dispose: async () => { try { await cpuContext.dispose(); } catch {} try { await cpuModel.dispose(); } catch {} try { await cpuLlama.dispose(); } catch {} }, }; }; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/memory-host-sdk/host/embeddings.ts Line: 220-253 Comment: **Concurrent VRAM failures can race on shared mutable state** `withEmbeddingContext` modifies `initPromise` and calls `disposeGpuResources()` directly. If two concurrent calls both await the same failing `initPromise` (both get the VRAM error), both will then independently call `disposeGpuResources()` and reset `initPromise = null`, resulting in double disposal of `embeddingContext`/`embeddingModel` and a double retry. The existing concurrency tests cover successful and single-failure cases, but not concurrent VRAM failures. Consider tracking a dedicated "retry in flight" flag or serializing the retry through a new promise so subsequent callers wait for the retry rather than each starting their own. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````
Reviews (1): Last reviewed commit: ["Fix local logging config resolution and ..."](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/e6cbac8a320e437a78fb35405974bc0ba84b6f18) | [Re-trigger Greptile](https://app.greptile.com/api/retrigger?id=27651902) - clawsweeper at 2026-05-01T04:27:35Z: Codex review: needs real behavior proof before merge. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.

**Summary** The PR rewrites logging config/file-path resolution and adds local node-llama-cpp embedding retry/CPU fallback behavior with tests. Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main has no VRAM retry/CPU fallback in the active local provider, and the PR tests model that failure path. I did not establish a live GPU/node-llama-cpp reproduction in this read-only review. **PR rating** Overall: 🧂 unranked krab Proof: 🧂 unranked krab Patch quality: 🧂 unranked krab Summary: Useful intent is present, but missing real behavior proof, conflicts, and blocking correctness issues make this PR not quality-ready for merge. Rank-up moves: - Rebase onto current main and port the embedding change to packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.ts. - Preserve contextSize, abort/close cleanup, CPU fallback cleanup, and write-time dated log rollover. - Add redacted real behavior proof in the PR body for the logging and local node-llama-cpp paths.
What the crustacean ranks mean - 🦀 challenger crab: rare, exceptional readiness with strong proof, clean implementation, and convincing validation. - 🦞 diamond lobster: very strong readiness with only minor maintainer review expected. - 🐚 platinum hermit: good normal PR, likely mergeable with ordinary maintainer review. - 🦐 gold shrimp: useful signal, but proof or patch confidence is still limited. - 🦪 silver shellfish: thin signal; proof, validation, or implementation needs work. - 🧂 unranked krab: not merge-ready because proof is missing/unusable or there are serious correctness or safety concerns. - 🌊 off-meta tidepool: rating does not apply to this item. Shiny media proof means a screenshot, video, or linked artifact directly shows the changed behavior. Runtime, network, CSP, and security claims still need visible diagnostics.
**Real behavior proof** Needs real behavior proof before merge: The PR body lists targeted Vitest results only; it needs redacted after-fix terminal output, logs, screenshots, recordings, live output, or a linked artifact proving the real logging and local embedding behavior before merge. After adding proof, update the PR body; ClawSweeper should re-review automatically. If it does not, the PR author or someone with repository write access can comment `@clawsweeper re-review`. **Risk before merge** - The branch is merge-conflicting and edits the old src/memory-host-sdk embedding path while current main's active local provider lives under packages/memory-host-sdk. - Porting the embedding change as-is could bypass current contextSize defaults/configuration, abort signals, and close/disposal lifecycle behavior for local embedding users. - The PR resolves YYYY-MM-DD in logger settings rather than at write time, which can stop long-running processes from rolling to the next day's log file. - The CPU fallback path can still leak native resources if CPU model/context setup fails before the returned disposer exists. - The PR body provides targeted unit test output only; it does not prove a real configured logging write or real node-llama-cpp VRAM retry/CPU fallback. **Maintainer options:** 1. **Rebase and port the active provider (recommended)** Move the VRAM retry/CPU fallback logic into packages/memory-host-sdk and preserve current contextSize, AbortSignal, close, and disposal semantics before merge. 2. **Req [truncated] ``` Changed files (6): ```text src/logging/config.test.ts src/logging/config.ts src/logging/logger-settings.test.ts src/logging/logger.ts src/memory-host-sdk/host/embeddings.test.ts src/memory-host-sdk/host/embeddings.ts ```

input.title: Feature: Support output dimensionality truncation for local GGUF embedding models
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #58765: Feature: Support output dimensionality truncation for local GGUF embedding models
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 58765
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58765
- Title: Feature: Support output dimensionality truncation for local GGUF embedding models
- State: OPEN
- Author: losz5000
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

The `outputDimensionality` config field currently only works for Gemini embedding-2 models. Local GGUF embedding models (e.g., Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B) always output their full dimension (1024), with no way to truncate.

## Use Case

Many modern embedding models support Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), where the first N dimensions contain most of the semantic information. For example, Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B outputs 1024 dimensions but the first 256-512 dimensions are sufficient for most retrieval tasks.

Benefits of truncation:
- **50-75% reduction** in vector storage for memory search
- **Faster similarity search** as the index grows
- **Minimal accuracy loss** (<2% for MRL-trained models)

## Proposed Solution

1. Make `outputDimensionality` work for all embedding providers (not just Gemini)
2. After computing the embedding vector, simply `slice(0, N)` before storing
3. When changing the dimension, trigger a full reindex (same as Gemini behavior)

Alternative: Add a separate `truncateDimensions` field under `memorySearch.local` if keeping `outputDimensionality` Gemini-specific is intentional.

## Config Example

```json
{
  "memorySearch": {
    "provider": "local",
    "local": {
      "modelPath": "Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf"
    },
    "outputDimensionality": 512
  }
}
```

## Bonus: Query Instruction Support

Qwen3-Embedding also supports "Instruction Aware" embeddings (prepend a task description to queries for 1-5% improvement). Adding a `queryInstruction` field would be valuable:

```json
{
  "memorySearch": {
    "queryInstruction": "Search for relevant information in intelligence notes and reports"
  }
}
```

## Environment
- OpenClaw v2026.3.24
- macOS ARM64
- Local GGUF embedding: Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-Q8_0
```

- Maintainers can comment `@clawsweeper explain` to ask for more context, or `@clawsweeper stop` to stop active automation.

</details>

**Summary**
Keep open: current main and the latest v2026.5.18 release still accept and forward `memorySearch.outputDimensionality`, but the built-in local GGUF provider ignores it and returns full normalized vectors. The closed OpenAI-compatible dimensions issue does not cover this local provider path.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence path: create the local provider with `outputDimensionality` and a mocked `node-llama-cpp` vector, then both query and batch paths return the full normalized vector because the option is never read. I did not run the repro because this review was read-only.

**Next step**
This is a narrow, source-proven local GGUF memory-search feature gap with clear implementation files, regression tests, and no open local truncation PR found.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Implement local GGUF truncation through `memorySearch.outputDimensionality`, include the dimension in local provider cache/reindex identity, add focused tests/docs, and leave query-instruction support for a separate product/config discussion.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, source inspection gives a high-confidence path: create the local provider with `outputDimensionality` and a mocked `node-llama-cpp` vector, then both query and batch paths return the full normalized vector because the option is never read. I did not run the repro because this review was read-only.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes for the main requested fix: reusing the existing `outputDimensionality` contract is narrower than adding a duplicate local-only config key. The bonus query-instruction idea is separate and would need its own config/product decision.

Label justifications:

- `P2`: This is a normal priority memory-search enhancement with clear user value and limited blast radius, not an urgent regression.
- `impact:session-state`: The request affects memory-search vector storage and recall behavior for agent memory indexes.

Acceptance criteria:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/provider-adapters.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts docs/reference/memory-config.md src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/schema.help.ts CHANGELOG.md

input.title: memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs default (120 s) is too low for local GGUF; error message doesn't surface the fix
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #74204: memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs default (120 s) is too low for local GGUF; error message doesn't surface the fix
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 74204
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/74204
- Title: memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs default (120 s) is too low for local GGUF; error message doesn't surface the fix
- State: OPEN
- Author: Skeptomenos
- Labels: (none)

Body:
```markdown
## Environment

- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.4.25
- **QMD version:** 2.1.0 (bab86d5)
- **Platform:** Ubuntu 24.04, GCP e2-standard-4 (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM)
- **Workspace size:** 37+ Markdown files in memory root + `memory/` tree
- **GGUF model:** default (`embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf`, ~0.6 GB, auto-downloaded)
- **searchMode:** `query` (hybrid BM25 + vector)

## Observed behavior

After enabling hybrid search (`searchMode: "query"`), the gateway emits this warning repeatedly every 2–4 minutes:

```
{"subsystem":"memory","message":"qmd embed failed (boot): Error: qmd embed timed out after 120000ms; backing off for 60s"}
{"subsystem":"memory","message":"qmd embed failed (interval): Error: qmd embed timed out after 120000ms; backing off for 120s"}
```

The embed process runs at 100–186% CPU on 4 vCPU for ~2 minutes before being killed. Peak RAM during GGUF model load: **9.6 GB**. The embed never completes — every attempt times out at exactly 120 s. Vector search is effectively disabled.

## Root cause

The GGUF embedding model takes **3–4 minutes** on a 4-core CPU to embed a 37-file workspace. The default `memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs` is **120 s** — less than the actual embed duration.

The fix (`memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs: 600000`) is only discoverable via `openclaw config schema` or the memory config reference page. The error message does not mention it.

## Inconsistency with built-in engine

`agents.defaults.memorySearch.sync.embeddingBatchTimeoutSeconds` correctly differentiates:
- **600 s** for local/self-hosted providers (`local`, `ollama`, `lmstudio`)
- **120 s** for hosted providers (OpenAI, Gemini, etc.)

`memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs` applies the same 120 s default regardless of whether the embedding model is a local GGUF or a hosted API. For local GGUF workloads, 120 s is consistently insufficient on commodity hardware.

## Suggested fixes (pick one or combine)

1. **Increase the default** for `embedTimeoutMs` to 600 s (matching the built-in engine's local-provider default), or detect when `searchMode` is `vsearch`/`query` with the default GGUF model and apply a longer default automatically.

2. **Improve the error message** to mention the fix:
   ```
   qmd embed timed out after 120000ms — consider increasing memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs (current default: 120000)
   ```

3. **Add to the QMD troubleshooting docs** a dedicated entry:
   > **`qmd embed timed out after 120000ms`?** Increase `memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs`. Default is 120 000 ms. On commodity hardware with the default GGUF model and a 30–50 file workspace, embedding takes 3–5 minutes. Set to `600000` or higher.

## Workaround

```json5
{
  memory: {
    qmd: {
      update: {
        embedTimeoutMs: 600000,  // 10 minutes — sufficient for ~40 files on 4-core CPU
      },
    },
  },
}
```

Or use a smaller/faster GGUF model via `QMD_EMBED_MODEL`:
```bash
export QMD_EMBED_MODEL="hf:Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf"
```

## Additional context

The gateway remains healthy throughout (Slack connected, Atlassian/GWS MCP tools working). The embed failures are WARN-level and non-blocking. However, with the default 120 s timeout, vector search is effectively permanently disabled on any commodity host with a medium or larger workspace, with no clear path to resolution from the error message alone.
```

input.title: fix: local GGUF embedding model warmup blocks Node.js event loop for minutes on startup (ARM64/Pi)
input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #75657: fix: local GGUF embedding model warmup blocks Node.js event loop for minutes on startup (ARM64/Pi)
input.github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 75657
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75657
- Title: fix: local GGUF embedding model warmup blocks Node.js event loop for minutes on startup (ARM64/Pi)
- State: OPEN
- Author: DerFlash
- Labels: (none)

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When `memorySearch.provider` is set to `"local"` (the default), the Gateway initializes a GGUF embedding model via node-llama-cpp on the main Node.js thread during startup. On ARM64 hardware (Raspberry Pi), loading a 314 MB GGUF model blocks the event loop for **approximately 6 minutes**, during which the Gateway is completely unreachable — even though it reports `active (running)` in systemd.

## Environment

- OS: Raspberry Pi, Linux 6.12.75+rpt-rpi-v8, arm64/aarch64
- Node.js: v24.14.1
- OpenClaw: 2026.4.29 (a448042)
- memorySearch config: `provider: "local"`, model: `hf:ggml-org/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-q8_0-GGUF/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf` (314 MB)
- Gateway mode: systemd user service

## Observed behavior

Gateway systemd service starts, but the WebSocket port is not usable for ~6 minutes:

```
14:58:07  systemd: Started openclaw-gateway.service
14:58:22  [gateway] loading configuration…        ← 15s just to load config
14:58:25  [gateway] starting...
             ... silence for ~6 minutes ...
15:04:04  [gateway] startup model warmup timed out after 5000ms; continuing without waiting
15:04:04  [diagnostic] liveness warning:
            reasons=event_loop_delay,event_loop_utilization,cpu
            eventLoopDelayP99Ms=18555.6
            eventLoopDelayMaxMs=18555.6            ← 18.5 second event loop block
            eventLoopUtilization=1
            cpuCoreRatio=1.05
15:04:04  [telegram] [default] starting provider
15:04:04  [gateway] ready
```

**Time from service start to `gateway ready`: ~6 minutes.**

During this window:
- `openclaw status` reports gateway as "unreachable (timeout)"
- `openclaw tui` fails with "gateway not reachable"
- `openclaw logs --follow` fails immediately
- Telegram receives no responses
- All WebSocket connections time out

The `startup model warmup timed out after 5000ms` message confirms the Gateway itself has a warmup timeout, but the model initialization continues blocking the main thread regardless.

## Additional context

- CPU usage holds at ~40–60% throughout the warmup window (confirmed via `ps`)
- RSS reaches ~700 MB on a Pi with 4 GB RAM during warmup
- The `lsof` on the gateway PID shows fd/1 and fd/2 both point to a socket (systemd journal), not a file — ruling out log-file I/O as the cause
- Manually testing `import('...node_modules/json5/...')` from the dist directory succeeds instantly, confirming earlier `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors during restarts were a race condition symptom of the warmup blocking, not a missing package

## Workaround

Switch `memorySearch.provider` to `"openai"` with `remote.apiKey`. This eliminates the local model load entirely. Not viable for offline/airgap setups.

## Suggested fix direction

1. Run GGUF model initialization in a Worker thread (Node.js `worker_threads`) so the main event loop stays free
2. Or: defer model warmup to first actual embedding request (lazy init), with a non-blocking queue for incoming requests during warmup
3. The existing `startup model warmup timed out after 5000ms` mechanism already acknowledges the issue — the timeout just doesn't prevent the blocking

## Impact

Any user running `memorySearch.provider: "local"` on ARM64/Pi (or slow x86) will experience this. Every Gateway restart (e.g. after `openclaw update`) causes a 6-minute outage window.
```

- clawsweeper at 2026-05-01T13:21:39Z:

**Summary**
Keep open. Current main avoids builtin/local startup work by default, but the local GGUF provider still resolves and loads `node-llama-cpp` in the Gateway process whenever local embeddings are initialized, so the core event-loop blocking risk remains. The related open PRs cover prewarm or diagnostic-budget changes, not the needed worker or child-process isolation.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible rather than live-reproduced: configure explicit local memory embeddings and trigger deep status, memory index/sync, or the first `memory_search`; current main still enters the in-process `node-llama-cpp` provider path. I did not live-measure the ARM64/Pi startup timing.

**Next step**
This is a concrete source-reproducible runtime bug with clear likely files and no required config or product-policy change; the repair should focus on local embedding isolation, not startup prewarm.


<details>
<summary>Review details</summary>

Best possible solution:

Run GGUF model resolution, model/context creation, and embedding calls behind a worker-thread or child-process boundary while preserving the existing local provider contract.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes, source-reproducible rather than live-reproduced: configure explicit local memory embeddings and trigger deep status, memory index/sync, or the first `memory_search`; current main still enters the in-process `node-llama-cpp` provider path. I did not live-measure the ARM64/Pi startup timing.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Yes, worker-thread or child-process isolation is the best direction. Startup deferral, prewarm reshuffling, or outer `Promise.race` timeouts do not keep native model loading off the Gateway event loop.

Acceptance criteria:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-timeout.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/search-manager.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-memory.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts
- pnpm build
- node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"

What I checked:

- **Current local provider loads GGUF in-process:** `createLocalEmbeddingProvider()` imports `node-llama-cpp` and `ensureContext()` awaits `getLlama`, `resolveModelFile`, `loadModel`, and `createEmbeddingContext` before embedding calls; there is no process or worker boundary here. ([`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.ts:23`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/532759e1ab2e/packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.ts#L23), [532759e1ab2e](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/532759e1ab2e))
- **Memory-core directly exposes the local provider:** The builtin `local` adapter directly awaits `createLocalEmbeddingProvider()` and advertises long inline local query/batch timeouts, so memory-core uses the same in-process provider contract. Body:
```markdown
# [Bug]: Tool result secret redaction mutates session history, breaking KV cache prefix matching for local LLM providers

## Summary

`sanitizeToolArgs()` and `sanitizeToolResult()` in `attempt.tool-run-context.ts` apply `redactStringsDeep()` → `redactToolPayloadText()` to all tool call inputs and outputs. Strings matching `DEFAULT_REDACT_PATTERNS` are truncated via `maskToken()` (e.g. `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` → `DISC…N`). The redacted content is stored in the session and sent to the model on subsequent turns.

For cloud API providers this is a reasonable security measure. For local inference providers (llama-server, exo, LM Studio, Ollama, vLLM, sglang) where tool results never leave the machine, the redaction provides no security benefit while causing a full KV cache re-prefill every time a tool result contains a pattern that matches the secret detector.

## Reproduction

1. Configure a local provider (`api: "openai-completions"`, `baseUrl: "http://localhost:..."`)
2. Have the agent read a file containing environment variable references (e.g. `${DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN}`, `${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}`)
3. On the next turn, observe the tool result content has been mutated in the session store
4. The KV cache diverges at the mutated token position; the engine re-processes everything from that point forward

## Evidence

Proxy diff between consecutive turns on same session showing tool result mutation:
```diff
- DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=\"${DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN:-}\"
+ DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=\"${DISC…N:-}\"
- TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=\"${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN:-}\"
+ TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=\"${TELE…N:-}\"
```

The mutation path: `executeToolCalls` → `buildToolRunContext` → `sanitizeToolArgs`/`sanitizeToolResult` → `redactStringsDeep` → `redactToolPayloadText` → `redactText(text, DEFAULT_REDACT_PATTERNS)` → `maskToken(token)` → `${token.slice(0, keep_start)}…${token.slice(-keep_end)}`

## Impact

On SWA models (Qwen, Kimi-K2) any mid-sequence token change forces re-processing of everything after the divergence point. A single redacted string in a tool result at message position N forces re-prefill of all tokens from position N to end of context. On a 50k+ token conversation this costs minutes of compute per turn.

Even on full-attention models, prefix caching (llama.cpp `--cache-reuse`, exo's built-in cache) is invalidated because the cached content no longer matches the modified session.

## Suggested fix

Gate redaction behind a config toggle:
```json
{
  "tools": {
    "redactSecrets": true
  }
}
```

Default `true` for cloud providers, `false` (or auto-detect) for local providers where `isStrictOpenAiCompatible === true` or the provider family is not in the cloud provider list.

Alternatively, apply redaction only to the transcript copy (via the existing transcript/runtime-context split) rather than to the session store copy that feeds the next prompt build.

## Workaround

No-op both functions in the dist bundle:
```js
function sanitizeToolArgs(args) { return args; }
function sanitizeToolResult(result) { return result; }
```

## Environment

- OpenClaw v2026.5.7 (eeef486)
- macOS, Apple Silicon
- Local inference via exo (MLX, Kimi-K2.5/K2.6)
- File: `attempt.tool-run-context-*.js`

## Related issues

- #19892 — 2026.2.15 breaks prompt cache for local model providers
- #20430 — Per-message metadata in system prompt invalidates llama.cpp KV cache
- #20894 — Inbound metadata injection into system prompt breaks Anthropic prompt caching
- #70829 — propagate timeoutMs to guarded dispatchers (local LLM 60s timeout)

Same mutation class as the removed `enforceToolResultContextBudgetInPlace` (in-flight compaction) and the removed `replaceMessages` (session store writeback) — prompt-build-time transforms that benefit stateless cloud APIs but destroy prefix-caching for stateful local engines.

```

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 43493 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/43493 - Title: feat: configure metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, etc.) for custom provider setup - State: OPEN - Author: IdrisHanafi - Labels: commands, size: L, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🦐 gold shrimp, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: ⏳ waiting on author, proof: 📸 screenshot Body: ```markdown ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets: - Problem: `openclaw configure --section model` sets up providers and auth but never prompts for model metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, reasoning, input types, cost). Users must hand-edit config or run many `config set` calls to configure these. - Why it matters: Custom/self-hosted endpoints default to contextWindow=16000 and maxTokens=4096, which are often wrong. Users have no interactive way to correct this during setup. - What changed: After custom provider setup in `promptAuthConfig`, the wizard now asks "Configure metadata for `/`?" and walks through the core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields (identity, capabilities, limits, cost) with current values prefilled. Built-in providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) are unaffected. Their catalog already provides defaults. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): The existing provider/auth flow is untouched. The metadata prompt is purely additive (appended after the custom provider path only). No config schema changes. No new CLI commands. Declining the prompt preserves the previous behavior exactly. Built-in provider flows are not modified. Compat flags (supportsStore, supportsTools, etc.) are not prompted for; they use sensible runtime defaults when absent and can be set manually in config by advanced users. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `openclaw configure --section model` with a **custom provider**: after provider setup completes, a new confirm prompt appears: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)". Answering Yes walks through all model metadata fields. Answering No preserves previous behavior. - **Built-in providers** (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.): no change. The metadata prompt does not appear. - Fields prompted (custom only): display name, API adapter, reasoning, input modalities, context window, max output tokens, token pricing (gated behind its own confirm). - All fields prefilled with current values; pressing Enter keeps defaults. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin 25.2.0) - Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm - Model/provider: Custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint ([thegrid.ai](https://thegrid.ai/)) - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): Standard `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with custom provider ### Steps 1. Run `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` 2. Select "Custom Provider" and complete auth/model setup 3. Observe the new prompt: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)" 4. Answer Yes and walk through the fields, or answer No to skip 5. (Optional) Repeat with a built-in provider (e.g. OpenAI) and confirm the metadata prompt does **not** appear ### Expected - After custom provider setup, a metadata configuration prompt appears - Answering Yes allows editing all core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields - Answering No skips metadata editing (same behavior as before this change) - Updated metadata is written to `models.providers..models[]` in config - Built-in providers skip the metadata prompt entirely ### Actual - Works as expected ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) 18 unit tests in `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` covering: `collectConfiguredModels`, `findModelInConfig`, `findPrimaryModelEntry`, `applyModelDefinitionUpdate`, `formatTokenCount`, `promptModelMetadata`, `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` (including skip for built-in providers). All passing. Existing `configure.wizard.test.ts` tests also pass. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Ran `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` end-to-end with a custom provider (thegrid-ai), confirmed metadata prompt appears after model setup. Ran with OpenAI, confirmed metadata prompt does **not** appear. - Edge cases checked: Declining metadata prompt preserves config unchanged; missing primary model gracefully skips; provider with no models returns empty list; built-in providers silently skip the prompt - What you did **not** verify: All built-in provider auth paths (only tested OpenAI). Other built-in providers use the same `authChoice !== "custom-api-key"` code path so are expected to behave identically. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes`: declining the new prompt produces identical config to before - Config/env changes? `No`: writes to existing `models.providers.*.models[]` fields - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the three-line addition in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` (lines 138-140: the `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` block calling `promptModelMetadataForPrimary`) - Files/config to restore: `src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts` (remove import + call), delete `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` and `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If the metadata prompt throws, it would interrupt the configure wizard after custom provider setup but before config write. The `WizardCancelledError` path is handled by the parent wizard. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Metadata prompt adds extra steps to the custom provider configure flow, potentially annoying for users who don't need it. - Mitigation: Gated behind a confirm ("Configure metadata?") defaulting to Yes but skippable with a single keypress. Cost is further gated behind its own confirm. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-11T21:54:02Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds an interactive metadata configuration wizard step to `openclaw configure --section model` for custom provider setups, allowing users to set `contextWindow`, `maxTokens`, API adapter, reasoning, input modalities, token pricing, and compatibility flags without hand-editing config files. The feature is purely additive and well-structured, but two logic issues need attention before merging. **Key changes:** - New `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` module with helpers for collecting, finding, updating, and prompting model metadata - `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` hooked into `promptAuthConfig` in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` - 18 unit tests covering the new helpers **Issues found:** - The call to `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` (line 138) is unconditional — it runs for every auth path, not just `custom-api-key`. The PR description explicitly states it should be gated, and the failure-recovery section even describes an `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` guard that does not appear in the actual diff. Users with manual model entries under built-in provider names (e.g. `models.providers.openai.models[…]`) will hit the metadata prompt unexpectedly. - In the compat cleanup loop (`model-metadata.ts` lines 263–267), all `false` values are pruned. Since `supportsModelTools` in `model-tool-support.ts` treats absence as `true`, an explicit `supportsTools: false` set by the user will be silently discarded and the model will be treated as tool-capable — exactly the opposite of what the user configured. Venice catalog models confirm `false` must be stored explicitly.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge as-is due to a logic bug that silently discards explicit `supportsTools: false` and a missing guard that could show the metadata prompt during built-in provider flows. - Two logic bugs identified: (1) the metadata prompt is called unconditionally rather than only for `custom-api-key`, deviating from stated design; (2) the compat `false`-value pruning loop will silently lose `supportsTools: false`, causing the system to incorrectly assume tool support for models where the user explicitly disabled it. Both are straightforward to fix but affect correctness and user trust. - `src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts` (missing `custom-api-key` guard) and `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` (compat pruning loop).
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts Line: 138 Comment: **Metadata prompt is not gated to custom providers** `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` is called unconditionally for every auth path — including built-in providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.). The PR description states the call is "appended after the custom provider path only" and the failure-recovery note even describes reverting an `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` guard, but no such guard exists in the actual diff. In practice this is safe only because `findPrimaryModelEntry` returns `undefined` when the primary model key belongs to a built-in catalog provider (whose entries are not stored in `cfg.models.providers`). However, any user who has manually added entries under a built-in provider name in their config (e.g. `models.providers.openai.models[…]`) will now see the metadata prompt after a built-in auth flow — contrary to the stated design. The fix should match the stated intent: ```suggestion if (authChoice === "custom-api-key") { next = await promptModelMetadataForPrimary(next, prompter); } return next; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/commands/model-metadata.ts Line: 263-267 Comment: **Pruning `false` values silently discards explicit `supportsTools: false`** The loop deletes every entry whose value is `false`. This means if a user explicitly answers **No** to "Supports tool/function calling?", the resulting `supportsTools: false` is deleted from the `compat` object before it is saved. `supportsModelTools` in `src/agents/model-tool-support.ts` uses the guard `compat?.supportsTools !== false` — absence is treated as `true`. So pruning `supportsTools: false` causes the system to behave as if the model *does* support tools, overriding the user's explicit configuration. This is confirmed by the Venice models catalog which stores `supportsTools: false` explicitly for models that lack tool support. The intent of the cleanup appears to be avoiding storing unnecessary `false` defaults for fields that are `false`-by-default (e.g. `supportsStore`, `supportsDeveloperRole`). The safer fix is to only prune the known `false`-by-default keys, or to skip `supportsTools` specifically since it defaults to `true`: ```suggestion for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(compat)) { if (v === false && k !== "supportsTools") { delete compat[k]; } } ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44136 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44136 - Title: fix(copilot): respect user-configured baseUrl in token flow - State: OPEN - Author: AZDSoftware - Labels: agents, size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary **Real-world validation**: Setting `models.providers.github-copilot.baseUrl` to `https://api.business.githubcopilot.com` in openclaw.json eliminates 421 errors for Business accounts. This PR builds on PR #29614 by implementing the priority logic reviewer feedback identified: **user-configured baseUrl should take priority over token-derived baseUrl**. ## Changes - Add conditional baseUrl assignment in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` (two locations: initial auth + token refresh) - Only apply token-derived baseUrl if: - Token provides one (which it should) - **AND** user hasn't configured a custom one (or it's still the default) This ensures: ✅ User-configured workaround (`baseUrl: "https://api.business.githubcopilot.com"`) continues to work ✅ Enterprise/Business accounts auto-correct when user hasn't configured anything ✅ Corporate proxy configs aren't overridden ✅ Backwards compatible (Individual accounts still work) ## Affected Accounts | Tier | Token `proxy-ep` | Current (broken) | After PR #29614 | After this PR | |---|---|---|---|---| | Individual | `proxy.individual` | Works | Works | Works | | Business | `proxy.business` | ❌ 421 | ✅ Works | ✅ Works + respects config | | Enterprise | `proxy.enterprise` | ❌ 421 | ✅ Works | ✅ Works + respects config | ## Related - Addresses review feedback from PR #29614 - Fixes: #41379, #8366, #17508 - Works with GPT-5.1-Codex, GPT-5.2-Codex, GPT-5.3-Codex, and other Copilot models **Tested on**: OpenClaw 2026.3.8, GitHub Copilot Business account ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T14:45:49Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds priority logic so that a user-configured `baseUrl` in `openclaw.json` takes precedence over the token-derived endpoint for GitHub Copilot Business/Enterprise accounts, building on the auto-detection work in PR #29614. The intent is correct, but there is a critical propagation bug: `effectiveModel` — the model object actually consumed by `runEmbeddedAttempt` — is computed as a shallow spread copy of `model` *before* `applyApiKeyInfo` is called. When context-window capping is active (a common scenario), writes to `model.baseUrl` are invisible to `effectiveModel`, meaning the token-derived `baseUrl` is never applied to the actual API call and the 421 fix would not function in those situations. Key points: - **Logic bug (both locations)**: `model.baseUrl` is mutated after `effectiveModel` is snapshotted; `effectiveModel.baseUrl` must be updated in parallel when `effectiveModel !== model`. - **Dead-code guard**: `copilotToken.baseUrl` is typed as `string` and is always non-empty, so the `copilotToken.baseUrl &&` guard in both sites is always true and should be removed for clarity.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge as-is: the core fix will silently do nothing when context-window capping is in effect, because `effectiveModel` (used for the actual API call) is a spread copy made before the baseUrl mutation. - The PR introduces a mutation to `model.baseUrl`, but `effectiveModel` — which is what `runEmbeddedAttempt` receives — is constructed as `{ ...model }` before `applyApiKeyInfo` is invoked whenever `ctxInfo.tokens < model.contextWindow`. In that spread path (common when a context cap is configured), the mutation is not reflected in the object the agent actually uses, so the Business/Enterprise endpoint fix does not take effect. This renders the primary goal of the PR unreliable without a fix to also update `effectiveModel.baseUrl`. - src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts — both added hunks need to also update `effectiveModel.baseUrl` when `effectiveModel !== model`.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts Line: 486-489 Comment: **`model.baseUrl` mutation not reflected in `effectiveModel`** `effectiveModel` is computed at line 388–391 — *before* `applyApiKeyInfo` is called — as a spread copy of `model` whenever `ctxInfo.tokens < model.contextWindow`: ```ts const effectiveModel = ctxInfo.tokens < (model.contextWindow ?? Infinity) ? { ...model, contextWindow: ctxInfo.tokens } // new object : model; // same reference ``` When a spread copy is created (the common case when a user has configured a context-token cap), `effectiveModel.baseUrl` is a snapshot of `model.baseUrl` taken at construction time. The subsequent mutations introduced by this PR (`model.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl`) write to the *source* object, but the *copy* passed to `runEmbeddedAttempt` at line 900 still holds the stale, pre-mutation value. As a result, the Copilot API call will target the wrong endpoint despite the token-derived URL being resolved correctly. The same problem applies to both the initial-auth path (this hunk) and the refresh path at line 632–635. To propagate the update correctly, `effectiveModel` must also be updated: ```ts if (copilotToken.baseUrl && (!model.baseUrl || model.baseUrl === DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL)) { model.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl; // Propagate to effectiveModel when it is a separate spread copy if (effectiveModel !== model) { effectiveModel.baseUrl = copilotToken.baseUrl; } } ``` The same change is needed at the second site (line 632–635). How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts Line: 486-487 Comment: **`copilotToken.baseUrl` guard is always truthy** `resolveCopilotApiToken` declares its return type as `{ baseUrl: string; ... }` and always assigns either the token-derived URL or `DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL` as a fallback via the nullish-coalescing operator: ```ts baseUrl: deriveCopilotApiBaseUrlFromToken(payload.token) ?? DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL, ``` Therefore `copilotToken.baseUrl` is always a non-empty string, and the leading `copilotToken.baseUrl &&` check is dead code. It misleads readers into thinking the value could be falsy. Consider dropping it: ```suggestion if (!model.baseUrl || model.baseUrl === DEFAULT_COPILOT_API_BASE_URL) { ``` The identical redundant guard also appears at line 632. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 69696 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/69696 - Title: fix(openrouter): make base URL normalization case-insensitive and add regression tests - State: OPEN - Author: zhouhe-xydt - Labels: agents, size: XS, extensions: arcee, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary This PR strengthens the OpenRouter base URL normalization introduced in #68574 to handle case-insensitive URL matching and adds regression tests for edge cases. ## Changes - Make `normalizeBaseUrl` case-insensitive by adding `.toLowerCase()` in: - `extensions/openrouter/provider-catalog.ts` - `extensions/arcee/provider-catalog.ts` - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.provider-runtime.test-support.ts` - Add regression tests covering: - Uppercase domain variants (e.g., `https://OPENROUTER.AI/v1`) - Already-correct URLs returning `undefined` (no-op) - Non-OpenRouter custom proxies returning `undefined` (no-op) ## Context Issue #69646 reported that OpenRouter requests were using `/v1` instead of `/api/v1`, causing HTML error responses instead of JSON. The root fix was already landed in #68574 (2026.4.18). This PR tightens the normalization logic and adds coverage to prevent regressions. ## Test plan - [x] `pnpm test extensions/openrouter` passes (8 tests) - [x] `pnpm test extensions/arcee` passes (7 tests) - [x] `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts` passes (50 tests) Fixes #69646 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-21T11:45:47Z:

This PR adds `.toLowerCase()` to the `normalizeBaseUrl` helper in three files (`extensions/openrouter/provider-catalog.ts`, `extensions/arcee/provider-catalog.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.provider-runtime.test-support.ts`) to make OpenRouter URL detection case-insensitive, and adds regression tests for uppercase variants, already-correct URLs, and custom proxy passthrough. The changes are minimal and correct: the lowercased intermediate value is only used for comparison against known-lowercase constants, so the actual URL returned is always the canonical `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL` constant — not the case-folded input.

input.target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #65054: [Bug]: provider-usage-DYewFt0n.js hardcodes CN endpoint for MiniMax usage polling — ignores configured baseUrl
input.github_context:

- Type: github_issue
